properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Although small compared to most Egyptian temples, the Temple of Dendur is a graceful example of a typical pharaonic temple and is a highlight of any visit to The Met. Caesar Augustus built this structure by 10 B.C. in Lower Nubia, the region adjacent to ancient Egypt’s southern border, to connect the population living there to the Roman Empire. Therefore, the monument is dedicated not only to the goddess Isis and other Egyptian deities, but also to several indigenous Nubian gods and two local saints. In 1965, Egypt gave the Temple of Dendur to the U.S. in recognition of the support our government and its citizens gave Egypt towards saving their Nubian monuments.

The Benkard Room

Many of the pieces on view in this parlor once belonged to the noted early twentieth-century collector Bertha King Benkard, for whom the room is named. It was originally part of a house built in Petersburg, Virginia, but prominently features furniture made in New York City by leading cabinetmakers such as Duncan Phyfe. Like the nearby Baltimore and Haverhill Rooms, the Benkard Room exemplifies the popularity in the young United States of the Neoclassical style evident in the work of the eighteenth-century British architects Robert and James Adam.

properties.popup_header: Henry R. Luce Center for the Study of American Art
properties.popup_body:
The Henry R. Luce Center for the Study of American Art

The Henry R. Luce Center for the Study of American Art is a visible-storage facility that displays more than ten thousand works of American fine and decorative art. Objects are arranged by material (paintings, sculpture, furniture and woodwork, glass, ceramics, silver, and metalwork), and within these categories they are arranged by chronology and form. Works of art that are sensitive to light, such as drawings, watercolors, textiles, and portrait miniatures, are not on view but can be seen by appointment. A digital catalogue of the American Wing collections is accessible on two public computer terminals within the center.

properties.popup_header: Henry R. Luce Center for the Study of American Art Corridor
properties.popup_body:
The Henry R. Luce Center for the Study of American Art

A rotating exhibition space for for focused or thematic displays of the American Wing’s varied holdings.

properties.popup_header: On the World Stage: The Columbia Exposition
properties.popup_body:
The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

In the late nineteenth century a new generation of artists and thinkers sought to transcend issues of nationality. The cultural educations they received in international art capitals redirected some of them toward cosmopolitan styles and themes. Many also applied their newly learned techniques to American subjects. Still others established permanent residency and built reputations abroad. The 1893 world’s fair in Chicago—an ambitious commemoration of the four hundredth anniversary of Christopher Columbus’s consequential voyage—was an ideal venue to display new art and ideas to international audiences. This gallery surveys the artistic innovations of this time as well as the rich exchanges that occurred when artists shared their work with broad publics at similar watershed events. Realists and Impressionists embraced classical traditions of the past while investigating the contemporary United States as a land of diverse cultural contrasts rooted in cities, rural environs, and the romanticized West.

Terian Family Gallery in the The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

After the Civil War the United States underwent profound changes, including rapid economic expansion and population growth, and emerged in the 1890s as a world power. Great wealth and conspicuous consumption characterized the period, now known as the Gilded Age. More Americans sought artistic training abroad, especially in Paris and Munich. They competed with European painters for commissions from wealthy patrons, who embraced portraiture to establish their identity, power, and social standing. A leading American expatriate, John Singer Sargent was born in Italy, studied and worked in Paris, and pursued a career with equal success in Europe and the United States. This gallery features Sargent’s iconic depictions of American and European sitters along with work by his contemporaries, including Cecilia Beaux, Thomas Eakins, and James McNeill Whistler. The paintings reveal diverse approaches to the art of portraiture at the turn of the twentieth century.

The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries

This large and imposing room has been treasured for its historical associations with the Founding Fathers since it was installed in the American Wing in 1924. Intended to host public assemblies and elegant balls, it was originally located on the second floor of the City Hotel, built in 1792 in Alexandria, Virginia. George Washington celebrated his last two birthdays in this room; Thomas Jefferson held his inaugural banquet as president here in 1801; and the Marquis de Lafayette was feted in this ballroom during his 1824–25 tour of the United States.

Situated on the Potomac River, Alexandria had developed into a sizable town and robust trading port for tobacco, wheat, and imported goods by the late eighteenth century. It also had a large population of enslaved and free Black laborers involved in building projects across the city—likely including the construction of the hotel from which this room was removed. Washington, whose Mount Vernon plantation stands approximately eight miles south, had close ties to Alexandria. He regularly conducted business there and even built a house to allow for overnight stays after attending social events, such as those that occurred in this very room.

The Artistic Legacy of George Washington

Looming large in United States history and art, George Washington (1732–1799) has been a subject of creative exploration from the 1770s through the present day, across a variety of media and in scales ranging from monumental to handheld. During his lifetime he sat for several painters, including Gilbert Stuart, Charles Willson Peale,and John Trumbull, who carefully crafted his image as a military general and statesman and tied their own careers and reputations to his representation. From the United States to Europe to China, works of art bearing Washington’s likeness circulated broadly. Paintings were often copied and reproduced in printed media and the decorative arts, reinforcing Washington’s status as a hero of national and international renown as well as popularizing and mythologizing him as an ideal American leader. Yet his persona takes on vastly different meanings in various contexts and in the hands of diverse artists. The multiplicity of identities portrayed in this gallery—war hero, patriot, first president, Founding Father, diplomat, enslaver, Hanadagá·yas (Town Destroyer, as the Haudenosaunee know him)—attests to the significance and complexity of Washington’s legacy. Taken together, these works invite viewers to reckon with him as a cultural icon.

properties.popup_header: The Calculated Curve: Eighteenth-Century American Furniture
properties.popup_body:
Joyce B. Cowin Gallery in the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

Visitors to the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries will encounter a dramatic presentation of masterworks in The Met collection of North American furniture produced between 1720 and 1775. During this period of great aesthetic achievement, talented makers rejected the heft and rectilinearity of earlier approaches and embraced more liberating designs conceived almost entirely of curves and abstract motifs inspired by nature. This display encourages greater consideration of the works’ sculptural expression and elastic forms, the interplay of light, and the artists’ abilities to push materials to their limits.

Furniture has always been intertwined with everyday life, but it is also an essential art form. Underlining its utility are thoughtful mathematical and architectural strategies, structural engineering, and aesthetic innovations akin to other mediums. The shapely forms and textured ornament, as well as the incorporation of ergonomics to accommodate the human body, reveal the fundamental artistry of furniture design.

Some of the brilliant furniture makers featured in this installation are identified, while others’ names were not recorded. Despite their crucial contributions, talented people of color, immigrants, and teenagers were least likely to receive credit for their work. Women were also critical to the production and reception of early North American furniture, providing immeasurable operational support to workshops and access to capital and patrons through kinship networks. Additional furniture holdings—and their fascinating stories—can be explored in the historical interiors, or period rooms, throughout the American Wing.

The architectural woodwork in this room demonstrates the retention of traditional Netherlandish building practices in communities of Dutch descent in late colonial New York and New Jersey. This main chamber comes from a house built in 1751 for Daniel Pieter Winne (1720–1800), whose ancestors came to Rensselaerwyck (present-day Albany County) in 1643 from Flanders via Curaçao.

Furnished as a gallery, this room includes a variety of forms of furniture, metalwork, and ceramics distinctive to New York households of Dutch heritage.

Lizzie and Jonathan Tisch Gallery in the Anna Wintour Costume Center


The main showcase of the Anna Wintour Costume Center, the Lizzie and Jonathan Tisch Gallery can transform to accommodate a wide range of installations and scenography. Its flexible design lends itself to frequent transformation with the latest video, sound, and wireless technology.
This gallery has no description.

properties.popup_header: Carl and Iris Barrel Apfel Gallery
properties.popup_body:
Carl and Iris Barrel Apfel Gallery in the Anna Wintour Costume Center

The Carl and Iris Barrel Apfel Gallery, part of the Anna Wintour Costume Center, orients visitors to The Costume Institute's exhibitions and holdings.

Robert Lehman Collection

This gallery has no description.

properties.popup_header: Egypt under Roman Rule in the First Century AD
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The wall cases display art from the early Roman Period (dating from 30 B.C. to A.D. 200), including a faience head of Augustus, the king who defeated Marc Antony at Actium, and superb panel portraits. During this period, people continued the custom of mummification, but Hellenistic and Roman traditions were also integrated into the funerary art. Consequently, panel portraits painted on wood using pigmented wax could replace the traditional idealized masks as may be seen in the case of the mummy of a man from Hawara, whose identity is unknown. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Robert Lehman Collection

Dedicated to eighteenth- and nineteenth-century paintings, Gallery 957 displays distinguished portraits by French and British artists, as well as several French landscapes, primarily by members of the Barbizon School. Among the highlights are Monet's Landscape near Zaandam and Ingres's arresting portrait of the Princesse de Broglie, in which he brilliantly transcribes the texture of satin, lace, and jewels.

Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries (Gallery 904 through Gallery 907) feature paintings and sculptures from the Gelman collection, which the Museum received in 1998. The collection's emphasis is on the School of Paris, comprised of French and often non-French artists who lived and worked in France before and after World War I.

On view are works by Pierre Bonnard, Georges Braque, Alberto Giacometti, Fernand Léger, Henri Matisse, Joan Miró, Pablo Picasso, Yves Tanguy, and others. This permanent installation offers a tour through the European Modernist movement from the turn of the twentieth century to the 1970s.

properties.popup_header: Arms and Armor from the Stone Age to the Iron Age
properties.popup_body:
Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


Long before the emergence of the written word, and with it what we call history, our ancestors developed weapons to hunt animals, to defend their lives and goods, and to fight enemies. These implements conferred such significant advantages that their possession alone, not to mention their display, signified power. Weapons were therefore symbols as much as tools from the beginning, markers of status and wealth valued beyond their utilitarian benefits.

In ancient cultures, weapons were often interred with the dead to accompany them to the afterlife. They were also sometimes ritually buried, thrown into rivers, or abandoned. In contrast, weapons seized from enemies could be prominently displayed, especially in places of worship as dedications to the gods.

This gallery explores the function, technology, circulation, and meanings of arms from the Stone Age through the Iron Age, and the particular significance of these tools during their working lives and beyond.

properties.popup_header: The Entrance to the Art of Ancient Egypt
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This chapel, once part of a mastaba tomb, dominates the entrance to the Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art. In about 2350 2400 B.C., the monument was built for an Old Kingdom court official named Perneb at Saqqara, the great cemetery of Egypt's ancient capital Memphis. The Met was able to purchase this tomb chapel from the Egyptian government in 1913. A granite lion and a statue of another official also introduce the visitor to the monumentality and permanency of ancient Egypt's art and architecture.

properties.popup_header: Life and Death in Middle Kingdom Egypt
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Objects from The Met’s excavations introduce visitors to life in Egypt between 2000 and 1550 B.C. House models, furniture, and vessels are brought together alongside small figures of household deities and magical instruments. Other objects represent local industries, including agriculture, fishing, animal husbandry, and textile manufacturing, material from workshops, and objects indicative of trade. Four female tomb groups demonstrate how social status was reflected in burial practices and that of Senebtisi illustrates funerary equipment belonging to a member of the royal court in about 1800 B.C. Her apron is a rare piece of ritual jewelry that connects her to Osiris, the god of the underworld.

Robert Lehman Collection

This gallery houses one of the most celebrated collections of Italian Renaissance maiolica in the world. The heart of the collection dates from the first half of the sixteenth century—the golden age of maiolica—when we begin to find a broader range of colors as well as complex figural compositions that were often based on the works of contemporary painters.

While it is uncertain whether these plates, bowls, and jars were intended for use as tableware or simply to be admired, we know that maiolica services were often commissioned to celebrate special occasions, such as marriages or births. Significant examples include pieces commissioned by Isabella d'Este of Mantua and Pope Julius II, which display the coats of arms of their distinguished patrons.

properties.popup_header: Leon Levy and Shelby White Court
properties.popup_body:
Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The spacious Leon Levy and Shelby White Court- a columned, rectangular peristyle with wide porticoes around the sides—evoke the splendor of imperial Rome. The natural light coming in through the roof beautifully highlights the plastic forms of the sculptures. Most works displayed here in central peristyle and the galleries opening to the left and the back belong to the period of the Roman Empire. In the middle of the Roman Court water splashes in a big stone basin, a modern pedestal fountain modeled on an imperial Roman counterpart. The original Roman gallery that opened in 1926 also had a fountain, set amid greenery to evoke the peristyle garden of a Roman villa. The current display inspires the same tranquility, although the atrium has a new polychrome floor of colored marble and the skylight has been raised to a second story which adds grandeur to the space as it was originally intended by the architects, McKim, Mead and White.

The Lawrence A. and Barbara Fleischman Gallery of Late Medieval Secular Art 

The Lawrence A. and Barbara Fleischman Gallery of Late Medieval Secular Art displays a range of works. Ivory caskets, mirror cases, a gaming board and chess pieces, ceramics, and glass give a sense of the kinds and quality of objects one might find in a wealthy medieval household. Among its highlights are a rare ceremonial saddle decorated with scenes of courtship, and the painted cover of an account book commissioned by the commune of Siena, which depicts civic administrators at work.

This new gallery was designed to house Tullio Lombardo's life-sized marble statue Adam, which ranks among the most important artworks of the Italian Renaissance to be found outside Italy today. The most talented member of the leading family of sculptors in Venice in the decades around 1500, Tullio carved Adam as the signature element for the monumental tomb of Doge Andrea Vendramin.

In the gallery's inaugural exhibition, Tullio Lombardo's Adam: A Masterpiece Restored, the sculpture is displayed in the round and accompanied by a presentation of the extensive conservation project that restored Adam to public view following a tragic accident in 2002.

Following this exhibition, Adam will move to a niche on the east wall that suggests his original placement on the Vendramin tomb. It will be joined by highlights of the Met's Venetian and northern Italian Renaissance sculpture, among them the recently acquired St. Catherine by Cristoforo Solari.

The gallery, a perfect cube, was designed with Renaissance ideals of proportion in mind, utilizing the golden ratio. It is meant to provide a peaceful, reflective space in which to encounter one of the great periods of European sculpture.

The installation of this gallery was made possible by Assunta Sommella Peluso, Ignazio Peluso, Ada Peluso and Romano I. Peluso.

Robert Lehman Collection

The Lehman Collection possesses one of the foremost drawing collections in the world, selections of which are exhibited here on a rotating basis.

When Robert Lehman first acquired Old Master drawings in the 1920s, there were very few private or public collections of this medium in the United States. Over a sixty-year period, Lehman amassed a distinguished collection of 750 sheets dating from the fifteenth to the twentieth century, including drawings by such renowned draftsmen such as Leonardo da Vinci, Dürer, Rembrandt, Tiepolo, and Seurat.

properties.popup_header: The Beginning of the Middle Kingdom
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The imposing statue of Nebhepetre Mentuhotep in his festival cloak dominates the room. This ruler, whose family came from the southern area of Thebes (modern day Luxor), unified Egypt around 2050 B.C. after a period when the state fragmented into regional leadership. With the country’s reunification, Nebhepetre Mentuhotep’s rule guided a period of cultural revival, the vitality of which may be observed in the delicately carved reliefs largely from his funerary temple and the tomb of his wife, Queen Neferu, both structures located at Deir el-Bahri.

The Federal period was the golden age of American sampler making, as demonstrated by the variety and beauty of the examples in this gallery. The era saw the flowering of private girls' schools known as "ladies academies," where girls learned such genteel skills as music, art, and needlework; academic subjects were usually of secondary importance. Many of the samplers here were made at these schools.

Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Ronald S. Lauder Gallery of European edged weapons offers an unparalleled display of finely decorated European swords dating from the sixteenth through the nineteenth century, including rapiers, hunting weapons, ceremonial swords, and presentation swords made of delicate and precious materials.

This gallery also features the last royal armor made in Europe, which was created in 1712 for the five-year-old heir to the Spanish throne, Luis, prince of Asturias.

Robert Lehman Collection

With a floor of inlaid marble, this gallery re-creates the dining room in the Lehman residence and displays the family's distinguished collection of European glass, now on view again after a recent gallery restoration. Significant examples include glasses belonging to the Medici popes of the sixteenth century and King Louis of France of the late fifteenth century. Sixteenth-century Venetian glass, the most coveted by wealthy European patrons, comprises the heart of the Lehman Collection's holdings in this medium. The fifteenth-century Italian mirror frames flanking the doorways belong to Robert Lehman's highly distinguished collection of antique frames.

The Iris and B. Gerald Cantor Galleries
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

This space within The Iris and B. Gerald Cantor Galleries houses four of the Museum's most popular pieces of nineteenth-century sculpture. The great Neoclassical sculptor Antonio Canova is represented by the figure of the Reclining Naiad (water nymph). The figure's pose and hairstyle recall sculpture from classical antiquity, as does the use of white marble. Marble is used to very different effect for the sculpture of Cupid with two sleeping figures. Signed by Lorenzo Bartolini, the highly detailed sculpture is a tour de force of marble carving. In contrast, Charles Cordier's two busts of African women are made of bronze embellished with enamel and onyx, reflecting a new taste for polychromed sculpture.

This gallery also displays a group of important Fabergé works on long term loan from the Matilda Geddings Grey Foundation, which includes three Imperial Easter Eggs and a Lily of the Valley Basket that is considered the most important Fabergé object in North America.

properties.popup_header: The Late New Kingdom Study Room
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this gallery are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces dating between about 1350 and 1100 B.C. The fragments along the north wall come from Amarna, King Akhenaten’s city, and belong to smashed statues of the royal family. These pieces continue to reveal information about the statuary program in the Amarna temples. On the south side are the remains from Tutankhamun's funeral activity discovered in 1907. Its find spot later helped to locate the king’s tomb in 1922. Late New Kingdom pieces on the east and west walls present material belonging to Ramesside culture and history.

The Henry R. Kravis Wing

This display evokes the practice of lining palace corridors with portrait busts of rulers and important citizens—a custom that attests to Europe's ongoing admiration of the values and ideals of ancient Rome, where the tradition originated. Serving to commemorate and to celebrate, portrait busts functioned as recognizable likenesses of their subjects, though sitters were often idealized to ensure a suitably imposing image. By including more of the figure to show costume details, sculptors could create exciting visual effects and convey additional clues about the sitter. Early eighteenth-century portrait busts often feature intense facial expressions and energetic details such as windswept curls—a continuing feature of the Baroque style. Greater restraint characterizes carved likenesses made later in the century, when artists sought inspiration from the sober elegance of ancient statuary.

This gallery is also hung with tapestries—another important feature of palace decoration. Mythological, historical, and allegorical scenes woven from richly colored threads were favored, as were coats of arms, serving as grand celebrations of their owners' wealth and social position.

properties.popup_header: The Early Middle Kingdom Study Room
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery displays Middle Kingdom objects from about 2000 to 1650 B.C. excavated by The Met in Thebes, organized as if in a storeroom. This arrangement allows visitors to study the range of objects that archaeologists find during excavation. The gallery also holds two cases of objects that once belonged to private collectors. The difference in the nature between these two groups is worth considering. Excavated objects come from a known place but their significance is often only understandable after much scholarly work, while pieces from private collections are chosen for their appeal and therefore often are in better condition.

This light-filled space presents an exceptional collection of American sculpture, architectural elements, stained glass, and decorative arts. It is anchored by the Neoclassical facade of the Branch Bank of the United States, originally located on Wall Street, and the striking loggia from Laurelton Hall, Louis C. Tiffany’s country estate. Augustus Saint-Gaudens’s gilded Diana heralds the exemplary holdings of nineteenth- and early twentieth-century sculptures on view throughout the space. A comprehensive history of American stained glass is also presented—from the Gothic Revival windows on the balcony to Prairie School windows, including one by Frank Lloyd Wright, on the mezzanine. Tiffany’s opalescent glass illuminates several windows and mosaics, including the luxuriant Garden Landscape filling the loggia.

The second-floor and mezzanine balconies feature wide-ranging examples of American silver, pewter, jewelry, ceramics, and glass, from the colonial period (beginning about 1600) through the mid-1900s. Arranged chronologically, the second-floor cases feature different mediums and thematic displays. This level and the mezzanine can be accessed by the glass elevator or the staircase from the Chicago Stock Exchange, designed by Louis Sullivan.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries (Gallery 904 through Gallery 907) feature paintings and sculptures from the Gelman collection, which the Museum received in 1998. The collection's emphasis is on the School of Paris, comprised of French and often non-French artists who lived and worked in France before and after World War I.

On view are works by Pierre Bonnard, Georges Braque, Alberto Giacometti, Fernand Léger, Henri Matisse, Joan Miró, Pablo Picasso, Yves Tanguy, and others. This permanent installation offers a tour through the European Modernist movement from the turn of the twentieth century to the 1970s.

properties.popup_header: The Art of the Late Period
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

During Dynasty 26, beginning around 664 B.C., artists increasingly reflected on works made in earlier times, combining various elements into new creations. These objects exhibit sensitive modeling and brilliant surface treatments, especially in hard stones. The gallery has many pieces of royal sculpture as well as objects that once graced a temple or shrine, including two exquisite statuettes of deities. Nespekashuty’s unfinished tomb wall shows how work progressed while carving relief in stone. To the west, a series of shelves exhibit a variety of amulets and small sculptures that display the remarkable talents of artisans of the time.

Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

While many painters, sculptors, and designers celebrated the dynamism of the metropolis, others recoiled from the dehumanizing scale, unhealthy congestion, and rampant consumerism of modern urban life. These artists, many of whom felt connected to mystical or metaphysical aspects of nineteenth-century culture, employed art as a means of retreat from the banality and rigor of daily life into the mind and spirit.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Small exhibitions are regularly held in this space that focus on specific topics drawn from the culture and history of ancient Egypt. They highlight groups of objects from The Met’s collection of Egyptian art.

properties.popup_header: Josephine Mercy Heathcote Gallery for 19th-Century British Art
properties.popup_body:
Josephine Mercy Heathcote Gallery

As the Industrial Revolution took hold, Britain transformed with massive growth in manufacturing, the development of the railway system, and a shift to a more urban society. Consumers were everywhere, eager to bring culture into their homes. Anything was now possible as eclectic styles dominated the marketplace: makers looked to the East and the Islamic world, to classicism and the Renaissance, to the Gothic, Baroque, and Rococo. Once again, aesthetic and commercial priorities adapted to an immense new world of production methods and customers.

Mary and Michael Jaharis Gallery

The grand, barrel-vaulted Mary and Michael Jaharis Gallery displays large-scale sculpture and other monumental works of the sixth, fifth, and fourth centuries B.C.

Works of the sixth century include examples from the Museum's distinguished collection of Panathenaic amphorae amid other works related to ancient Greek athletics. In the center of the gallery are displayed large-scale marble copies—made during the Roman period—of bronze statues that were created in Greece during the fifth and fourth centuries but were lost or melted down over time. Original marble statues of the fourth century B.C. are shown by the crowning sculptures of tall Athenian grave monuments.

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

properties.popup_header: From the Pyramid Temple of King Senwosret I at Lisht South
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The pyramid complex of Lisht South once had a magnificent enclosure wall whose remains were later excavated by The Met’s archaeologists. Four of the massive relief panels that decorated this wall have been reconstructed for display in this gallery. Senwosret I, the king who built this complex, reigned for about forty-two years beginning around 1960 B.C. A headless statue of this king in a smooth dark stone dominates the gallery’s center. Other royal reliefs hang on the walls, while additional statues and tombstones (stelae) belonging to officials, from Senwosret I’s court as well as from other sites in Egypt, are presented.

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery presents an outstanding collection of painted coffins mostly excavated by The Met, dating from about 1300 to 700 B.C. The display clearly shows how these caskets could come in sets: outer coffin, inner coffin, and a lid laid over the mummified body. Many coffins are yellow in color and vividly decorated with detailed religious scenes of unparalleled variety. Not to be missed are the beautifully inscribed funerary papyri, especially the one belonging to Nany. These documents also come from the tombs along with boxes filled with funerary figurines known as shabtis. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

The Bothmer Gallery I

The Bothmer Gallery I, named for Dietrich von Bothmer, former chairman of the Department of Greek and Roman Art, focuses on the art of Athens, particularly on painted vases in the black-figure technique, which flourished from about 600 to 530 B.C.

The vases were all made for actual use and they are decorated with mythological scenes as well as reflections of Athenian life. Contemporary works in marble, bronze, and silver present many of the same themes as on the pottery.

See also Gallery 157.

The Wrightsman Galleries

French interiors during the final decades of the Ancien Régime (the monarchy) are legendary for their elegance and refinement. Furniture and furnishings from 1715—when the young Louis XV became king until the French Revolution in 1789—demonstrate outstanding craftsmanship and high levels of specialization, as well as the increasing demand for luxury and comfort. White and gold paneling, enlivened by mirrors, dominated the formal reception rooms of royal and aristocratic residences, where large sets of richly upholstered chairs lined the walls. Skilled craftsmen and artists embellished cabinets and desks with veneers of imported woods, gleaming Japanese lacquer, brightly colored porcelain, and splendid gilt-bronze mounts. Sets of Asian or Sèvres porcelain and elaborate clocks adorned mantelpieces and the marble tops of console tables and commodes. Flickering candlelight was reflected in the textured surfaces of gilt-bronze wall sconces and crystal chandeliers, giving these rooms great liveliness.

properties.popup_header: Funerary Equipment of the Ptolemaic Period
properties.popup_body: This gallery focuses on the equipment needed for a good burial during the time of the Ptolemaic rulers, that is, from about 332 to 30 B.C. The mummified bodies of Irtirutja, Nesmin, and Tasheriteniset are still in their original wrappings and feature elaborate cartonnage decoration as well as gilded masks. A visitor should take note of the dynamic statuette of the jackal-headed god Anubis with his upraised arms. A papyrus scroll over 71 ft. long and inscribed with text and images from a set of spells known as the “Book of the Dead,” hangs on the wall opposite the coffins. Its many illustrations are fascinating to study. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

The Wrightsman Galleries

The display of furniture and Sèvres porcelain in gallery 529 highlights the role of the influential marchands merciers, luxury dealers and tastemakers who established new fashions in eighteenth-century Paris.

properties.popup_header: Burials from the Site of Meir
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery features burial equipment of the elite officials and family members who resided at a provincial site called Meir, about 200 mi. south of Cairo. Their mummified bodies were often provided with masks, then placed inside human-shaped coffins that were deposited inside large, heavy and beautifully decorated rectangular coffins. The dead also received a variety of magically potent objects to protect them, some of which connected the dead person to Osiris, the god of the underworld. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

properties.popup_header: The Northern Renaissance: European Sculpture and Decorative Arts, 1520-1630
properties.popup_body:
Works of art shaped the Renaissance, a rediscovery of antiquity that began in the fifteenth century. Traders and traveling artisans spread prints illustrating Greek and Roman art, architecture, and manuscripts across the Alps from Italy. In Northern Europe the Renaissance joined with the Reformation, which destabilized Catholic dominance and introduced new Protestant denominations. With the Bible and ancient philosophy as inspiration, humanist scholars reshaped discussions on art, religion, and society.

The Holy Roman Empire (962-1806) dominated northern lands and united hundreds of independent territories and imperial city-states. Conflicts between religious groups, peasant wars against oppressive aristocracy, and brutal clashes with neighboring nations kept these social and political boundaries in flux. Dealings with European colonial powers, who were exploiting people and land overseas, added to the region's wealth as merchant networks aggressively extended their markets.

In this complex society, art expressed belonging and belief. Royals, competing fiercely to commission artists, understood that it instilled awe and projected power. Valued for their refinement, the marvels in this gallery were objects of encounter, knowledge, wonder, and personal meaning. Many were specially placed in Kunst-und-Wunderkammern, the art and science collections that were the first step toward today's museums. Now they offer a window into the past.

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

It was the age of the Tudor and Stuart courts. The rule of King Henry VIII gave rise to the English Reformation, and that of Charles I to the English Civil War. Patronage was predominantly secular, and British monarchs competed hungrily to match the artistic glory of papal Rome, the French courts, and the centers of Habsburg power. The destruction of works of art through religious iconoclasm and the Great Fire of London was followed by the resurgence of talent and craftsmanship.

Josephine Bay Paul Gallery
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

Many of the sculptures shown here are small clay models for larger works in other materials such as marble. Sculptors used their hands to mold the soft clay into the desired shape and special tools to create the details. The model was fired in a kiln to harden and preserve it (terracotta literally means "baked earth"). Several of the examples on view were rapidly made to capture an idea or to work out a composition. Full of spontaneous energy, these loose sketches record fleeting moments in the creative process, often showing fingerprints and other traces of the artist's handling of the clay. Sculptors might produce more finished models to present to patrons before committing their vision to stone or metal.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The scenes of nature and of activities that surrounded the preparation for an individual’s afterlife are depicted on the walls of this tomb chapel. The chapel was created in about 2400 B.C. for a man called Neferiretenes, who was a judge and inspector of scribes. His relatives would have presented offerings in front of the stylized reproduction of an Egyptian doorway (false door) that symbolized the connection between this world and the next. Later the tomb was taken over for Raemkai, a king’s son, whose name has replaced that of Neferiretenes on the false door.

properties.popup_header: Art of Imperial Rome: Second Century A.D.
properties.popup_body:
The Lamont Wing

Gallery 168 displays fine examples of material culture from the second century A.D., when the Roman empire was at the height of its power and prosperity and when people in Rome, throughout Italy, and in many of the provinces enjoyed a standard of living and a way of life that were unequaled both in antiquity and in more recent times.

Objects associated with activities that either ensured or benefited from imperial protection (the Roman army, the state religion, the games, and the baths) are also on view.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The Old Kingdom, which includes Dynasty 3‑6 and dates between 2700 and 2200 B.C., was a formative period in the development of monumental architecture and gave voice to many principles of Egyptian art. Although the pyramids are the most famous monuments from this time, decorated tomb chapels and statues of wealthy officials display the extraordinary talents of the sculptors who worked in stone and wood. In addition, exceptionally skillful artisans created other objects for the temples and tombs, such as stone vessels in Egyptian alabaster. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Robert Lehman Collection

This re-creation of the Lehman's sitting room—with red velvet damask wall coverings—serves as a dramatic setting for seventeenth-century Dutch portraits and genre scenes as well as works by Spanish masters. Highlights include Rembrandt's poignant portrait of the artist and critic Gerard de Lairesse and El Greco's Saint Jerome, which synthesizes the saint's roles as scholar and ascetic.

Also on display are enameled objects produced in Limoges, France, during the sixteenth century.

properties.popup_header: Fifteenth-Century Central and Northern Italian Paintings/Flemish Tapestries
properties.popup_body:
Robert Lehman Collection

Gallery 959 houses fifteenth-century central and northern Italian paintings as well as sixteenth-century Flemish tapestries, reflecting the dynamic cultural crosscurrents between northern and southern Europe during this period.

On view is Bernaert Van Orley's Last Supper, one of the most important tapestries in America, which reveals the influence of both northern and southern sources. In a display case, among the three fifteenth-century Italian paintings of the Annunciation, is a small panel painted by Botticelli, probably commissioned as a private devotional image.

properties.popup_header: Sculpture and Decorative Arts in Central Europe, 1700–1800
properties.popup_body:
The Lesley and Emma Sheafer Collection in The Wrightsman Galleries

The furniture, ceramics, silver, and glass in this gallery were made in the German-speaking states of Central Europe. The extravagant curvilinear forms and exuberant natural motifs reflect a distinctly Germanic interpretation of the lively Rococo style that originated in France in the 1730s. Evoking trellises heavy with blooms, the set of carved furniture from Seehof Castle, near Bamberg, is one of the purest expressions in the decorative arts of the Rococo fascination with nature and fantasy.

Central European sculptors primarily carved wood, favoring elongated figures in rhythmic poses that were often painted. The porcelain and earthenware objects on view attest to Germany's preeminence in ceramic production during the first half of the eighteenth century. The first true European porcelain was developed at the Meissen factory, near Dresden. The pieces shown here are among Meissen's greatest achievements.

Judy and Michael H. Steinhardt Gallery

The Judy and Michael H. Steinhardt Gallery is unique in the Western Hemisphere in its display of the three major types of Greek freestanding marble sculpture of the sixth century B.C.: the kouros and kore, which served as funerary monuments or dedications, and the pillar-like grave stele. This is also the period when Athens supplanted Corinth as the center of pottery production, exemplified by several works attributed to the Amasis Painter, one of the most skillful and innovative of the black-figure artists.

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

The Helen and Milton A. Kimmelman Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Many artists cared little about creating imagery that others might perceive as being avant-garde. These artists resolutely held onto the human figure as the basis of their art despite its association with tradition. Many painters and sculptors, especially those who relied on sales of their work for income, adopted relatively conservative styles to appeal to art dealers and collectors.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Members of the French royal family and nobility are portrayed on many of the medals shown here. On the reverse of the medals are images and inscriptions celebrating the sitter's virtues or accomplishments, such as a military victory. Medals are both portable and durable and could be reproduced, making them ideal for spreading and preserving an individual's fame and achievements. Ancient Roman rulers who were admired in seventeenth-century France had used coins for this purpose. Louis XIV (1638–1715) relied on medals to promote the powerful royal image he had carefully cultivated as part of his mission to strengthen the monarchy. Also displayed in this gallery are carved hardstone cups, enameled watches, bronze statuettes, and other precious objects representative of the French nobility's taste for luxurious collectibles.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

properties.popup_header: Patio from the Castle of Vélez Blanco, 1506–15
properties.popup_body:
Vélez Blanco Patio

Masks, vases, and birds eating berries are among the sensitively carved details adorning the windows and other architectural elements of this marble patio from the castle of Vélez Blanco in Andalusia, Spain. The decoration also features a rich array of fantastic creatures, including sphinxes, dragons, and rams with serpent tails. These motifs were inspired by the ornamental vocabulary of ancient Rome as interpreted by Italian stonecutters working in Spain during the early sixteenth century. The coats of arms of the owners—members of the powerful Mendoza family—are carved between the patio's arches. Inner courtyards were an important feature of Spanish castles during this period, providing privacy as well as access to light and fresh air.

The owner of the castle sold the patio's marble fittings in 1904. George Blumenthal purchased them in 1913 and had them installed in his Park Avenue home. The nearly two thousand blocks of marble that form the structure were reassembled at the Museum in 1964. Monumental Italian sculptures from the sixteenth and early seventeenth centuries are displayed in the patio.
The entrance foyer to "Art of Native America," with rotating displays of Native American art.

Over the course of the sixteenth century, French royal power became increasingly centralized, and courtly etiquette grew more formal and refined. François I (1494–1547; ruled 1515–47), known to his contemporaries and to later historians as the Father of Arts and Letters, gave impetus to this trend by attracting to his court humanists and artists from Italy and other parts of Europe. Working under François and his successors at the château de Fontainebleau and elsewhere, these artists developed a new vocabulary of fantastic shapes, abstract patterns, natural motifs, and elongated figures, whose sinuous proportions were in keeping with Italian Mannerist taste but also harked back to native Gothic aesthetics. Diffused through prints, their innovations were applied to a wide range of art forms, not least among them those of the decorative arts. The excellence of the resulting works reflects a long tradition of French craftsmanship as well as the heightened demand for luxury objects from an increasingly sophisticated, largely noble clientele. Although possessing functional shapes, many of the pieces exhibited here, such as the Saint-Porchaire ware and the tin-enameled earthenware dishes, were meant exclusively for display, as their fragility and often complex narratives make clear.

properties.popup_header: Bedroom from the Sagredo Palace in Venice, ca. 1718
properties.popup_body: Movement, theatricality, and a harmonious marriage of different materials—defining features of the early eighteenth-century Italian Baroque style—characterize the decoration of this room. Twenty-nine winged angels, or putti, modeled in stucco (a mixture of plaster and glue or resin) seem poised to detach themselves from the walls and glide through the air, creating a joyful atmosphere. The richly ornamented ceilings include the painting Dawn by Gaspare Diziani, and a dome surrounded by a stucco imitation of a canopy with cascading folds. Silk fabric and wood panels carved with designs inspired by architecture and nature cover the walls. The bed, not original to the room, exhibits a later, more curvaceous style. Located on the piano nobile (second floor), such a grand bedroom suite might have served as an official reception room rather than sleeping quarters. Less ornate bedchambers were situated in the more private upper floors of palaces.

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

Lansdowne House, located off of London’s Berkeley Square, belonged to two (rather unpopular) British prime ministers: John Stuart, 3rd Earl of Bute, and William Petty Fitzmaurice, 1st Marquess of Lansdowne. The latter was despised by George III, but his efforts on behalf of the American colonies were appreciated abroad. Benjamin Franklin, David Garrick and Samuel Johnson were among the many who made Lansdowne House the center of the most liberal and cultivated society in London. Its dining room, now displayed as a sculpture gallery, features two Canovas similar to those once in the collection of the 3rd Marquess.

The Sharp Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing


While many artists responded to modern urban life by withdrawing into the mind or spirit, others retreated into history or nature. Some sought pleasant, soothing, and even restorative personal and artistic experiences, wherein contemporary life could give way to timelessness and transcendence.

properties.popup_header: European Arms and Armor, Late Medieval to Renaissance
properties.popup_body:
Bashford Dean Gallery in the Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Ronald S. Lauder Gallery includes rare and distinctive examples of early field and tournament armor, swords, shields, and crossbows that span the development of the art of the armorer from the fourteenth century through the early sixteenth century in Italy, Germany, and other parts of Europe.

properties.popup_header: Robert Lehman Collection Gallery for Special Exhibitions
properties.popup_body:
Robert Lehman Collection

This gallery often displays the Robert Lehman Collection's nineteenth- and twentieth-century European paintings and also houses a range of exhibitions.

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

The production and consumption of textiles was hugely important for eighteenth-century British society. The regularly changing display of textiles in these galleries are representative of the styles and influences from abroad that have shaped Britain‚Äôs domestic productions.

properties.popup_header: Making It Modern: European Ceramics from the Martin Eidelberg Collection
properties.popup_body:
Ceramic artists in Europe at the turn of the century sought to break from the past by developing new, distinctive modes of making. With a renewed interest in the natural world as a source of artistic inspiration, they experimented with decoration, forms, and ideas to make their art modern.

Making It Modern: European Ceramics from the Martin Eidelberg Collection celebrates Dr. Martin Eidelberg’s gift to The Met of approximately 80 ceramic works dating from the 1880s to 1910s. Although the years in which the Eidelberg ceramics were produced coincided with the emergence of the popular Art Nouveau movement in which artists brought abstracted forms from nature into their work, few of these pieces align closely with the common conception of that style. Instead, these ceramics reflect the desire for individual artistic expression and the enormous creativity that make this period such a rich chapter in European ceramic history.

This exhibition is accompanied by an issue of The Metropolitan Museum of Art Bulletin.

The Met’s quarterly Bulletin program is supported, in part, by the Lila Acheson Wallace Fund for The Metropolitan Museum of Art, established by the co-founder of Reader’s Digest.
  
Exhibition Open: Through June 14, 2026

The Iris and B. Gerald Cantor Galleries
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

Complex, lavish, and eclectic, the works of art in this gallery are both deliberately conservative and highly inventive. During the first half of the nineteenth century, European artists and designers sought inspiration in the art of the past. Gothic- and Renaissance-revival styles were especially popular. Artists, however, rarely desired stylistic purity and had little interest in making faithful reproductions of earlier works. They developed a new decorative vocabulary, combining motifs from different artistic moments to create objects that referenced several pasts at once. Revival styles emerged largely as a response to the changes affecting Europe during this period, marked by the Industrial Revolution and widespread imperial conquest. Uneasiness about the future fostered romantic nostalgia for the artistic achievements of previous centuries, which offered a source of comfort and national pride. In revisiting the past, artists played an important role in proclaiming the endurance of their nations' cultural excellence.

Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Attempting to identify desirable artistic alternatives to staid and limiting European traditions, many artists looked to unindustrialized cultures outside of Western Europe for inspiration. Modern artists rather romantically interpreted the art of these diverse cultures to be authentic and close to nature.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this gallery are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces. The range of objects displayed here will delight the viewer, who can study painted textiles, bronze statuettes representing a variety of deities, faience funerary figurines, and hundreds of amulets from the period between about 1100 B.C. and A.D. 300. In the center, several cases contain elaborately decorated coffins that are important to the understanding of ancient Egyptian funerary practices. The east wall case displays objects from the Nubian kingdom of Meroe. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

Before its removal in 1931, the dining room at Kirtlington Park, in Oxfordshire, was widely regarded as one of the most beautiful Rococo rooms in England. Sir James Dashwood devoted much of his energy and fortune to the building and furnishing of Kirtlington Park, which he inherited at the age of nineteen while on the Grand Tour in Italy. Kirtlington interiors were designed by the somewhat obscure London architect John Sanderson, who designs exhibit distinctly Palladian influences.

Ronald S. Lauder Gallery in the Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Ronald S. Lauder Gallery focuses on lavishly decorated ceremonial armor, shields, and weapons of the sixteenth century from Germany, Italy, and France. These works exemplify artistic styles from the high Renaissance through Mannerism in exuberant etching, embossing, and gold and silver ornament. Highlights include a superbly sculptural helmet in the antique style by Filippo Negroli and an ornate armor made for Henry II, King of France, that embodies the king both as a warrior and a patron of the arts.

properties.popup_header: Prehistoric and Early Greek Art: Fifth Millennium–ca. 700 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The Robert and Renée Belfer Court

Gallery 151, located on the west side of The Robert and Renée Belfer Court, represents several regions—the Cycladic Islands, Crete, and the parts of the Greek mainland centered around Mycenae—where major works of art were first produced. The respective cultures, known as Cycladic, Minoan, and Mycenaean, flourished during the prehistoric period known as the Bronze Age, between about 3200 and 1050 B.C.

Displayed nearby are works of the subsequent Geometric period of about 1050–700 B.C., when the first Greek city-states and sanctuaries were established.

Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


This gallery includes the finest display outside of Japan of Samurai armor, edged weapons, equestrian equipment, and accessories from the Kofun Period in the fifth century to the end of the Edo Period in the late nineteenth century. The centerpiece of the gallery is the armor of Ashikaga Takauji, Shogun of Japan in the early fourteenth century.

properties.popup_header: Late Roman and Early Byzantine Secular Objects (North Gallery)
properties.popup_body:
Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries

Part of the Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries, this area exhibits late Roman and early Byzantine secular art alongside contemporaneous art from the Latin West. Highlights of the gallery include the magnificent set of silver David Plates, a floor mosaic with a personification of Ktisis (Generous Giving), delicate gold jewelry, and beautifully carved marble portrait busts.

The gallery also displays domestic furnishings, lamps, and measuring devices. The distinctive jewelry produced in the emerging kingdoms of western Europe finds its place here, as does an intriguing hoard of gold and silver associated with the nomadic Avars.

This vast, light-filled space presents the Museum's unsurpassed collection of American monumental sculpture, architectural elements, and stained glass. It is the grand vestibule to the American Wing, which houses period rooms and galleries for American decorative arts, paintings, and sculpture.

The south end of the court houses the exotic entrance loggia designed by Louis C. Tiffany for Laurelton Hall, his country estate on Long Island. Behind and at either side of the Tiffany loggia is a pair of staircases, each two stories high, from the Chicago Stock Exchange Building designed by Louis H. Sullivan in 1893. Selections of stained-glass windows by Tiffany, Arts and Crafts metalwork by Samuel Yellin, and relief sculptures by Augustus Saint-Gaudens complement the architectural elements.

The Robert and Renée Belfer Court

On the east side of The Robert and Renée Belfer Court, this gallery is devoted to the Archaic period of about 700 to 480 B.C. The emphasis is on works that display the colorful, complex multiplicity that characterized the Greek world for more than two centuries.

Featured are the arts of the Greek colonies in western Asia Minor, Southern Italy, Sicily, Crete, and various areas of mainland Greece that flourished at this time: Corinth, Lakonia, Boeotia, Euboea, and Attica.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Some of the most famous objects in The Met’s collection of Egyptian art are displayed in this gallery. The pristinely preserved models depicting food production, a garden, a graceful female offering bearer, and six magnificent boats were created to activate rituals that would provide the chief royal steward, Meketre, with the activities and goods they represented in the afterlife. Nearby is the funerary equipment of Wah, Meketre’s estate manager. His jewelry is the finest known from this time and his mummy mask portrays a vibrant face.

properties.popup_header: The Early New Kingdom Study Room
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

In this gallery, the objects are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces among the thousands of architectural fragments, small statues, tombstones (stelae), cosmetic containers, jewelry, and dozens of amulets dating between 1550 and 1300 B.C. Also of interest is the collection of cattle, goat, and sheep skulls with horns (bucrania) found as part of Pan Grave tombs, which date to about 1650 B.C. These pieces represent people whose heritage was most likely to be found among the nomadic people of Egypt’s Eastern desert.

Evoking a church interior, Gallery 305 is dominated by a complete Spanish choir screen from the Valladolid cathedral of 1763.

Large-scale medieval sculptures, altarpieces, tapestries, and furniture dated from about 1300 to 1500 are exhibited in the gallery. In addition, fine, smaller-scale Northern European sculptures in wood and stone are displayed in cases along the east and west sides of the gallery.

Robert Lehman Collection

Gallery 956 houses an exceptional collection of Renaissance paintings from Siena that, along with the holdings of the Department of European Paintings, constitutes one of the finest of its kind outside the Tuscan city.

Among the fifteenth-century Sienese masters, Sano di Pietro and Giovanni di Paolo are particularly well represented. In addition to images of the Virgin, there are engaging narrative scenes depicting lives of the saints, set in richly articulated interior spaces and extraordinary landscapes. Frequently, these small narrative panels were part of an altarpiece's predella, the lowermost horizontal component.

The Carolyn, Kate, Elizabeth, Thomas, and Jonathan Wiener Gallery 


The Carolyn, Kate, Elizabeth, Thomas, and Jonathan Wiener Gallery presents a fine collection of marble grave markers from Athens dating from the mid-fifth century B.C. through the early fourth century B.C.

In the fifth century B.C., the Greeks reached levels of excellence in art, literature, and philosophical thought that have served as models and formed the foundation for much of Western European culture. Despite wars—first against the Persians and then against Sparta, its major Greek rival—Athens became the political, economic, and cultural leader of Greece, producing works of art and literature of the highest quality.

properties.popup_header: From the Pyramid Complex of King Amenemhat I at Lisht North
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery displays important finds from the Museum's excavation of the pyramid complex of Amenemhat I, who reigned for some thirty-one years beginning around 1980 B.C. He was the founder of Dynasty 12, a cultural high point in the history of ancient Egypt. The room features reliefs from the king’s temples, an imposing offering table, a royal head from a sphinx, as well as objects once buried under the walls to protect his new temple.

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

Tea, sugar, coffee, and cocoa were commodities that drove Britain's artistic innovation from the late seventeenth through the eighteenth century. The sudden abundance of these products motivated local makers to supply novel objects that assisted in their consumption, such as the teapot. Makers embraced spontaneity and freedom as they experimented with designs for both pricey and inexpensive price points. Tea had become the emblem of a mercantile empire that extended to the Indian subcontinent, the West Indies, and America. But stories of violence and oppression corrupted this national success, as much of the wealth of this period was built on the labor of enslaved Africans and on the appropriated resources of other countries.

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

In 1760, George William, 6th Earl of Coventry engaged the fashionable architect Robert Adam to design several of the principal rooms for Croome Court, including this tapestry room. In the immediate aftermath of the Seven Years War, Coventry traveled to Paris where he visited the Royal Gobelins Manufactory and commissioned this suite designed by the architect Jacques Germain Soufflot with medallions containing scenes by François Boucher. His new design, which could be adjusted to fit any size wall, allowed for endless variation of animals and flowers in the decorative surrounds, and could be complemented with matching furniture covers.

Robert Lehman Collection

Gallery 961 and Gallery 962 display the Lehman Collection's nineteenth- and twentieth-century European paintings, representing a variety of schools, including Impressionism, Post-Impressionism, Neo-Impressionism, the Nabis, and Fauvism. The collection of modern European paintings reflects the particular taste of a collector who acquired modern art often from the artists themselves.

Spyros and Eurydice Costopoulos Gallery

Most of the works of art in the Spyros and Eurydice Costopoulos Gallery are funerary monuments, including large-scale marble vases that were used as grave markers.

In addition, terracotta statuettes of fashionable women or girls, prized today for their naturalness, vitality, and charm, are displayed here. The jewelry in this gallery presents the wealth of the Classical Greek world on the eve of the Hellenistic period that flourished after the death of Alexander the Great in 323 B.C.

properties.popup_header: Art of the Later Roman Empire: Third Century A.D.
properties.popup_body:
John A. and Carole O. Moran Gallery
The Lamont Wing

The works on view in the John A. and Carole O. Moran Gallery demonstrate changes in social and artistic trends that emerged during the later imperial period. One of these was the adoption of burial (internment) in place of cremation, prompting the creation of an empire-wide industry making large and imposing marble sarcophagi. Oriental cults spread across the Roman world and exerted a growing influence on people's lives in this period of political and economic uncertainty.

Also on view is a display of gold, silver, and bronze coins that were minted primarily to supply money for state expenditure and to facilitate the collection of taxes.

Carroll and Milton Petrie European Sculpture Court
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

The Carroll and Milton Petrie European Sculpture Court features large-scale sculpture presented chronologically from the east end to the west end of the gallery. These mythological, allegorical, and historical works highlight the range of expressive content and visual effects that can be achieved in stone and metal. The material is all-important in sculpture—affecting how light and shadow play across the surface, and furthering the ambition of the design. Carving projecting limbs in stone, for example, requires tremendous skill since the potential for breakage is great. With bronze, sculptors could create a deep, lustrous finish by applying varnishes or chemicals to the surface to form a patina. This coloration also occurs naturally over time as a result of the metal's exposure to air. Several of the works in this gallery were originally displayed in palace gardens, acting as dramatic focal points in carefully arranged natural settings.

This room comes from a townhouse built for Henry Craig (1767–1823), a Baltimore merchant and ship owner. Although it served as the Craig family's parlor, it is here presented and furnished as a dining room in order to display examples of the adoption and adaptation of the English Neoclassical style in the early Federal period.

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

Robert Lehman Collection

The Netherlandish and German paintings of the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries on view in this gallery demonstrate the remarkably naturalistic effects and great luminosity that artists of the Northern Renaissance achieved by mixing ground pigments with oil.

In Petrus Christus' Goldsmith in his Shop, for example, we see finely wrought details of goldsmiths' work in what appears to be a vocational painting or a genre scene.

This gallery also displays several examples of bronze aquamanilia, water vessels that were used for hand washing in both religious and secular contexts.

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

properties.popup_header: Hellenistic Sculpture and Architecture: Third–First Century B.C.
properties.popup_body:
Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The Leon Levy and Shelby White Gallery features Hellenistic portraits of marble and bronze, monumental painted terracotta vases from Southern Italy, and architectural fragments from the Temple of Artemis at Sardis, including the massive marble Ionic column and elements of the doorway, porch, and roof.

Sardis was the ancient capital of Lydia, in southwestern Turkey, and its temple to Artemis ranks among the seven largest of all Greek temples.

The Florence Gould Galleries
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

The smooth contours and restrained ornamentation of the objects and furniture in this gallery were largely inspired by the art and architecture of the ancient world. Interest in classical antiquity was stimulated by the archaeological explorations of the ancient Roman sites of Herculaneum and Pompeii, rediscovered in 1738 and 1748, respectively. Vases, columns, garlands, and other motifs derived from ancient buildings, sculpture, and paintings influenced the forms and decoration of three-dimensional objects produced in a variety of materials.

The initial, rather literal, dependence on classical decoration gradually became more creative. In the early nineteenth century, Napoleon favored a more formal Neoclassical style heavily indebted to architecture, and intended to evoke France's association with imperial Rome and conquest of Egypt. Napoleon's power ensured the spread of the so-called "French Empire style" to all corners of Europe.

The Marietta Lutze Sackler Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Throughout the early twentieth century, abstraction developed in painting, sculpture, and design, ranging in style from geometric to organic, with variations in between. Many artists and designers considered abstraction to be universal and egalitarian because a viewer's appreciation of abstract art did not depend on learned knowledge of history or literature.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

On the east side, the cases contain objects deposited under the walls of Pharaoh Hatshepsut’s temple, including a large number of beautifully carved scarabs. In the center, the contents of Hatnefer’s burial are exhibited. Her son Senenmut gave her a worthy burial that included food, cosmetics, jewelry, furniture, linen sheets, and a gilded mask. Nearby, the funerary equipment of a Theban family has been bought together. A charming statue names and depicts each family member, including the father, Neferkhawet, who was in charge of an ancient workshop. This sculpture gives faces to the people who once used the adjacent burial goods.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The reliefs in this gallery come from some of the temples commissioned by King Nectanebo I, whose rule began in 380 B.C., and Nectanebo II, who followed shortly thereafter. These were the last indigenous pharaohs of ancient Egypt and they invigorated the arts by carrying out extensive building programs. Statues from this time exhibit a remarkable sensitivity in the rendering of the human body, for example, the kneeling ritual figure delicately captures in wood a king in the action of honoring a divine being. The large stone slab known as a stela is the finest example of a type of magically potent monument and the ancient Egyptians believed that water poured over such an object had healing powers.

properties.popup_header: The Black Room: Late First Century B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The Lamont Wing

This reconstruction of a room from the villa of Agrippa Postumus at Boscotrecase incorporates surviving panels from the original walls. They must have been painted by artists working for the imperial household during renovations after 11 B.C.

Compared to the paintings found at Boscoreale (see Gallery 165), these panels reflect a new and different style, known as Third Style, with a taste for insubstantial, delicate forms set against a flat, monochrome surface and including Egyptianizing motifs and medallions with portraits of members of the imperial family.

properties.popup_header: The Studiolo from the Ducal Palace at Gubbio, 1478–82
properties.popup_body: The walls of this studiolo, or study room, are decorated with images made using wood inlay, or intarsia. This demanding and expensive technique involves cutting grained woods of different colors into thin pieces, or veneers, shaped according to the desired design and laid into a matrix. Optical illusions abound, such as the cabinet doors that appear to be ajar and the shadows seemingly cast by the legs of the benches. The decorative program of the studiolo reflects the personality of its powerful patron, Federico da Montefeltro, Duke of Urbino (1422–1482), whose interests included mathematics, music, and literature. His emblems, such as the ermine (a small animal symbolizing purity) and the ostrich with a spearhead in its beak (representing victory in the face of adversity), are incorporated in the decoration. Studioli became an increasingly important feature of Italian Renaissance palaces, providing their owners with an intimate retreat where they could engage in learned pursuits, keep their precious collections, and entertain important visitors.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery houses some of the finest examples of the creativity and skill available to Middle Kingdom and later rulers from about 2000 to 1550 B.C. Highlighted at the gallery’s entrance, the extraordinary jewelry of Princess Sithathoryunet dazzles visitors. Important pieces of royal sculpture grace the room, including the imposing sphinx of King Senwosret III and the guardian figure wearing a Red Crown that dates to about 1890 B.C. The Met’s “mascot,” the faience hippo known as William also resides here along with the faces of many officials. Jewelry from the time of the Hyksos speaks to the wealth commanded by these Egyptian kings bearing Semitic names, who ruled northern Egypt from a Delta stronghold known as Tell el Da’ba from about 1650 to 1550 B.C.

properties.popup_header: Italian Baroque Sculpture and Decorative Arts, 1600–1750
properties.popup_body:
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

Brimming with energy and opulence, the works of art in this gallery belong to the Baroque, a style that originated in early seventeenth- century Rome. The furniture and liturgical accessories reflect the sumptuousness of the Baroque style, as well as the virtuosity of Italian craftsmen. The popes were leading patrons of Baroque art, using it to proclaim the supremacy of the Catholic Church as part of their mission to curtail the spread of Protestantism. Baroque sculptors appealed to their audience's emotions by giving their figures dynamic poses and using theatrical touches such as billowing draperies and swirling clouds to heighten the expressiveness of their compositions.

properties.popup_header: Reliefs from a Temple of Ramesses I
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art
