The reliefs exhibited in this space come from a chapel that King Seti I, who reigned around 1300 B.C., built at Abydos to honor his father, Ramesses I. The center scene shows the two kings worshiping the "fetish" of Osiris, a symbol of the chief god of the underworld and main deity worshipped at the pilgrimage site of Abydos. Another relief shows Ramesses' I face with signs of age: a rare example of realism in the representation of a royal individual.

Dick Wolf Gallery


Many of the Renaissance works of art in this gallery are in pristine condition, which suggests they were objects for display rather than practical use. They share a decorative vocabulary born of the Italian desire to emulate and supersede their ancient Roman forebears. An ornamental language with fantastic figures—one bird or beast transforming into another—twisting vegetation, and strict geometry was discovered in the 1480s within the buried palace of the Roman emperor Nero. These motifs ignited the imaginations of Renaissance artists working in different materials.

Among Italy's specialties was maiolica—tin-glazed earthenware with elaborate narratives, often mythological, painted in brilliant colors. Even more precious were the porcelains, imitating Chinese blue-and-white vessels, commissioned by the powerful Medici family of Florence. Stonecutting and inlay were other areas of expertise; the imposing table in this room was made in Rome for Cardinal Alessandro Farnese (1520–1589) using material excavated from ancient sites. Italy's rich and varied artistic output was stimulated by the demands of discerning patrons who wished to have their taste and affluence reflected in the objects surrounding them.

The Bothmer Gallery II

The Bothmer Gallery II, named for Dietrich von Bothmer, provides examples of Athenian vase painting between about 530 and 400 B.C., when the flexibility of the newly developed red-figure technique permitted artists to draw freely over the convex and concave surfaces of vases.

A high proportion of the vases were made for containing, pouring, and drinking wine. On view also are significant examples of painted marble and terracotta sculpture, as well as coins and gems.

See also Gallery 155.

The illusionistic panels displayed here were created in 1547–48 by juxtaposing pieces of different-colored wood to create secular and religious compositions that mimic the effects of painting. This technique, called intarsia, flourished in northern Italy, where these panels were made. They were commissioned for a chapel in the château built in the Naussac region of France by Claude d'Urfé (1501–1558), whose interest in Italian art intensified after serving as royal ambassador to Rome.

Appreciating the inherent properties of wood was central to creating intarsia, in much the same way that understanding the fragility and transparency of glass was key to designing stained-glass windows. By the sixteenth century, advances in glass cutting reduced the need for lead joins and supports, allowing a glass painter to treat the window surface more like a canvas. The windows on view, made for a church in the Lorraine region between 1531 and 1533, are remarkable for their lofty figures, plunging landscapes, and details such as the architectural frames and armor derived from the artistic vocabulary of ancient Greece and Rome.

The Lamont Wing

Designed as an intimate space that is conducive to the appreciation of exquisite small-scale works of refined beauty, this gallery houses some of the most important treasures of Hellenistic art in the Museum's collection. It features gold, silver, and glass vessels from different regions of the Hellenistic world, including Magna Graecia, Egypt, the Ionian coast of Anatolia, and Parthia.

Fine necklaces, diadems, finger rings, earrings, and gold armbands provide a glimpse of the mastery of the goldsmiths' art for articles of personal adornment during the third through first centuries B.C.

The Wrightsman Galleries

This carved-oak shop front (1775–77) from the Île Saint-Louis, Paris, reveals how significant the taste for decoration evoking ancient Greece and Rome, as expressed in the Neoclassical style, had become in France. It is not known what type of merchandise was originally shown behind this shop's windows. Catering to an upscale clientele, however, the marchands merciers (dealers in luxury goods) who owned such shops displayed precious silver, snuffboxes, and mounted porcelain pieces. These objects were made especially to attract the attention of the discriminating consumer. While the marchands merciers did not execute the pieces themselves, they played a role in their design and arranged for upkeep and repair.

properties.popup_header: Temporary Exhibition Space for Departmental Rotations
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery is devoted to departmental rotations. The sarcophagus that anchors the gallery space once belonged to a man named Wennefer, who lived in the fourth century B.C.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the eighteenth century, continued to transform society and work in the early twentieth century. Painters and sculptors celebrated these feats of industrial might and innovation, incorporating the streamlined forms and contours of assembly-line production into their imagery. Many artists with leftist political leanings exalted physical work rather than industry and tended to identify personally with laborers.

properties.popup_header: European Armor for Field and Tournament
properties.popup_body:
Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Ronald S. Lauder Arms and Armor Court offers the most extensive selection in the United States of rare and finely made sixteenth- and seventeenth-century European armor for men and horses, created for kings and noblemen to use on the battlefield and in tournaments.

The gallery features a group of elaborately decorated Greenwich armors, from the English Royal workshops founded by King Henry VIII, and one of Henry's personal armors, made in Italy and worn by the king in his last campaign against the French at Calais in 1544.

The Haverhill Room is the formal parlor (now furnished as a bedroom) from a house that originally belonged to James Duncan Jr. (1756–1822). Its interior represents the Neoclassical style of architecture that prevailed during the early 1800s and provides an appropriate backdrop for the American Wing's strong collection of New England furniture from this era.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

properties.popup_header: Art of Augustan Rome: Late First Century B.C.–First Century A.D.
properties.popup_body:
Sylvia Josephs Berger and Joyce Berger Cowin Gallery

The Lamont Wing

The Sylvia Josephs Berger and Joyce Berger Cowin Gallery contains many exquisite examples of Roman imperial art. The reign of the first emperor Augustus (r. 27 B.C.–A.D. 14) saw the development of a new form of state art, the imperial portrait, created in both lifesize statuary and small-scale works such as gems and coins. Imperial patronage of the arts is evident in monumental architecture and sculpture as well as in other media such as wall paintings, silverware, and glass.

Much of the inspiration for the Augustan style came from the Greek East, as did many of the architects, sculptors, and artists themselves.

Robert Lehman Collection

Gallery 961 and Gallery 962 display the Lehman Collection's nineteenth- and twentieth-century European paintings, representing a variety of schools, including Impressionism, Post-Impressionism, Neo-Impressionism, the Nabis, and Fauvism. The collection of modern European paintings reflects the particular taste of a collector who acquired modern art often from the artists themselves.

Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Ronald S. Lauder Gallery of firearms is devoted to hunting and sporting weapons as intricate and evocative works of art, featuring richly decorated European firearms, crossbows, and accessories from the sixteenth to the nineteenth century.

Examples highlight the art of the gun maker from the Renaissance, Baroque, Rococo, and Neoclassical periods, including weapons that belonged to Emperor Charles V and King Louis XIII of France.

properties.popup_header: Egypt under Roman Rule in the Second to Fourth Centuries AD
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The pieces in this gallery mark the last phase of ancient Egyptian culture as the last Egyptian temple closed around A.D. 400. The beautifully adorned mummy of a woman named Artemidora is the focus of the room. There are panel portraits, including the young boy, Eutyches, as well as funerary masks of plaster. One case holds glass vessels, while another has a fine example of funerary portrait of a woman painted on linen. Many objects here illustrate a mixture of Greco-Roman and Egyptian styles, for example the Isis-Aphrodite statue and the gold jewelry. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this room record the early history of people in the Nile Valley beginning around 300,000 B.C. through the time of Egypt’s first pharaohs, whose last king died about 2700 B.C. Most pieces were excavated from burials and were items that people believed necessary for the next life. The images seen in and on many of these objects reflect life in and around the Nile River, floodplain, and the desert and many of the themes expressed continued into the Pharaonic period. One case presents sets of objects whose arrangements suggests how Predynastic burials of different periods looked.

properties.popup_header: Objects from Malqata, the Festival-City of Amenhotep III
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Malqata is the modern name of the festival-city built by King Amenhotep III around 1350 B.C. to celebrate his three jubilees. The city had two mudbrick palaces, a vast harbor, a temple, a viewing platform, administrative quarters, manufacturing areas, and small settlements. The objects on view include pieces of decorated floors and walls and a collection of richly painted pottery vessels. Fragments from glass vessels and amulets document the activities carried out within the city. Hundreds of ink inscriptions, which labelled the contents of containers used in the celebrations and smashed afterwards, are the source of the sherds on display.

properties.popup_header: Statues of the Female King Hatshepsut
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The greatest female king, Hatshepsut, commissioned the monumental sculptures in this gallery to adorn her spectacular funerary temple at Deir el-Bahri, near modern day Luxor. Sometime after her death around 1450 B.C., her co-regent and successor Thutmose III, who ruled more than fifty-four years, ordered the destruction of her images. In order to remove any memory of her rule, Hatshepsut’s sculptures were smashed into thousands of pieces. The Met excavated the fragments and painstakingly reassembled many destroyed sculptures, the most complete of which remained in Egypt. Twelve reassembled statues are exhibited here, re-creating the splendor of temple statuary destroyed some thirty-five hundred years ago.

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

properties.popup_header: Middle and Late Byzantine Objects (Apse Gallery)
properties.popup_body:
Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries

Within the Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries, the Apse Gallery exhibits religious works of art from the middle and late Byzantine periods.

Visitors enter the gallery through an evocation of a templon, the barrier that separates the sanctuary of the Byzantine church from the congregation. On view are the exquisite Jaharis Gospel Lectionary; ivory, cloisonné enamel, and painted icons; crosses and cross reliquaries; and objects made for use in the church. Also showcased are ivories and metalwork created in the early Medieval West, under Carolingian and Ottonian rulers as well as the Vikings.

Robert Lehman Collection

Like a cabinet of wonders, the six cases in this gallery contain small, precious decorative objects that were similarly displayed in the Lehman residence, including porcelain, ancient Roman glass, eighteenth-century French glass figurines, and dioramas of religious scenes fashioned from a variety of materials. Among the jewelry on display is an important pocket watch that once belonged to Louis XIV, King of France.

The gallery is crowned by a stained-glass dome that originally formed a skylight in the Lehman home. It may have been comissioned for the Lehman residence, built in 1904.

Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries

Part of the Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries, this space features the arts of Byzantium, the empire based in Constantinople that ruled from 330–1453 C.E. In addition to early liturgical and personal religious objects, the gallery exhibits secular art from later Byzantine centuries, including carved ivory caskets, stunning cloisonné enamels, and ceramics.

Also on view are a monumental sarcophagus and gold glass from Rome, Judaic objects, Kievan Rus' enamels, and Armenian manuscripts.

Annie Laurie Aitken Galleries

These galleries are about the seduction of retail in the eighteenth-century: business boomed in Georgian Britain with private commissions from aristocrats inspired by their international education, Enlightenment thinking, and a desire to cast Britain's stature in the mold of ancient Rome. Classicism and Rococo took hold as a profound intellectual ideal and a ubiquitous design source while London's artistic community exploded into a collaborative scramble of talent, money, opportunity, and competition.

Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries (Gallery 904 through Gallery 907) feature paintings and sculptures from the Gelman collection, which the Museum received in 1998. The collection's emphasis is on the School of Paris, comprised of French and often non-French artists who lived and worked in France before and after World War I.

On view are works by Pierre Bonnard, Georges Braque, Alberto Giacometti, Fernand Léger, Henri Matisse, Joan Miró, Pablo Picasso, Yves Tanguy, and others. This permanent installation offers a tour through the European Modernist movement from the turn of the twentieth century to the 1970s.

Pierre and Maria-Gaetana Matisse Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The idea of the metropolis became a powerful symbol of modern life. For many onlookers, including artists, New York was the archetypal modern city, a densely populated, dynamic space that embodied "the new." The symbolic newness of the metropolis was often distilled in art and design in the form of the skyscraper. An undeniable sign of human achievement, the modern city could also feel overpowering and dehumanizing.

properties.popup_header: Bedroom from the Sagredo Palace in Venice, ca. 1718
properties.popup_body: The coat of arms of the Sagredo family hangs in this gallery, above the entrance to the bedroom from the Sagredo palace that once overlooked the Grand Canal in Venice (Gallery 507). Below, gondola prows flank the doorway. The gallery also contains a selection of eighteenth-century Italian decorative arts, including religious sculpture in glazed porcelain from Naples and Florence, as well as an embroidered chasuble (vestment).

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

The objects in this gallery are exhibited as if in a storeroom, allowing visitors to discover their own favorite pieces among the thousands of relief fragments, figurines, scarabs, amulets, vessels, and pieces of personal jewelry dating between 1980 and 1550 B.C. Archaeologists, mostly working for The Met, excavated these pieces from Lisht in northern Egypt, as well as Abydos and Thebes in the southern part of the country in the early 20th century.

properties.popup_header: Early New Kingdom Tombs from the Asasif
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This gallery emphasizes the excavations The Met conducted at Asasif, a cemetery east of Hatshepsut’s funerary temple at Deir el Bahri, near modern day Luxor. The display also includes objects from the site that The Met purchased from Lord Carnarvon, who sponsored excavations there with Howard Carter as his archaeologist. These fascinating tombs, dating around 1550 B.C., illustrate what was important funerary equipment at the time: coffins, vessels, toilet equipment, and jewelry. Their forms indicate the development of new styles that surfaced after the struggles of the Second Intermediate Period concluded about 1550 B.C. [Human remains reside in this gallery.]

properties.popup_header: The Jack and Belle Linsky Collection
properties.popup_body:
Jack and Belle Linsky Galleries

Seven galleries were opened in 1982 to house the Jack and Belle Linsky Collection of almost four hundred European sculptures, paintings, and decorative arts from the fifteenth to the nineteenth century. Amassed over more than forty years, these works reflect the Linskys' highly personal collecting style, and their admiration for precious materials and rich details. Highlights include a mechanical table made for Madame de Pompadour (1721–1764), the mistress of Louis XV, and rare examples of eighteenth-century French furniture displayed in settings that evoke the Linskys' Fifth Avenue apartment.

In the 1920s the Linskys began assembling a distinguished collection of eighteenth-century European porcelain from most of the major centers of ceramic production. The figures they favored reflect the contemporary fascination with representing individuals engaged in the activities of daily life, as well as characters from the popular Italian theater known as commedia dell'arte.

properties.popup_header: Afterlives: Contemporary Art in the Byzantine Crypt
properties.popup_body:
Artists and artisans have been intrigued and inspired by the topic of death and visions of life thereafter for millennia. Afterlives: Contemporary Art in the Byzantine Crypt brings together modern-day works that reckon with death and visualize the afterlife and Byzantine Egyptian funerary art and artifacts in part of the Mary and Michael Jaharis Galleries known as the Byzantine Crypt (Gallery 302). The intimate and enchanting gallery with exposed brick walls and arched portals was unveiled in the year 2000 after a renovation that reclaimed the space beneath the Museum’s Grand Staircase. In this transhistorical presentation, the Byzantine Crypt’s religious and secular jewelry, textiles, ivory objects, vessels, and architectural sculpture from Early Christian and Coptic monastic sites  are complemented and enriched by contemporary sculptures, works on paper, and installations from the 1960s to present day that similarly  serve as memorials, reliquaries, and tokens to ward off evil.

As part of the exhibition, Adrian Piper’s editioned work Everything #4 invites viewers to contemplate its otherworldly statement and their reflection in a variety of contexts. It will be installed in the heart of the Byzantine Crypt and five other spaces across The Met Fifth Avenue and The Met Cloisters, listed below.

The Met Fifth Avenue:Gallery 302 – Byzantine CryptGallery 512 – European Sculpture and Decorative ArtsGallery 617 – European PaintingsGallery 726 – The American WingGallery 914 – Modern and Contemporary ArtUnknown block type "image", specify a component for it in the `components.types` optionThe Met Cloisters:Gallery 9 – Gothic Chapel

The exhibition is made possible by The Jaharis Family Foundation.
  
Exhibition Open: Through January 10, 2027

properties.popup_header: Hellenistic Art: Third Century B.C.–First Century A.D.
properties.popup_body:
Tarsi and John Georgas Family Gallery

The Lamont Wing

The Tarsi and John Georgas Family Gallery features a variety of aspects of Hellenistic art of the third through first centuries B.C. and their transmission to the Roman world. Hellenistic artists drew from the serene Classical ideal but also moved in new directions, reflecting the many contacts with various cultures after the conquests of Alexander the Great.

Wall paintings from a Roman villa at Boscoreale—some of which echo lost Hellenistic Macedonian royal paintings—and exquisite bronze sculptures provide an impression of the refined Classical environment that was cultivated, especially in private homes.

Gallery 306 displays large Gothic sculptures from the Chateau of Biron in the Dordogne region of France, stained-glass panels, ivories, manuscripts, and goldsmiths' work from the later Middle Ages. The stained glass comes from important churches in Northern Europe. Liturgical objects made of precious materials such as silver and ivory are complemented by illuminated manuscripts, as well as by objects created in a variety of material for secular purposes, including personal adornment.

properties.popup_header: Facsimiles of Ancient Egyptian Tomb Paintings
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Artists, who were part of the Museum’s Graphic Expeditions to Egypt in the 1920s and 1930s, made the precise color copies (facsimiles) of scenes seen in this gallery that were then visible on the walls of New Kingdom tombs largely dating from about 1400 B.C. to 1100 B.C. They copied only what they saw, making the paintings to scale with exact coloring. These facsimiles are significant archival records of these tombs and Egyptologists continue to use them as important sources of information about this ancient culture.

properties.popup_header: Late Baroque and Rococo Decorative Arts, 1680–1760
properties.popup_body:
The Florence Gould Galleries

The Henry R. Kravis Wing

The furniture, sculptures, and objects in this gallery are vibrant and intricately adorned, making them truly captivating. The Baroque style, which originated in seventeenth-century Italy, is recognized for its sculptural quality and architectural symmetry, drawing inspiration from the classical Roman era. In contrast, the Rococo style emerged in the eighteenth century, focusing on lightness, delicacy, and movement as a reaction to the grand formality of Baroque. Artists began to incorporate motifs from nature, such as flowers, birds, and butterflies, along with small pebbles and shells—terms known as rocailles in French, which is where the term Rococo comes from. As European fascination with Asia increased, artworks reflecting these distant cultures became popular, leading to the integration of chinoiserie motifs into the Rococo style. The Judaica, or Jewish ritual art, featured in this gallery was utilized in homes or synagogues; sophisticated symbolism and historical inscriptions intentionally highlight its significance. The seriousness of the Baroque is contrasted with the lively, almost choreographed rhythms of the playful rocaille formations.

Dominated by a twelfth-century marble ciborium, or altar canopy, Gallery 304 exhibits works of art in all media from about 1050 to 1300.

In addition to large-scale sculpture and stained-glass windows, the room contains masterpieces in ivory, enamel, and precious metals in the Romanesque and Early Gothic styles. Many were made for use on and around the altar, while others were used in private devotion, and some, like game pieces, reveal secular life in the Middle Ages.

Robert M. Lee Gallery in the Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Ronald S. Lauder Gallery features American swords and firearms from the colonial period through the late nineteenth century, representing outstanding examples of American silver- and goldsmiths' work, products of the Industrial Revolution, and American folk art.

Highlights include silver-hilted swords from the time of the American Revolution; an extensive series of Colt revolvers; firearms lavishly decorated by Tiffany & Company; Kentucky rifles; and engraved Colonial-era powder horns.

This elaborate example of Baroque interior decoration originates from a small manor house in Flims, in eastern Switzerland. The room was called the Reiche Stube ("rich room") and was used for the reception of important guests. It was built for Johann Gaudenz von Capol, an important local figure and a much-traveled diplomat and mercenary commander for the Swiss Federation. Of utmost rarity is the contemporary faience stove by David II Pfau of Winterthur. It is decorated with biblical motifs, and the intricate openwork crowning the "tower" is a characteristic feature of Winterthur faience creations.

The Iris and B. Gerald Cantor Galleries
The Henry R. Kravis Wing

During this period artists working in different materials combined motifs inspired by the Middle Ages, the Renaissance, and the Rococo to create works that reflect the past but are unmistakably nineteenth century in origin. The end of the century witnessed the expanded role of the independent artist, who frequently supplied manufacturers with designs for a wide variety of objects. The Arts and Crafts movement, born in the 1860s in reaction to England's growing industrialization, celebrated the importance of the craftsman by advocating a return to traditional techniques and simpler forms of the past.

The Aesthetic Movement, which shared many of these values, emphasized beauty as the supreme goal of the decorative arts. The majority of its practitioners were strongly influenced by the clean lines and relative simplicity of Japanese art.

The allure of historical styles diminished toward the close of the century, when a new artistic movement favoring flowing, natural forms and stylized ornament emerged across Europe. This style—known as Art Nouveau in French and Jugendstil in German—dominated design well into the 1920s.

properties.popup_header: The Art of the Ramesside Period
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Contacts between Egyptians and non-Egyptians, interactions with divine beings, the rule of kings, and funerary activities are topics explored in this gallery through art dating from about 1300 to 1100 B.C., that is, during the time of the Ramesside kings. There is the spectacular funerary statue of the couple Yuny and Renenutet. Nearby objects display elements that speak to interactions with worlds beyond Egypt’s borders and the massive head of a captive stands out. Across the gallery, dynamic representations of two kings in vastly different materials illustrates the skill of royal workshops. Sketches on bits of limestone reveal the humanness of the ancient Egyptians.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Works in this gallery mostly originate from Amarna, a site in Middle Egypt where Akhenaten, initially crowned Amenhotep IV, established his residence. Akhenaten, who reigned for seventeen years beginning around 1370 B.C., spread the exclusive worship of one aspect of the solar deity, the light of the sun, along with the veneration of himself and his queen, Nefertiti. In the arts, the king's pioneering religious ideas opened up an equally innovative period of naturalism and emotional expression. The gallery also displays art from the post-Amarna period, including several sculptures bearing the features of Tutankhamun.

The works on view in this gallery exemplify the style of figural carving and lavish ornamentation with costly materials—such as rosewood and mahogany veneers, marble, and gilded brass and gesso—that spread from France to the United States around the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries. The doorframes of this gallery were originally part of the interior of the Halsted House, erected in Rye, New York, around 1825. The walls were painted to simulate the faux ashlar masonry in the entry hall of the Alsop House in Middletown, Connecticut (built 1838–39).

The Robert and Renée Belfer Court

This gallery at the center of The Robert and Renée Belfer Court for Early Greek Art provides the introduction to all the galleries of ancient Greek and Roman art and includes a map of the ancient world. In it are displayed prehistoric and early Greek art as well as, from time to time, rotating major artworks on loan of varying periods.

The Lamont Wing

This is an accurate reconstruction of a bedroom from the Roman villa at Boscoreale (ca. 50–40 B.C.) that was buried by the eruption of Mount Vesuvius on August 24, A.D. 79. The walls are decorated with highly ornate and colorful frescoes of the so-called Second Style, comprising urban landscapes with towering architectural vistas on the side walls and rocky outdoor scenes populated by various songbirds on the rear wall.

Known as the villa of P. Fannius Synistor, the building was excavated in 1900.

The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

properties.popup_header: French Decorative Arts: Louis XV Room, "The Morgan Alcove"
properties.popup_body:
The Wrightsman Galleries

Devoted to the decorative arts of seventeenth- and especially eighteenth-century France, The Wrightsman Galleries (522–529, 531–533, and 545–547) display the Museum's holdings of furniture, Savonnerie carpets, gilt bronze, Sèvres porcelain, silver, and gold boxes. Since the 1963 acquisition of the paneling from the Hôtel de Varengeville and the Palais Paar with funds given by Mr. and Mrs. Charles Wrightsman, these rooms have borne the Wrightsmans' name.

One of the most spectacular rooms in the American Wing, endowed in memory of Annie Laurie Aitken, the Richmond Room offers a glimpse of the grandeur of early nineteenth-century domestic life as it was lived by wealthy Americans. Purchased from a house built for the Richmond lawyer William Clayton Williams (1768–1817), the room's most notable features are its rich mahogany woodwork and blue-and-gray King of Prussia–marble baseboards. The modern wallpaper featuring scenes of Paris is a reproduction of the type sold in the United States in the 1810s. The elegant furniture by Charles-Honoré Lannuier and Duncan Phyfe further enhances the sophisticated Anglo-French aesthetic of the room.

properties.popup_header: Greek Art: Fifth–Early Fourth Century B.C.
properties.popup_body:
Stavros and Danaë Costopoulos Gallery

The works of art displayed in the Stavros and Danaë Costopoulos Gallery reflect the artistic accomplishments of Athens at its height, as well as the influence that the Parthenon (built between 447 and 432 B.C.) had on objects in other media.

Prominent also are funerary vases covered with a white slip and decorated in a range of colors not previously used in Greek ceramics.

Robert Lehman Collection

The Lehman Collection's rich holdings of fourteenth- and fifteenth-century Italian panel paintings are displayed in this gallery. The majority of these paintings originate from Florence and Siena, providing the opportunity to compare two distinct schools of painting in these thriving artistic and economic centers in Tuscany.

While Florentine art possesses a sculptural, monumental, and solemn quality (exemplified by Bernardo Daddi's Assumption of the Virgin), Sienese art has a more decorative, elegant, and lyrical character (seen in Bartolo di Fredi's Adoration of the Magi).

Several of the paintings displayed here were once part of large altarpieces with multiple panels and wings, known as polyptychs. Two panels in this gallery originate from the same altarpiece: the Virgin and Child and Saint Ansanus by Simone Martini, a preeminent Sienese artist. A third panel from this altarpiece is on view alongside other fourteenth-century Italian paintings in Gallery 602.

Faience, or tin-glazed and enameled earthenware, first emerged in France during the sixteenth century and is foremost distinguished by the milky opaque white color achieved by the addition of tin oxide to the glaze. Although characterized as more provincial in style than porcelain, French faience was used at the court of Louis XIV as part of elaborate meals and displays. The term faience derives from the northern Italian city of Faenza, once a major center of maiolica production that influenced the early production of French faience. Regional variations in vessel shapes and decorative motifs gradually emerged in cities such as Nevers, Rouen, Lyon, Moustiers, and Marseille.

properties.popup_header: South Italian Art: Fourth–First Century B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The Lamont Wing

Greek emigrants founded colonies in Southern Italy and Sicily as early as the mid-eighth century B.C. They flourished in their own right and profoundly influenced the artistic production of the indigenous populations. This interplay is particularly evident in the South Italian vases of the fourth century B.C., produced especially for funerary use. Furthermore, it appears in the predilection for subjects derived from Greek drama. Owing to a dearth of fine marble, terracotta was also a primary medium for sculpture.

properties.popup_header: The Burials of Foreign Wives of Thutmose III
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

This rich equipment belonged to the three foreign wives of King Thutmose III, who reigned for fifty-four years beginning around 1480 B.C. The women had non-Egyptian Semitic names, suggesting they may have been daughters of rulers from neighboring countries married to the Egyptian pharaoh to strengthen political ties. Their burials, however, were outfitted almost entirely according to Egyptian customs and beliefs so that only their names indicate their foreign origin. The northern case contains golden funerary jewelry, while the southern case holds jewelry worn while participating in cultic activities during their lifetimes.

Fifteenth-century Italian artists saw themselves as working in a period of cultural rebirth, a "renaissance" of the art and philosophy of antiquity. Inspired by ancient Greek and Roman statuary, sculptors focused their attention on the human form, either unveiling it or using drapery to assert its physical presence. Subjects from Roman history and mythology offered new opportunities to experiment with the nude, while Christian devotional images were made more compelling through natural-looking gestures and facial expressions. Emotional expressiveness was a particular preoccupation of that most innovative of Florentine sculptors, Donatello (ca. 1386–1466), whose impact can be seen in several of the works shown here.

Many of the sculptures on view were carved or modeled. In carving, the form is revealed by removing material—for instance, stone or wood—using sharp tools. In modeling, wet clay is built up (and sometimes scooped out) into the desired shape and then fired in a kiln. The resulting material is called terracotta (literally, "cooked earth"). Color and gilding could be added to make a work more lifelike or beautiful. Also featured in this gallery are domestic furnishings and religious accessories, revealing the breadth of techniques in which Italian artists excelled—from working with gold and wood to silk weaving and embroidery.

properties.popup_header: Monumental and Temple Art from around 1200-600 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

In this gallery, the visitor once again has an opportunity to experience the monumental in Egyptian art. The granite jamb of a massive gateway probably comes from the funerary temple of Ramesses II, who reigned for an astonishing sixty-six years beginning around 1290 B.C. The limestone relief belongs, at about 575 B.C., to another gateway that was part of the palace of King Apries at Memphis. Stone coffins, statues, and royal heads are highlighted here as well, including an especially elegant gilded bronze statue of Ptah, the patron god of Memphis.

In addition to interpreting and caring for the collection, the staff of the Modern and Contemporary Art also organizes temporary exhibitions throughout the year. While many of these are small thematic presentations composed of objects from the Museum's collection, the department also organizes loan exhibitions drawn from collections throughout the world.

The Esther Annenberg Simon Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

A military term referring to the front line of an infantry unit, the "avant-garde" in the visual arts describes a spirit of self-conscious artistic rebellion against tradition that took many forms in the early twentieth century. It is most commonly associated with specific styles, like Cubism, and artistic movements, like Italian Futurism, that rejected conventional ideals of beauty as superficial.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Tomb reliefs belonging to the treasurer Khety and the vizier Dagi were made during a period of cultural revival around 2000 B.C. Nearby the private papers of Heqanakht, a landowner whose family lived around 1950 B.C. may be viewed. These letters document that this ancient Egyptian landowner bullied and badgered his family and staff to take care of his affairs while he was away. In 1921, Met archaeologist Herbert E. Winlock excavated these four-thousand-year-old papyri that give us a glimpse into social and economic concerns of an ancient Egyptian family. [Translations of the letters are available on The Met’s website.]

The intimate sculptures shown here were meant to be held and examined closely, delighting the senses of sight and touch. They are a product of the learned and innovative atmosphere that prevailed in Italy during the cultural reawakening known as the Renaissance. Sculptors drew inspiration from ancient Greek and Roman art, as well as from the natural world, concentrating on the idealization of its highest expression—the human body.

Italian Renaissance artists refined the technique of bronze casting using the lost-wax method, which involves making a mold from a wax sculpture, then filling its cavity with molten metal. Once cooled, the surface of the sculpture was worked with tools until the desired finish was achieved. Gilding and lacquers could then be applied to the surface to create patinas ranging from luminous reddish gold to rich brown and opaque black. Gradually, bronze founders learned how to create several casts from the same model. Bronze had been prized as the noblest material during antiquity, and, accordingly, these sculptures were highly valued by discerning Renaissance collectors who displayed them on tabletops and mantelpieces.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Delicate faience inlays that once decorated a shrine, deity figurines in faience and bronze, and vessels of silver and bronze, all dating to the Ptolemaic era from 332 B.C. to 30 B.C., are among the objects displayed in this gallery. The group of limestone plaques with animal and human figures in relief represent significant donations to temple cults. There are also wonderful depictions of various royal figures, including those of famous queens: Berenike II, Arsinoe II, and Cleopatra VI, as well as a number of Ptolemaic kings. The wall case provides the visitor with additional examples of Ptolemaic art.

Muriel Kallis Steinberg Newman Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

A military term referring to the front line of an infantry unit, the "avant-garde" in the visual arts describes a spirit of self-conscious artistic rebellion against tradition that took many forms in the early twentieth century. It is most commonly associated with specific styles, like Cubism, and artistic movements, like Italian Futurism, that rejected conventional ideals of beauty as superficial.

Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The Jacques and Natasha Gelman Galleries (Gallery 904 through Gallery 907) feature paintings and sculptures from the Gelman collection, which the Museum received in 1998. The collection's emphasis is on the School of Paris, comprised of French and often non-French artists who lived and worked in France before and after World War I.

On view are works by Pierre Bonnard, Georges Braque, Alberto Giacometti, Fernand Léger, Henri Matisse, Joan Miró, Pablo Picasso, Yves Tanguy, and others. This permanent installation offers a tour through the European Modernist movement from the turn of the twentieth century to the 1970s.

Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor

The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


This gallery features a changing selection of Japanese sword and dagger blades, mountings, and fittings from the fourteenth to the nineteenth centuries shows masterpieces in steel, lacquer, and gold that represent some of the highest achievements of the arts of the Samurai.

Arthur Ochs Sulzberger Gallery in the Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing


The Arthur Ochs Sulzberger Gallery in the Ronald S. Lauder Galleries houses exhibitions and rotating installations of objects from the Museum's permanent collection of arms and armor.

properties.popup_header: Arms and Armor of Islamic Cultures
properties.popup_body:
Ronald S. Lauder Galleries of Arms and Armor
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

Rare and beautifully decorated armor and weapons in this gallery represent a wide spectrum of Islamic cultures from India, the Middle East, Turkey, and the Caucasus from the fourteenth to the nineteenth century.

The Wrightsman Galleries

The objects in this gallery, dating from the reign of Louis XIV (1638–1715), are made of such precious materials as tropical woods, tortoiseshell, ivory, and gilt bronze. Known as the Sun King, Louis was one of the longest reigning and most powerful monarchs in France. With the help of Jean-Baptiste Colbert (1619–1683), the superintendent of finance, the arts of the day revolved around the king's personal taste and celebrated the magnificence and formality of his court. Royal symbols frequently served as decoration; rarely has a style been more closely associated with the personality of a ruler. Louis XIV focused his attention on transforming his father's hunting lodge at Versailles into a royal residence that became the setting for his court in 1682. The king himself is depicted as Jupiter in the embroidered hanging to the left of the bed. This lavish set of hangings was commissioned by the king's mistress, Madame de Montespan (1641–1707), about 1684–85.

Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

This gallery is devoted to the Museum's presentation of Thomas Hart Benton's America Today mural.

Learn more about the mural and its acquisition in this MetCollects video and the Winter 2015 Bulletin.

properties.popup_header: Before Yesterday We Could Fly: An Afrofuturist Period Room
properties.popup_body:
Seneca Village—a vibrant nineteenth-century community of predominantly Black landowners and tenants—flourished in an area just west of The Met, in what is now Central Park. By the 1850s, the village comprised some fifty homes, three churches, multiple cemeteries, a school, and many gardens. It represented both an escape from the crowded and dangerous confines of lower Manhattan and a site of opportunity, ownership, freedom, and prosperity. In 1857, to make way for the park, the city used eminent domain to seize Seneca Village land, displacing its residents and leaving only the barest traces of the community behind.

This project has roots in the homes of Seneca Village, of which only a fragmented history remains. Like other period rooms throughout the Museum, this installation is a fabrication of a domestic space that assembles furnishings to create an illusion of authenticity. Unlike these other spaces, this room rejects the notion of one historical period and embraces the African and African diasporic belief that the past, present, and future are interconnected and that informed speculation may uncover many possibilities. Powered by Afrofuturism—a transdisciplinary creative mode that centers Black imagination, excellence, and self-determination—this construction is only one proposition for what might have been, had Seneca Village been allowed to thrive into the present and beyond.

https://www.metmuseum.org/exhibitions/afrofuturist-period-room

properties.popup_header: Eighteenth-Century French Decorative Arts: The Lauzun Room
properties.popup_body:
The Wrightsman Galleries

Monumental in size and decorated with exquisite carving, textiles, and trim, state beds were among the costliest pieces of furniture to adorn seventeenth- and eighteenth-century interiors. They were not intended for resting or sleeping, but functioned instead as ostentatious settings where aristocrats could receive guests paying official visits on special occasions such as the birth of a child.

Dating to 1782–83, the bed's original Beauvais tapestry hangings are too fragile to be displayed and have been replaced by modern silk, but the inside of the domed canopy retains its original floral tapestry. According to a 1791 inventory, the bed—which belonged to the duchesse de Praslin, the wife of a statesman of Louis XV—was furnished with three mattresses, giving it a boxlike appearance. The piled-up bedding was quite high, making the use of bed steps necessary. The seating furniture placed along the walls was not meant to be used, demonstrating the formal nature of the room, while other pieces were more practical. The bedside table held the chamber pot. The multipurpose table near the barrier is equipped for dressing, breakfasting, and writing, and its upper section could be removed to serve as a bed tray.

Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

A number of unique works may be viewed in this gallery that date around 1400 B.C., and belong to a time when Egypt was at its peak in wealth and political stability, and the arts. Highlights include a battle relief representing Asiatics being trampled under the pharaoh's chariot, faces of King Amenhotep III, an exquisite sphinx in blue faience, the decorated side of Thutmose IV’s throne, and three delicately carved animals in ivory. Another case explores the participation of women in religious life.

properties.popup_header: The Leon Levy and Shelby White Study Collection
properties.popup_body:
Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The Study Collection contains some 3,500 objects, extending from the Neolithic period in the Aegean (sixth millennium B.C.) to the end of antiquity in the Mediterranean world (early seventh century A.D.). The objects are arranged largely in chronological order and also often by material. In addition, there are display cases that represent specific styles, geographical area, or themes.

Leon Levy and Shelby White Court and Galleries
The Lamont Wing

The Etruscan heartland was delimited by the River Arno to the north and the Tiber to the south and east. Its culture developed from an earlier one, called Villanovan, and was enriched by a variety of influences, notably Phoenician, Greek, and Roman. By the early third century B.C., Rome effectively controlled most of the Etruscan territory.

The Etruscans were consummate metalworkers, as indicated by their jewelry and bronzes, most notably the ceremonial chariot from Monteleone. Another medium in which they excelled was carved amber. Their accomplished craftsmanship and cultural complexity is well illustrated in this gallery within the Leon Levy and Shelby White Galleries.

properties.popup_header: The Gioconda and Joseph King Gallery
properties.popup_body:
The Gioconda and Joseph King Gallery in the Leonard A. Lauder Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.
This gallery features a rotating display of special exhibitions organized by the curators of the Department of Greek and Roman Art.

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

properties.popup_header: The Blanche and A.L. Levine Court for Modern and Contemporary Art
properties.popup_body:
Blanche and A.L. Levine Court
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

The Robert A. Ellison Jr. Collection showcases the continuum of artistic ceramics in the United States from 1876 to 1956. The earliest examples date to the Centennial Exhibition in Philadelphia, the catalyst for the emerging American art pottery movement. Ceramists absorbed styles and techniques from France and England, and from the Far East. The tradition of painted ceramics was explored in myriad ways. By the late 1890s, the Arts and Crafts movement took hold with its flat, simplified, and conventionalized nature motifs and executed mainly with matte glazes. The period saw a proliferation in glaze experiments, from the rich red of the Chinese sang de boeuf and innovative matte glazes to luster and iridescent finishes, as well as jewel-like crystalline surfaces. The 1920s through the 1950s reflected the major art movements and ultimately ushered in the era of the studio potter.

The Museum's preeminent collections of American silver, pewter, ceramics, and glass are installed on this level and on the mezzanine balcony below.

Colonial

Displayed on the northern section of the East Balcony of The Charles Engelhard Court are colonial American silver, pewter, glass, and ceramics, including redware, stoneware, and porcelain. As early as the 1660s, immigrant craftsmen began to establish workshops in which they produced wares that closely followed English and Continental fashions. Goods created during the late seventeenth and early eighteenth centuries typically reflected Baroque traditions. By the mid-eighteenth century, again following Europe's lead, colonial American craftsmen rejected Baroque massiveness and symmetry in favor of the lighter, more curvilinear forms of the Rococo. Imported objects as well as immigrant craftsmen played central roles in transmitting the latest European styles to the Colonies. The objects displayed here offer insights into the domestic environments, craft traditions, and customs of colonial Americans. Colonial American silver is also on view in Gallery 710, Gallery 712, and Gallery 750.

Early Years of a New Nation

The southern section of the East Balcony is devoted to objects produced during the period when America was a young nation. On display is a diverse array of wares, some high style and others more utilitarian, in a variety of media including silver, glass, earthenware, and stoneware. At this time, Neoclassicism became the prevailing aesthetic. First popular in Europe, the style quickly took hold in America, where its allusions to the Classical world resonated as appropriate symbols of the young republic. Immigrant and native-born craftsmen pioneered new techniques, such as the invention of the glass-pressing machine. European-inspired decorative vocabularies also took on distinctive regional characteristics in the hands of American craftsmen. These objects helped define taste and style for the new nation. Other objects dating to this period are on view in Gallery 731 and Gallery 733. Additional Neoclassical silver is also on view in Gallery 750.

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

Paintings executed for churches and palaces were often gigantic; they required a tremendous outlay of time and expense. Most painters made drawings that helped them work out the composition and iconography of a painting in advance, but sketches done in oil paint allowed them to resolve additional issues, including the relationship between colors and the distribution of light. Contracts for paintings frequently required the patron’s approval of an oil sketch before the artist began the final canvas or fresco. In large studios such as Peter Paul Rubens’s, oil sketches could also serve as a guide for the painter’s assistants or for the production of a composition in other media altogether, such as tapestry.

Although oil sketches were long seen as merely tools in an artist’s working process, eighteenth-century collectors came to value them in their own right. The look of the oil sketch, with its lively summary brushstrokes, in turn influenced the style of finished works by artists like Giovanni Battista Tiepolo and Jean Honoré Fragonard.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles. Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

properties.popup_header: The Interconnected World of the Ancient Near East
properties.popup_body:
This gallery is arranged thematically, exploring topics such as deities, medicine and magic, money and weights, the biblical world, and the relationship between art and text in the ancient Near East.

The phenomenon of artistic exchange receives special attention, with objects on display that demonstrate links among the great civilizations of the Near East and their neighbors, which were forged through trade and diplomacy during the second and first millennia B.C. Phoenician-style ivories and gold work provide visual evidence for maritime contacts across the Mediterranean. Rare textiles and sculptures from Dura Europos and Palmyra—situated along the legendary Silk Road—and stone and metal sculpture from the incense-trading kingdoms of southwestern Arabia display the arts of major centers of cultural exchange during later periods.

properties.popup_header: The Assyrian Empire and Its Sphere of Influence
properties.popup_body:
This gallery contains objects created during the early first millennium B.C., during the height of the Assyrian empire.

At its greatest extent, the empire expanded from its core region in northern Mesopotamia to include an area from Babylonia to the Mediterranean Sea and parts of Egypt, Iran, and Anatolia. Assyrian palatial and luxury arts, represented here by selected pieces excavated at the capital of Nimrud, influenced the form and style of works of art from cities outside the Assyrian heartland, including Hasanlu in Iran and Arslan Tash and Tell Halaf in Syria. Meanwhile, distinctive local styles and techniques, such as the use of orthostats (large stones set upright) in northern Syria, made an impact on the arts and architecture of Assyria.

properties.popup_header: Flip Sides: Seeing Korean Art Anew
properties.popup_body:
Most objects are displayed to show their “best” angle meant to impress, sometimes hiding intriguing details. Flip Sides: Seeing Korean Art Anew invites close looking and offers multiple views of the inside, reverse, or hard-to-see aspects of objects.



Bringing together approximately 50 objects from The Met collection, with more than half displayed for the first time, this exhibition, with a rotation, shows treasured Korean traditions. By presenting them in ways that give us a fuller picture into each object’s unique form, Flip Sides provides insight into the construction and function of works in metal, wood, ceramic, textiles, and lacquer. A Buddhist sculpture that held offerings inside, a porcelain jar of striking openwork that conceals an inner chamber, a bronze mirror with a delicately incised image, and a king’s lacquer letter box with linings featuring impressive calligraphy are among the featured objects in Flip Sides.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: March 16–October 18, 2026
Rotation 2: October 31, 2026–May 31, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 31, 2027

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

properties.popup_header: Arab Lands and Iran under the Umayyads and Abbasids, 661-1258
properties.popup_body:
Qatar Gallery
The Lamont Wing

This gallery explores early Islamic art, focusing primarily on the periods linked to the Umayyad dynasty (661–750), whose capital was Damascus, and the early Abbasid dynasty (750–ca. 900), which was based in Baghdad and Samarra. Objects on display trace pre-Islamic styles of Late Antiquity, from Byzantium to Sasanian Iran, and their evolution under the Umayyads. During the Early Abbasid period that followed, accomplishments in the arts, sciences, and literature represent a ‘golden age’ of creativity.

Among the many treasures on view are an outstanding selection of manuscripts and early Qur'an pages in Kufic script. These are displayed alongside early Islamic textiles- including examples from the Met’s inscribed tiraz collection - from Yemen to Egypt and ceramics, including luster-painted pottery; wooden doors in the beveled style from Samarra in Iraq; metalwork; and glass.
This corridor displays architectural elements and fragments, including a window from the Marmion house and a painted panel (ca. 1760–1790) from the Hoagland Farm in Griggstown, New Jersey.

Robert Wood Johnson, Jr. Gallery

The Robert Wood Johnson Jr. Gallery features installations of photographs from the permanent collection that span the history of the medium and present a variety of subjects, processes, dates, and countries of origin, often including notable recent acquisitions. Installations change every four months.

The princes and princesses of Renaissance Italy—powerful rulers of city states including Milan, Urbino, Ferrara, and Mantua—competed with one another by commissioning grand, sophisticated works of art. Carefully tracking each other’s artistic activities, they were eager not to be outdone—or worse, to “displease by imitation,” in the words of Beatrice d’Este, duchess of Milan.

The Italian ideal of rulership emphasized “magnificence”: a moral code that required the wealthy to spend generously on public and private artworks that would bring glory to a city. To accommodate their ambitious demands, rulers hired artists to live at court and work for them, offering a stipend (at least in theory), clothing allowances, and, in some cases, noble titles.

Many of the artists featured here worked at the court of Ferrara; under the rule of the Este family it became internationally renowned for its exceptional music, literature, art, and architecture. Painters shaped the visual identity of the court, which favored elaborate, obscure subjects and embraced originality and eccentricity.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millennium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Major strengths include pre–Angkorian Khmer and related stone and bronze sculpture, Javanese gold jewelry and bronze sculpture, and glazed ceramics from Thailand and Vietnam.

Note: The arts of the Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

Christen Sveaas Gallery in the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries
Michael C. Rockefeller Wing

The Christen Sveaas Gallery, unique in the United States, displays landscapes by Caspar David Friedrich and his Romantic followers in Dresden, including Carl Gustav Carus and August Heinrich. Another colleague of Friedrich's, the Norwegian Johan Christian Dahl, is represented by major paintings depicting views in Denmark, Germany, Italy, and his native country. These works showcase Dahl's role as a figure whose artistic sources and influences were international in scope. Small-scale sketches and large pictures reflect these early-19th-century artists' experience of the Sublime in nature—whether the haunting beauty of a moonlit night, the awesome power of an alpine waterfall, or the fearsome display of an active volcano.

Intimately-scaled scenes of daily life represent the so-called Golden Age of Danish painting. The charming Biedermeier and German Realist styles are represented in works by Eduard Gaertner and Adolph Menzel, who both worked in Berlin.

The gallery features major paintings given by Christen Sveaas to The Met in 2019, in celebration of its 150th anniversary. It is one of ten rooms that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries

The Powel Room in The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries was originally located on the second story of a house that still stands today at 244 South Third Street in Philadelphia. It was constructed in 1765–66 by Charles Stedman (1713–1784), a Scottish-born shipmaster and merchant. The house was sold to Samuel Powel (1738–1793) in 1769, at the time of his marriage to Elizabeth Willing (1743–1830). Powel was the last mayor of Philadelphia under British rule and the first after the American Revolution. The couple hosted many members of the Continental Congress, including Thomas Jefferson, John Adams, and George Washington. The room is furnished with superb examples of Philadelphia Rococo–style furniture of the type that the Powels might have owned.

properties.popup_header: The Calculated Curve: Eighteenth-Century American Furniture
properties.popup_body:
The George M. and Linda H. Kaufman Galleries in the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art


Visitors to the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries will encounter a dramatic presentation of masterworks in The Met collection of North American furniture produced between 1720 and 1775. During this period of great aesthetic achievement, talented makers rejected the heft and rectilinearity of earlier approaches and embraced more liberating designs conceived almost entirely of curves and abstract motifs inspired by nature. This display encourages greater consideration of the works’ sculptural expression and elastic forms, the interplay of light, and the artists’ abilities to push materials to their limits.

Furniture has always been intertwined with everyday life, but it is also an essential art form. Underlining its utility are thoughtful mathematical and architectural strategies, structural engineering, and aesthetic innovations akin to other mediums. The shapely forms and textured ornament, as well as the incorporation of ergonomics to accommodate the human body, reveal the fundamental artistry of furniture design.

Some of the brilliant furniture makers featured in this installation are identified, while others’ names were not recorded. Despite their crucial contributions, talented people of color, immigrants, and teenagers were least likely to receive credit for their work. Women were also critical to the production and reception of early North American furniture, providing immeasurable operational support to workshops and access to capital and patrons through kinship networks. Additional furniture holdings—and their fascinating stories—can be explored in the historical interiors, or period rooms, throughout the American Wing.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles. Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

properties.popup_header: Gothic by Design: The Dawn of Architectural Draftsmanship
properties.popup_body:
Long before the towers of Gothic cathedrals could pierce the sky, architects outlined their vision and ambitions by creating intricate designs. The Gothic era, known for soaring structures like Notre-Dame in Paris, produced some of the Western world’s most breathtaking buildings. Their overall height, tall spires, pointed arches, and light-filled spaces were a striking departure from the more rounded and fortress-like features of the preceding Romanesque period. These innovative elements resulted from a new focus on the design process that is documented in original architectural drawings.



Gothic by Design: The Dawn of Architectural Draftsmanship reveals how master masons and other artists began to visualize and communicate their complex ideas for cathedrals and other architecture-inspired structures in drawings and, later, prints. Between the 13th and 16th century, these little-known artworks on parchment and paper became a significant factor in the stylistic evolution of Gothic architecture and art at large.



This rare presentation of more than 90 works of art—drawings and prints alongside goldsmith works, architectural elements, sculpture, and painting—sheds new light on the artistic choices and expansive knowledge that informed the Gothic building practice. Explore the vision, imagination, and skillful artistry embedded in the carefully drawn plans of medieval architects and other artists of the day.
  
Exhibition Open: Through July 19, 2026

properties.popup_header: The Annenberg Collection: Nineteenth- and Twentieth-Century Masters
properties.popup_body:
The Annenberg Galleries

The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818–826) includes more than fifty works by eighteen of the most renowned artists working in France during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, among them Manet, Monet, Renoir, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Picasso.

The Annenberg Collection (Galleries 821-823) was assembled by Walter H. Annenberg (1908–2002) and his wife, Leonore Annenberg (1918–2009), over a period of four decades, beginning in the early 1950s. The Annenberg gift stipulates that the collection must be exhibited together and always remain on the premises of the Metropolitan Museum.

For noble Italian families, the Renaissance home was a richly decorated space brimming with art. Objects celebrated marriages, served as portals to religious devotion, entertained through the telling of history and myth, and pictured ancestors and family members. People interacted with these objects, using some as instruments for meditation and prayer, and others for practical purposes—as with marriage chests (cassoni), wedding gifts that doubled as storage and seating. Increasingly popular were portraits of children, which suggests that parents doted on them much as they do today.

Alongside this ran a taste for the distant and unfamiliar in the form of imports supplied through thriving international trade. Products like ceramics, central to the social activity of dining, were status symbols, especially when they came from faraway places. While this kind of abundant variety was reserved for the wealthy, household inventories show that nearly every home, even the most modest, had an image of the Madonna and Child hanging on its walls.

Guide them around the house and in particular show them some of your possessions, either new or beautiful, but in such a way that it will be received as a sign of your politeness and domesticity, and not arrogance: something that you will do as if showing them your heart.
—Pietro Belmonte, 1587

Belmonte wrote a treatise on conduct for new brides that includes this advice about showing guests objects around the home.

Portraits helped to solidify power across Spain’s global empire. At King Philip IV’s court, once a prototype of a person’s likeness had been approved, replicas were commissioned as diplomatic gifts or signs of political alliance. Velázquez, who moved from Seville to Madrid in 1623, pioneered a comparatively informal approach while adhering to this system for circulating official images. In individual head studies, the immediacy of his method—loose brushwork applied directly to the canvas while studying the live model—is readily apparent. From these intimate canvases, Velázquez built the formal full-length portraits that announced a sitter’s prestige and financial might through grand size, symbols of rank, and sumptuous dress. The Sevillian painter Bartolomé Estebán Murillo saw Velázquez’s portraits on a brief trip to Madrid in 1642 and adopted many of their innovative features.

The most famous painting in this gallery, Portrait of Juan de Pareja, depicts a man of African descent who was enslaved by Velázquez for over two decades. After gaining his freedom in 1654, Pareja established his own successful career as a painter. His story highlights the multiracial character of Spanish society and the role of enslaved labor in its art and visual culture.

properties.popup_header: A Search for Truth in Lombardy
properties.popup_body:
C. Michael Paul Gallery

Milan, the economic and political center of the Lombard region in northern Italy, hosted some of the most famed artists of the Renaissance. Ruled by the dukes of the Sforza family, the city fostered regional artistic talent and became home to Leonardo da Vinci, who left his native Florence to work at the court of Ludovico Sforza around 1482. During his roughly two decades in Milan, he influenced generations of artists.

At the heart of Lombard painting was a search for truth. In the work of Leonardo’s followers, some of whom are featured here, we find evidence of his careful observation of nature, his interest in capturing human psychology, and his technique of sfumato, a way of rendering forms through soft gradations that lend a smoky effect. Works of art from northern Europe also passed through Lombardy via trade networks and inspired an even greater interest in descriptive detail. In their stark contrasts between beauty and ugliness, humility and the divine, the painters active in this region would lay the foundation for the groundbreaking later work of Caravaggio.

Three cities, each with its own distinctive architectural landmarks and civic rituals, dominated artistic production in Flanders during the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries: Bruges, Antwerp, and Brussels. In one of the most urbanized regions of Europe, these centers enjoyed great prosperity and relative autonomy that contributed to their flourishing art markets. Competition spurred innovation, encouraging the development of new modes of painting characterized by visual flamboyance and attentiveness to the details of everyday life.

In the 1400s, Bruges was the busiest port in Europe, with many international residents who patronized local artists. After its port silted up and Bruges lost its direct access to the sea, Antwerp took its place, enjoying a period of economic and artistic growth that drew astonished commentary from visitors. Brussels, meanwhile, was an important center of court culture, home to the region’s Habsburg rulers and a renowned tapestry industry.

Small and portable, the paintings here speak to the many spaces where people used images for prayer. Religious works of this scale could be packed for travel, set up in homes or chapels, kept in the private cells of monks and nuns, or carried in religious processions. Artists designed many of the objects to be held in the hand or placed on a tabletop, inviting a form of worship that engaged the sense of touch as well as the eye. Their size encouraged painters to focus on individuals, forgoing densely populated dramatic stories for single-figure compositions—usually emotionally charged and aimed at stirring compassion.

These works would have been appreciated as interactive instruments of devotion, facilitating regular prayer and contemplation for people outside the walls of the church. When placed throughout the household, they also acted as reminders of the constant presence of the divine and thus the importance of maintaining virtuous behavior.

As for the reason [why we pray], it is because prayer unites you with God, makes you his familiar, he listens to you willingly, he grants you an audience at any hour and any time that you wish, and in any place where you might be.
—Sebastiano Aiello, 1594

Aiello’s writing describes the many practices of prayer that operated beyond church walls, supported by works of art like those on view here.

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing
