Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

In 1648 an academy of painting and sculpture was established in Paris. While its founders acknowledged the enormous significance of Italian art, from ancient times onward, they aimed to forge a new school of distinctly French painting. This institution regulated taste and professional success in the French art world well into the 1800s, often challenging the new directions taken by pioneering artists.

In its early years, the academy prioritized intellectual engagement with ancient history, claiming this set artists apart from mere craftsmen. Its founders favored works that employed the jewel tones, clarity of light, and classical decorum found in paintings by Simon Vouet and Charles Le Brun. As a royally sponsored institution, the academy aligned itself with the priorities of the absolute monarch, Louis XIV, whose palace of Versailles became a nexus for artistic activity.

An alternate thread of expression built on the vision of Caravaggio and his many followers in northern Europe, focusing on subjects that had been largely looked down on by the academy. Artists such as Georges de La Tour captured the poetic beauty of everyday life with a careful orchestration of light and restrained sense of composition.

Artists across seventeenth-century Europe made pilgrimages to Rome, due in part to the widespread cultural significance of ancient Greece and Rome. They found antiquity alive in Rome’s monuments or imagined age-old pastoral scenes set in the surrounding countryside.

In 1624 the painter Nicolas Poussin, who trained in Paris, arrived in Rome to further his education and joined the intellectual circle of the connoisseur and antiquarian Cassiano Dal Pozzo. Rejecting many current trends in Roman painting, Poussin set out to paint a rigorous, idealized vision of the classical world. His compositions often combine architectural stage sets inspired by ancient ruins with carefully choreographed groups of figures based on ancient sculpture. For generations to come, Poussin’s version of classicism became a model either to emulate or to rebel against.

The French painter Claude Lorrain also spent most of his career in Italy, joining an international circle of mainly northern European landscape painters in the 1620s. He soon settled on the subjects that would make him famous: luminous skies, often pictured at dawn or dusk, and sweeping landscapes given intellectual weight with classical figures and architecture.

properties.popup_header: The Reformation and the Age of Dürer
properties.popup_body:
The German-speaking lands of the Holy Roman Empire were at the center of the political, religious, and social upheavals that convulsed Europe in the 1500s. Paradoxically, this time was also a golden age for German art. Some art historians have called this era “the age of Dürer,” due to the contributions of Albrecht Dürer, who became an international artistic celebrity in his own lifetime. As a painter, printmaker, and art theorist, he shaped future generations of followers while serving as a conduit for Italian ideas and techniques that he encountered on his extensive travels.

Close cultural and economic ties linked the German-speaking lands with the Netherlands. Artists of both regions produced intense devotional paintings, many of which revel in the gruesome sufferings of holy figures in an attempt to captivate the viewer. These images became an increasing subject of controversy. Some Protestant reformers eventually embraced the wholesale destruction of religious paintings as a means to “cleanse” the church.

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

This gallery features a display of the three significant periods in the medieval history of the eastern Mediterranean, under the Fatimid (909–1171), Ayyubid (1169–1260), and Mamluk dynasties (1250–1517). On view is an outstanding selection of gold jewelry from Fatimid Egypt, luster and painted ceramics from Ayyubid Syria, textiles, and splendid inlaid metalwork and enameled glass from the Mamluk period. Known for its history, rich culture, and diverse population, Cairo has played a vital role in the artistic life of the Islamic world for centuries. Under Mamluk rule, Cairo became one of the wealthiest cities in the Near East and a hub of artistic and intellectual activity in the Arab world.

This gallery also offers another point of entry into the whole suite of Islamic art galleries, from the adjoining Lane Galleries for Nineteenth-Century European Painting (804), which is overseen by the Department of European Paintings.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles.

Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

The Lore Heinemann Gallery

Much like tourists today, travelers in the past sought souvenirs to commemorate their time abroad. Vedute, an Italian word for topographical “views,” refers to paintings and prints that range from the documentary to the imaginary. They flourished in Rome and especially in Venice, where the painters Canaletto and Francesco Guardi produced series of works that record the city’s sites. Often purchased and hung in dense groups, as in this gallery, they functioned like luxurious snapshots that could transport their viewers and recall shared experiences on the grand tour.

Canaletto and Guardi each developed a signature style. Canaletto employed optical devices and precise perspectival drawings to suggest clarity and objectivity, presenting Venice bathed in bright, even sunlight. His exacting geometries, often made with a straightedge, can be seen tracing individual buildings and the perspective lines of his squares and canals. Guardi preferred a looser paint handling; detail is deliberately sacrificed in favor of atmospheric effects of light and weather. His large decorative paintings take inspiration from the outskirts of Venice’s marshy lagoon but include invented follies, or fanciful architecture. These additions suited a European fashion for asymmetry and ruins that became known as the “picturesque.”

properties.popup_header: The Vélez Blanco Patio: Spanish Decorative Arts, 1450-1700
properties.popup_body:
The objects arranged around this balcony from a great Spanish courtyard attest to the artistic diversity of the kingdoms of Spain. Rich decoration and animated forms were nurtured by contact with Islam, Italy, Northern Europe, and the Americas. By the late fourteenth century, the area around Valencia had become a leading producer of prized lusterware—ceramic vessels that derive their seductive iridescence from a glaze containing copper and silver compounds. The potters' techniques and designs, combining geometry with nature, reflect an Islamic influence that is discernible in many Spanish arts, including textiles and furniture.

Wood was the preferred material for sculpture, primarily made to stimulate religious devotion, and usually painted to enhance its realism and poignancy. Sculpted holy figures were exported throughout the Spanish colonies, including those in the New World, to promote Catholicism. The discovery of the Americas and its precious-metal mines boosted Spanish goldsmiths' work, providing a steady stream of silver for fashioning religious accessories and secular vessels, grandly adorned with masks in the ancient Roman style and other Italian Renaissance ornamental motifs.

Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

The Paris Salon—the official, juried exhibition of the Académie des Beaux-Arts—was a high-profile venue for contemporary art for much of the nineteenth century; a successful (or controversial) Salon submission could make a career. Paintings commensurate with the conservative tastes of the Salon—technically pristine, idealized pictures, or, in later decades, refined variations on Realist and Impressionist styles—were snapped up by a cosmopolitan elite. The promise of popular acclaim and financial success attracted not only French artists but also an international contingent, many of whom trained or worked in Paris, eager to try their fortunes in the capital of the art world.

properties.popup_header: Kingdoms and Empires: The Ancient Near East ca. 2000 B.C.–A.D. 200
properties.popup_body:
The arts of Iran from the second and early first millennia B.C. are represented in this gallery of Iranian Art generously supported by The Hagop Kevorkian Fund by fine ceramics, metalwork, sculpture, and jewelry, including finds from Hasanlu in western Iran. Works of art from Assyria and Syria from about 1400–612 B.C., including luxury metalwork and ivories from the capital city of Nimrud, illustrate the rise of the Assyrian empire.

Two glazed-brick panels of striding lions from the Processional Way at Babylon evoke the grandeur of the Neo-Babylonian city in the sixth century B.C. Sculpture, vessels, weapons, and personal ornaments in a variety of metals and techniques characterize the arts of the first millennium B.C. in the Anatolian kingdoms of Urartu and Phrygia as well as the cultures of Transcaucasia and the Black Sea region.

The André Meyer Galleries
Michael C. Rockefeller Wing

This gallery features works by painters known collectively as the Barbizon School.

Beginning in the 1820s, many of France's leading landscape painters made regular excursions to the celebrated Forest of Fontainebleau, southeast of Paris. With the tiny village of Barbizon as their base, they set about depicting the forest's richly varied terrain. Théodore Rousseau's paintings of rugged woodlands and lush meadows powerfully convey the magnificent unruliness of nature—a Romantic ideal given expression earlier in French art by Théodore Gericault. Other artists, such as Jean-François Millet, found inspiration in the rustic life of peasants. His paintings of humble farm laborers find an urban counterpart in Honoré Daumier's unsentimental portrayals of the Parisian working class. The work of these artists represents a major step in the establishment of pictorial naturalism in France.

This gallery is one of two that comprise The André Meyer Galleries (see also Gallery 801).

The Annenberg Galleries

Anchored by major masterpieces by Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Seurat, this gallery highlights the parallel achievements of artists commonly referred to as Post-Impressionists, who came of age during the last two decades of the nineteenth century.

This gallery is one of nine that comprise The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818-826).

properties.popup_header: Mughal South Asia and Later South Asia
properties.popup_body:
Galleries 463 and 464 unify the rich holdings of the Islamic and Asian departments in grand adjoining spaces, thus presenting a historically cohesive overview of the many facets of the later art of the region (15th to 20th centuries). The two galleries highlight the artistic and cultural diversity of the Indian subcontinent and its broader connections with the Islamic world, Europe, and beyond.

The first major space displays works of art from the Sultanate, Mughal, and Deccan courts in a chronological and regional sweep from about 1450 to the nineteenth century. Masterpieces include celebrated folios from the Emperor's Album, jades and jewels of the Mughal period, and fine examples of Deccan court arts. The second gallery, which offers an independent entrance into the larger suite of galleries, presents vibrant examples of Jain, Rajput, Pahari, and "Company" school painting from the sixteenth to the nineteenth century, as well as textiles and decorative arts, showcasing the artistic variety of the Indian courts.

Generous support for these galleries is provided by the South Asian-American community.

The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

properties.popup_header: The Rise of Civilization: The Ancient Near East ca. 8000–3000 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The objects in this gallery illustrate the rise of civilization in the Near East.

Works of art from the Neolithic period dating to the eighth through sixth millennium B.C. were produced at a time that permanent settlements were established and the first ceramic vessels were made. This is followed by the Chalcolithic period ("Copper-Stone Age," ca. 5500–4000 B.C.), characterized by the first experimentations in metallurgy and the production of highly decorated and skillfully made pottery. Fundamental changes followed during the Uruk period in Greater Mesopotamia (ca. 4000–3100 B.C.), which saw the rise of the first cities and states, significant royal and ritual architecture, the institution of kingship and administrative practices that ensued, with the invention of cuneiform writing and the use of cylinder and stamp seals.

properties.popup_header: Chinese Courtyard in the Style of the Ming Dynasty
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

Inspired by Brooke Russell Astor, who spent part of her childhood in China, The Astor Court (Gallery 217) and this adjoining reception room featuring Ming dynasty hardwood furniture opened to the public in 1981, a gift of the Vincent Astor Foundation.

Modeled on a seventeenth-century courtyard in the Garden of the Master of the Fishing Nets in Suzhou, the court was entirely constructed using traditional tools and techniques. An eighteenth-century imperial kiln was reopened to fire the ceramic tiles; rare nan wood was hand-planed into columns; specimen Taihu rocks were used for the rockeries, and a granite terrace was hand chiseled from a Suzhou quarry.

A team of twenty-six Chinese craftsmen (along with a chef) spent six months in New York assembling the components.

Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

At the turn of the twentieth century, a surge of creative energy swept across Europe. In previous decades, all eyes had turned to Paris. Now artists in many different cities made a bid for international prominence, advancing innovative styles and ideas. Among others, the Vienna Secession and the Wiener Werkstätte in Austria established forums for cutting-edge art and design; the Nabis in France developed a boldly simplified, decorative approach to both modern and traditional subjects; and the Symbolists, particularly in Northern Europe, sought to evoke the intangible realms of the mind and spirit.

Despite the diversity of interests, common ground emerged: a newly invigorated classicism; an inclination toward lyricism (expressed through references to music and dance); a move away from naturalistic representation in favor of experimentations with color, pattern, and form; and a desire to convey higher meanings, evident in a fondness for allegories and legends.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

The artists and sitters featured in this gallery led lives fractured by political executions and exile as they rode out multiple regime changes. The statesman Talleyrand, who appears in three portraits at The Met, served successively under Louis XVI, the revolutionary government, Napoleon and, finally, the restored kings Louis XVIII and Louis Philippe. Portraits could be highly charged public statements of political alliances in such volatile times. The grand full-length format of many examples here—traditionally associated with the aristocracy and royalty—was adjusted to accommodate newly powerful individuals in society, including scientists, generals, and influential women.

France’s prosperity in the years leading to the revolution in 1789 derived significantly from its colonial territories. These included parts of India and the Caribbean, where enslaved labor ensured the profits of the tobacco, coffee, and sugar industries. Though not readily visible on the surface of every portrait, the lives of the artists and sitters here intersected with this international network through birth, politics, and economics.

properties.popup_header: Medieval Iran and Central Asia (9th–13th centuries)
properties.popup_body: This room focuses on the medieval eastern Islamic world, which includes the artistic achievements of the eleventh-century Ghaznavid and twelfth-century Seljuq Sultans, and an affluent middle class that ushered in a brilliant and inventive period of art and culture in Iran and Central Asia. Among the highlights are luster-painted and other luxurious ceramic vessels from twelfth-century Iran, a pair of life-size statues of courtly figures, and a twelfth-century monumental inlaid bronze incense burner in the shape of a stylized feline with hooves.

The Annenberg Galleries

Unlike his fellow Impressionists Monet, Pissarro, and Sisley, who concentrated on landscape, Auguste Renoir was primarily a figure painter. This gallery displays several of Renoir's most celebrated portraits and genre paintings, most notably, Madame Charpentier and Her Children, which was purchased by the Museum in 1907.

Also featured in this room are works by Renoir's contemporaries: Berthe Morisot, Alfred Sisley, and Henri Fantin-Latour.

This gallery is one of nine that comprise The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818-826).

Constance and Walter Burke Gallery

Anchored by two masterpieces—Pieter Bruegel the Elder’s Harvesters (1565) and Nicolas Poussin’s Blind Orion Searching for the Rising Sun (1658)—this installation showcases works from the Department of European Paintings in a new light by examining how artists helped shape cultural conceptions of the natural world, and how human and nonhuman histories are intertwined. Dating from around 1525 to 1775, paintings included in this gallery trace the broad shift in European depictions of nature from expressions of embeddedness toward assertions of dominance. Individual artists’ conceptions nevertheless remained varied and complex. Summer abundance is celebrated, for example, in many of these paintings, yet they were created during a period known as the “Little Ice Age.”

The installation additionally explores how, as physical objects, paintings alter natural resources, from flax woven into linen canvases to mined minerals ground into pigments and bound with linseed or walnut oils. Reacting to changes in temperature, humidity, and light over centuries, these materials continue to have lives of their own.

B. Gerald Cantor Sculpture Gallery

The B. Gerald Cantor Sculpture Gallery features The Met’s historic collection of bronzes, marbles, terracottas, and plasters by Auguste Rodin (1840–1917). The Museum acquired many of the works displayed here directly from the great French sculptor. Others are gifts from renowned collectors such as Iris and B. Gerald Cantor, after whom the gallery is named. All reveal Rodin’s intense exploration of the human figure as he strove, in his words, to “imitate not only form, but life.”

The paintings in this gallery provide a glimpse into the creative forces that shattered conventions and invigorated individual expression across Europe during Rodin’s lifetime. Front and center are monumental scenes that reconceive traditional subjects from history, religion, and myth in modern terms. Artists were equally inspired by the beauty and vitality of the body, the dynamism of nature, and the world of the imagination. Today, as then, the emotional eloquence and inventive modeling of Rodin’s sculptures speak to an astonishing range of pictorial subjects and styles.

properties.popup_header: Chinese Painting and Calligraphy: Selections from the Collection
properties.popup_body:
The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display.



The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 10, 2026

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

properties.popup_header: Fin de Siècle Art and Design: Wisteria Room, ca. 1914
properties.popup_body:
This dining room, the only complete French Art Nouveau interior in an American museum, comes from the Paris apartment of Auguste Rateau, an engineer and art connoisseur. It was created by Lucien Lévy-Dhurmer, a ceramist who, about 1895, turned his hand to painting and decorating.

Wisteria symbolizes welcome, and Lévy-Dhurmer incorporated the motif throughout the room: pointillist-style murals depict birds in wisteria-laden landscapes; walnut-veneered paneling is inlaid with purplish amaranth representing clusters of blossoms; further clusters are carved on the furniture and stamped on the leather upholstery; the bronze-and-alabaster standing lamps evoke the trunks of wisteria vines. The outer gallery features a selection of works by Redon and other artists.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millenium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Major strengths include pre–Angkorian Khmer and related stone and bronze sculpture, Javanese gold jewelry and bronze sculpture, and glazed ceramics from Thailand and Vietnam.

Note: The arts of the non–Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

Candace K. and Frederick W. Beinecke Gallery



On the heels of the American Revolution, begun in 1775, and the French Revolution, begun in 1789, a so-called Age of Revolution unfolded around the globe, from Haiti to Greece, India to Peru. Though revolutionary forces often sought freedom from colonial powers, in France the rise of Napoleon as self-appointed emperor instead initiated rapid territorial expansion. This would completely redefine the boundaries of Europe, including claims to present-day Egypt. In art and architecture, references to ancient Greek and Roman history became touchstones for legitimizing newly erected governments.

Artworks in this gallery attest to the rapid social change of this era. Women artists experienced greater opportunities; Paris was more multicultural than ever before; and “the public” became a viable entity, unified around political causes. Change was not unilaterally progressive, however. Slavery, abolished during the French Revolution, was reinstated by Napoleon in 1802. Many liberties afforded women by the revolutionary government had been retracted by 1804.

Domenikos Theotokopoulos, known as El Greco (The Greek), stands alone in European painting. His idiosyncratic vision left few followers in subsequent generations, before suddenly inspiring key figures of modernism at the turn of the twentieth century, including Pablo Picasso, Paul Cézanne, and Max Beckmann.

Born on the island of Crete, then part of the Venetian empire, El Greco trained in the production of tradition-bound Byzantine icons before he moved to Italy in the late 1560s. There, he integrated into his work the contorted, elongated human bodies of Mannerist painting and the chalky, high-key palette and dynamic compositions of Venetian artists like Titian and Paolo Veronese. In 1577 El Greco moved to Toledo, Spain, where he established a workshop that created small-scale devotional works, portraits, and vast altarpieces that melded these diverse sources.

Here is an old master who is not merely modern, but actually appears a good many steps ahead of us, turning back to show us the way
—Roger Fry, 1920

Deemed incomprehensible, even aberrant, for three centuries after his death, El Greco’s paintings spoke to many artists and critics—including Roger Fry—in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

Music is a central part of every culture across time and place. Local musical traditions and instruments are shaped by available materials, specific technologies, and interactions with other cultures. Historically, music and instruments, language, and cuisine have accompanied people on paths forged by global trade, war, enslavement, and migration. The adaptation of musical concepts and new materials along with belief systems and political structures reflects the creative impetus of converging cultures, traditions, and history. The Mapping the Art of Music gallery explores the impact of geography on musical culture. The displays focus on the development of regional styles, methods of transmission, adaptation and assimilation of musical instruments, and the dynamics between musical change and the continuity of cultural identity.

European portraits and portraitists often traveled long distances in the 1500s, linking together far-flung relatives, prospective spouses, or political allies. A person’s painted likeness might communicate information about their physical appearance, social status, faith, and intellectual interests. Extensive inscriptions, accessories like books, pets, or artworks, and the complex language of fashion and jewelry all contributed to the individualization of sitters. Portraitists also sometimes worked on a massive scale or with a pictorial ambition unknown to their predecessors.

Bringing together works painted across Western Europe, this gallery reveals the cultural exchanges and itinerant careers that spurred portraiture’s development over the course of the sixteenth century. Artists like Hans Holbein, who traveled widely, developed a distinct combination of realistic physical features, stark backgrounds, and learned inscriptions that contributed to the rise of a pan-European style. Other artists pursued more eccentric approaches, pushing portraiture to its limits as they incorporated aspects of narrative or religious painting.

“Baroque” originated as a pejorative word, describing something that was overly complex and flawed in its logic—or, at its best, beautiful in the way of an irregularly shaped pearl. Bucking the staid stability of Renaissance compositions, a generation of Italian artists working around 1600 explored new approaches to narrative subjects that harnessed theatrical gestures and dramatic light effects. Instead of idealizing their figures according to classical norms, they painted the quirks of their models’ faces and physiques.

Emerging out of the expressive figure style of Mannerist painting, members of the Carracci family pioneered many of the trends that became known as Baroque. The painter Caravaggio, however, has come to embody this movement in his stormy personality, his daring use of lighting, and his unflinching depiction of unidealized bodies. In the words of the French painter Poussin—who remained adamantly classical in the wake of these developments—Caravaggio “came to destroy painting.”

The Dutch Republic experienced major political changes beginning around 1650 that profoundly affected painting. A relatively peaceful period following the Treaty of Münster (1648), ending eighty years of war with Spain, brought wealth to Dutch merchants. This new prosperity fueled an emulation of aristocratic manners and pastimes. Amorous pursuits and the civilized leisure of the well-to-do became the subject of so-called high-life genre paintings that broke with the coarse and rowdy humor of earlier scenes of everyday life. Gerard ter Borch and Pieter de Hooch pioneered this new form of domestic tableau, while Johannes Vermeer is known today as its greatest interpreter.

The private lives of women, usually those in upper levels of society, also became a key subject for mostly male artists, who might adopt a voyeuristic vantage point. Showing anonymous women in moments of dressing, music making, or contemplation, these paintings record the dominant social mores of the period through idealized images of femininity. Other works depicting flirtation or mischief-making may have been intended to titillate and moralize simultaneously.

Drue Heinz Gallery

The eighteenth century witnessed major transformations in British art, spurred by the nation’s emergence as a global economic and military power. Artists and critics began to champion a distinctly British school, and the Royal Academy of Arts, founded in 1768, conferred new prestige on painting with its annual exhibitions.

While theorists asserted the superiority of history painting, portraits dominated the market. The range of people depicted in portraiture expanded beyond the aristocracy, extending to not only the newly rich but also actresses and athletes. Other popular images catered to an urban audience’s fascination with the rural poor, sentimentalizing the inequities behind the period’s cultural flowering.

In scenes of everyday life and contemporary events, British artists responded to the transatlantic slave trade and colonial warfare, often idealizing the brutality occurring beyond Britain’s shores. At the same time, many painters from the newly independent United States relocated to London, eager to make a name for themselves in one of the world’s great artistic centers.

Koç Family Galleries 

The Koç Family Galleries (459 and 460)—Carpets, Textiles and the Greater Ottoman World and Arts of the Ottoman Court (14th–20th centuries)—present the art of the Ottoman world in a series of grand spaces, showing the rich diversity of courtly, provincial, and village art. The galleries provide a comprehensive overview of the multilayered nature of Ottoman patronage. Among the strengths of gallery 460 are works from the imperial workshops of Istanbul, Bursa or elsewhere under the reign of Sultan Süleyman, including Iznik ceramics, drawings, and calligraphy, as well as courtly objects and fine textiles.

The Arthur M. Sackler Gallery
Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Arthur M. Sackler Gallery houses part of the Museum's holdings of Chinese Buddhist sculpture, the largest collection of its kind in the West. (Additional examples are featured in Gallery 208.) Elegant bronze icons, monumental stone sculptures, and colorful sculptures made of wood, clay, and lacquer illustrate China's complicated adaptation of the Indian religion of Buddhism. Early Chinese Buddhist sculpture often shows ties to Indian and Central Asian traditions; after the twelfth century, however, when Buddhism disappeared in India, Chinese sculptures often illustrate exchanges with artistic traditions from Nepal and Tibet.

Highlights of the collection include several monumental stone sculptures, a nearly lifesize gilt-bronze sculpture of Buddha Maitreya dated 486, an example of a seventh-century piece made using the dry lacquer technique, and rare and important wood sculptures with inscriptions dating to 1282 and 1385.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles.

Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

The Havemeyer Galleries

Four galleries (Gallery 814–817) feature the Museum's collection of works by Degas, one of the world's largest and most comprehensive.

The richness of these holdings is due in large part to Mrs. Louisine Havemeyer, one of the principal collectors of modern European art in America at the turn of the twentieth century. She began acquiring Degas's work in the late 1870s at the urging of her friend, Mary Cassatt, the famous American Impressionist, and came to prefer his work above all other living artists.

By the time of her death, Mrs. Havemeyer had amassed a collection of more than sixty paintings, drawings, and pastels by Degas, as well as all seventy bronzes and a large number of prints. The bulk of her collection—the most complete group of Degas's works ever assembled—entered the Metropolitan upon Mrs. Havemeyer's death in 1929. This gallery also includes pastel portraits by Manet and oil sketches by Lautrec.

The R.H. Macy Gallery

Around 1500, religious painting in Italy underwent a major shift. The multipanel, or polyptych, format was gradually abandoned in favor of one large canvas or wood panel. This change invited a new relationship between viewers and the divine. While multipart altarpieces like those in this gallery by Raphael and Perugino could tell many stories, single-scene works prioritized the experience of a worshipper boldly coming face-to-face with the subject of devotion. Freed from compartmentalizing frames, artists transformed sacred figures into grand entities who gazed directly at viewers, conveyed palpable emotions, and at times appeared to move beyond the picture.

Public altarpieces performed several functions, including serving as the focus of Mass, educating the faithful, and honoring the patron who commissioned them. For artists, they provided prominent settings to display their work and advance their careers. As such, altarpieces were increasingly seen as vehicles for experimentation and arenas for competition. The search for novelty led to great variety, from intimate single-figure compositions to dramatic multifigure narratives that bring scripture to life.

Donald and Vera Blinken Galleries
Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

properties.popup_header: View Finding: Selections from The Walther Collection
properties.popup_body:
The world looks different through a camera’s viewfinder—like a picture frame or a windowpane, it redirects the eye. In modern and contemporary photographs from an esteemed private collection, artists use the camera to navigate shifting terrain. Registering and reshaping environments in flux, they look anew at how we traverse them.

When Artur Walther began to acquire photography, he aimed to expand the parameters of the field. Assembled over three decades and across five continents, his vast collection brings together a diverse group of makers—some celebrated and others still unknown. View Finding: Selections from The Walther Collection introduces his landmark gift of over 6,500 works and highlights the collection’s pioneering point of view. Presenting international perspectives on hyperlocal subjects, it showcases the camera as a tool for creativity and critique. With inventive eyes, the photographers in View Finding study the sidewalks of Nairobi and storefronts of Fifth Avenue. They search public parks from Tokyo to Tangier. In private bedrooms, parking lots, and other places easy to overlook, they focus in, finding—here and there—unlikely sites of self-reflection and social change.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 3, 2026

The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries

This impressive, seven-sided room in The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries was the principal parlor of Marmion, a plantation house built around 1756 by the Fitzhugh family of Virginia. The room's elaborate painting was probably executed about twenty years later in the 1770s. It is the most ambitious decorative painted room to survive from eighteenth-century America. The Marmion Room has been left unfurnished so that visitors may study all the hand-painted surfaces. The painting on the room's entablature, Ionic pilasters, and dado paneling imitates Siena marble to match the fireplace, while Rococo-style ornaments embellish the paneled walls. Picturesque landscapes adorn a wall and the spaces above the fireplace and the door entrance.

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

Before the fifteenth century, independent portraits were rare in Europe. Those that did appear—usually in sculpture, coinage, or manuscript illumination—most often pictured an idealized royal or holy figure. The proliferation of painted portraits of individuals around the year 1450 aligned with a new emphasis on personal religious belief and self-commemoration, as well as the increased spending power of merchants and bankers—all characteristics of the period historians now term the Renaissance. Artists often designed painted portraits to be portable. These works traveled across Europe as part of diplomatic and personal exchanges, accelerating contact between different artistic traditions.

Both painters and sculptors looked to portrait types from ancient Greece and Rome to depict their sitters as influential or learned. Others took advantage of the developing medium of oil paint to achieve new effects of realism. Details of wrinkles, scars, or stubble add powerful presence to painted faces, while carefully rendered textiles and jewels record status and wealth for posterity.

This gallery contains two groups: the enormous paintings by Tiepolo that were once part of immersive interiors and a rotating selection of pastels. The production of framed oil paintings that could be easily picked up and moved was just one aspect of European painters’ careers. Many artists were also engaged in the design of theater sets, festival architecture, furniture, tapestries, and gardens, as well as commissions to decorate huge expanses of ceilings and walls in churches, palaces, and public buildings. The examples on view are by Tiepolo.

Pastels contain many of the same pigments as oil paint, bound together with gum and powder into ready-to-use sticks. Portable and not requiring a drying process, pastels allowed artists to work quickly, applying color directly to paper or vellum. The medium’s distinctive visual effects—powdery surfaces, intense colors, and lines that reveal the artist’s touch—became the calling cards for celebrity pastelists who traveled between European cities at the peak of pastel’s popularity in the eighteenth century.

properties.popup_header: The Hagop Kevorkian Fund Special Exhibitions Gallery
properties.popup_body:
The Hagop Kevorkian Fund Special Exhibitions Gallery

The Hagop Kevorkian Fund Special Exhibitions Gallery is known for its focused presentations drawn primarily from the Museum's holdings and occasionally supplemented by important loans. The gallery provides the opportunity to present the depth of the Museum's Islamic collection while exploring innovative, stimulating, and unexplored aspects of the field.

In the 1750s, art critics began accusing French painters such as François Boucher and Charles Joseph Natoire of letting the grand allegorical painting of King Louis XIV’s reign (1643–1715) disintegrate before their eyes. The art that later became known as Rococo was deemed “feminine” and “decorative,” words that continue to color the way these paintings are understood.

In fact, these artists were responding directly to changes in lifestyle by wealthy Parisians. Art patrons had left the court at Versailles for newly built urban townhouses where intimate rooms and informal entertainment reigned. Many paintings were intended to be installed into carved paneling and integrated into complex multimedia displays that required the combined skills of craftspeople who specialized in porcelain, mirrors, textiles, woodwork, gilding, and metalwork.

On the outskirts of Paris, maisons de plaisance, small retreats built specifically for entertaining, also prompted jewel-box displays that blended paintings and decorative art, indoor and outdoor. The canvases by Hubert Robert displayed here were commissioned for Bagatelle (a French word meaning “a little trifle”), the suburban retreat of King Louis XV’s brother.

Muslim North Africans occupied large parts of southern Spain for over seven hundred years, battling for territory with Catholic rulers, who took definitive control in 1492. For Spanish art, this resulted in profound expressions of Catholicism built atop one of Europe’s most multicultural societies.

Through marriages and political alliances, the so-called Catholic Monarchs of Spain controlled not only large portions of the Americas, but also Flanders and southern Italy. Spanish painting, therefore, took inspiration from a wide range of sources that innovatively combined regional styles, with Peter Paul Rubens and Caravaggio standing out as touchstones. Prints and foreign artwork were readily accessible in the new capital of Madrid, where Spanish kings amassed vast collections, and in Seville, the economic center of the global empire and a key artistic nexus.

properties.popup_header: Flesh and the Spirit in the Age of Rubens
properties.popup_body:
For seventeenth-century art theorists, history painting—depicting scenes from the Bible and ancient poetry or myth—represented the highest test of artistic ability. Classical literature offered painters not only examples of stoicism and sacrifice, but also pretexts for sensual and even erotic images.

In composing history paintings, many European artists were able to study the physical remains of ancient civilizations during lengthy sojourns in Rome. The Flemish painter Peter Paul Rubens, for example, immersed himself in classical history and archaeology during the eight years he spent in Italy; he went on to become famous for his ability to endow ancient prototypes with the vigor and sensuality of living bodies.

Rubens was one of many artists who were motivated by the Catholic Counter-Reformation and sought to create religious paintings that made a direct appeal to the beholder’s emotions. These artists looked both to the realism of Caravaggio and to the austerity of late medieval art in their attempt to renew centuries-old compositions.

properties.popup_header: Gothic by Design: The Dawn of Architectural Draftsmanship
properties.popup_body:
Long before the towers of Gothic cathedrals could pierce the sky, architects outlined their vision and ambitions by creating intricate designs. The Gothic era, known for soaring structures like Notre-Dame in Paris, produced some of the Western world’s most breathtaking buildings. Their overall height, tall spires, pointed arches, and light-filled spaces were a striking departure from the more rounded and fortress-like features of the preceding Romanesque period. These innovative elements resulted from a new focus on the design process that is documented in original architectural drawings.



Gothic by Design: The Dawn of Architectural Draftsmanship reveals how master masons and other artists began to visualize and communicate their complex ideas for cathedrals and other architecture-inspired structures in drawings and, later, prints. Between the 13th and 16th century, these little-known artworks on parchment and paper became a significant factor in the stylistic evolution of Gothic architecture and art at large.



This rare presentation of more than 90 works of art—drawings and prints alongside goldsmith works, architectural elements, sculpture, and painting—sheds new light on the artistic choices and expansive knowledge that informed the Gothic building practice. Explore the vision, imagination, and skillful artistry embedded in the carefully drawn plans of medieval architects and other artists of the day.
  
Exhibition Open: Through July 19, 2026

properties.popup_header: Chinese Painting and Calligraphy: Selections from the Collection
properties.popup_body:
The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display.



The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 10, 2026

Can fashion photography be dangerous? Lillian Bassman was told as much when, in 1950, she started making photographs so abstract that you could barely see the clothes. Depicting midcentury style for the pages of magazines, she distilled gowns and girdles to their essential silhouettes; in her photographs, chance gestures and elegant lines convey the sensations of garments, as their details dissolve into atmospheric blur. What Bassman did not show she evoked in her expressive prints—products of darkroom distortion, achieved with tissues, brushes, and bleach.

In an earlier era, such an approach might have precluded commercial success. Bassman’s timing, though, could not have been better; in 1941, at age 24, she took a design job at Harper’s Bazaar, where a group of artists and editors were then reimagining how the magazine should look. With them, she helped introduce an avant-garde sensibility to the American newsstand, advancing new possibilities for photography in print.

In works from a remarkable gift to The Met, Lillian Bassman: Bazaar and Beyond highlights the influence and audacity of her magazine career. The exhibition flips between the New School in Manhattan and the “New Look” in Paris, charting Bassman’s course from design apprentice to art director and accomplished photographer. Its rare vintage prints, collages, and maquettes lay out an unlikely history of modernism, refashioned for the pages of the popular press.
  
Exhibition Open: Through July 26, 2026

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

properties.popup_header: Iran and Central Asia in the Mongol Age (13th-16th Centuries)
properties.popup_body: Gallery 455 displays material from the thirteenth to the early sixteenth century under the Mongol, Turkmen, Timurid, and Uzbek dynasties that came to flourish in such royal capitals as Tabriz, Samarkand, and Herat. Among the highlights are manuscript pages from fifteenth-century Herat, such as painted folios from the famous Conference of the Birds or Mantiq-al-Tair, and other examples of the arts of the book. A chronological overview of the art of the Persianate world, while underscoring its many connections with other cultures, continues in Gallery 462.

The Harry Payne Bingham Gallery

The influential trio of Titian, Jacopo Tintoretto, and Paolo Veronese painted both religious and secular images with heightened sensitivity and theatrical intensity. Inspired by poetic traditions, Venetian artists in the 1500s became perceptive interpreters of human emotion, exploring devotion, desire, joy, and despair. They introduced a deep intimacy to painting, championing what was then an uncommon subject—the sensuous female nude—and transforming familiar biblical narratives into introspective scenes set at night.

With dazzling color, soft contours, and light effects, painters in Venice positioned themselves in opposition to the rival tradition in Florence that focused on draftsmanship and the science of anatomy. Innovative to the point of rebelliousness, they harnessed the expressive potential of oil painting, applying paint thickly and employing a sketchy, almost vibrating style of brushwork. Their legacy would span centuries, touching the work of artists such as Peter Paul Rubens, Velázquez, Eugène Delacroix, and Edouard Manet.

The figures should stir the spectators’ souls—disturbing them in some cases, cheering them in others, in others again inciting them either to compassion or disdain, depending on the character of the subject matter. Failing this, the painter should not claim to have accomplished anything.
—Ludovico Dolce, 1557

A Venetian humanist, prolific author, and art theorist, Dolce traveled in the circles of artists such as Titian. He engaged with the artistic controversies of the day in his famous treatise on painting.

Flowers, shells, silverware, and the occasional skull provide the motley components for one of the most innovative kinds of painting in early modern Europe: still life. Although their subject matter might seem simple, these artworks conveyed a broad spectrum of cultural associations. Still lifes linked art and science in a period of increased observation of the natural world. At the same time, paintings of flowers, fruits, and other objects continued to have symbolic associations and may have served as the focus for devotional practices. A number of women artists became famous still-life painters at a time when social conventions highly restricted their artistic training and particularly their access to the nude model.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

The Met is home to one of the world’s most diverse and important collections of musical instruments. Our 1830 Thomas Appleton organ is one of the oldest functioning pipe organs made in the United States.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

The Met is home to one of the world’s most diverse and important collections of musical instruments. Through the theme of The Art of Music, the galleries explore the artistry of instrument making and music across 4,000 years of history and around the globe in the context of the Met’s encyclopedic collections.

Jan and Warren Adelson Gallery in the The Joan Whitney Payson Galleries

In the early twentieth century, a group of urban realists—William Glackens, George Luks, Everett Shinn, and John Sloan—explored aspects of modern life in their art. Working initially as newspaper illustrators in Philadelphia, they took classes with the realist painter Thomas Anshutz at the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts. They also studied with the charismatic artist-educator Robert Henri, who encouraged them to embrace contemporary city scenes. Later dubbed the Ashcan group, they relocated to New York by 1904 and went on to participate in several landmark exhibitions of progressive art in the city. These shows laid the groundwork for modernism in American art and offered many Americans their first in-depth introduction to international modern art. This gallery features work by these key figures as well as their peers and students, including women artists, who shared an interest in working-class urban themes and portraiture.

The flamboyance and formality of seventeenth-century court culture informed not only royal portraits, but also those of financiers, bureaucrats, and other people seeking power. As Europe’s global reach extended, these pictures made political statements, announced alliances, and bestowed a veneer of respectability on figures whose wealth often came from the exploitation of other individuals—including enslaved people—who rarely appear in portraits. Artists, too, articulated their newly elevated place in the world by representing themselves splendidly attired and equipped with attributes of learning and social status.

Aspirational imagery coexisted with likenesses in which formal protocol was relaxed to express more intimate identities. The selection of works in this gallery reflects the cosmopolitanism of both artists and their sitters, who often led itinerant lives as they moved among various centers of princely power.

properties.popup_header: Chinese Painting and Calligraphy: Selections from the Collection
properties.popup_body:
The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display.



The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 10, 2026

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

For more than three hundred years, privileged young European and American men were expected to make what became known as a “grand tour,” a multiyear journey that capped a particular cultural education. Its cornerstone was the art and architecture of Rome. Depending on interest, time, and budget, the itinerary might also include Paris, Dresden, Venice, and Naples—or even push the boundaries of Europe into the Ottoman Empire, which included present-day Greece.

An extensive visual culture of souvenirs sprang up to record this tradition, from cork models of ancient ruins to painted portraits and city views. Pompeo Batoni, for example, received commissions for full-length portraits with choice antiquities as props, and Giovanni Paolo Panini’s market-savvy compositions feature postcard-like collections of ancient and modern sites.

This shared experience and material legacy instilled and perpetuated cultural norms, including the centrality of ancient Greece and Rome to European culture. A home displaying works of art like those on view here attested to the owner’s participation in this rite of passage.

This gallery for Assyrian Art has been arranged to evoke the main audience hall of the ninth-century B.C. Assyrian palace of Ashurnasirpal II at Nimrud in northern Mesopotamia.

The reliefs that line the walls come from various rooms in the palace, and were excavated during the mid-nineteenth century in one of the earliest archaeological expeditions to the Near East. Guarded by a colossal winged, human-headed lion and bull, wearing the horns of divinity, the reliefs depict the king performing a ritual, surrounded by attendants and supernatural creatures facing stylized trees. The famous Standard Inscription of Ashurnasirpal, which records his achievements, runs across each relief panel. It is written in cuneiform, the script used for the Akkadian language then in wide usage throughout the Near East.

properties.popup_header: The Annenberg Collection: Nineteenth- and Twentieth-Century Masters
properties.popup_body:
The Annenberg Galleries

The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818–826) includes more than fifty works by eighteen of the most renowned artists working in France during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, among them Manet, Monet, Renoir, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Picasso.

The Annenberg Collection (Galleries 821-823) was assembled by Walter H. Annenberg (1908–2002) and his wife, Leonore Annenberg (1918–2009), over a period of four decades, beginning in the early 1950s. The Annenberg gift stipulates that the collection must be exhibited together and always remain on the premises of the Metropolitan Museum.

The Damascus Room is a reception chamber from an upper-class home in Damascus, Syria and an outstanding early eighteenth-century example of domestic Ottoman architecture. A high point of the 2011 installation is the repositioning of the room within its proper regional and chronological context, off the gallery dedicated to the Ottoman arts (Gallery 460), underscoring the dynamic interactions between the arts at the court in Istanbul and those in the provinces. An extensive scholarly examination and conservation effort have resulted in a more accurate reinstallation of the room, closer to its original layout.

This luxurious chamber was designed to host visitors and important guests. The opulent décor and poetic inscriptions imbue a feeling of heaven on earth, a place where all the senses would engage and a welcome reprieve from the hectic city. The Damascus Room also reflects the rich cultural heritage of its time and its city, a bustling metropolis with a rich ancient past that stood at the crossroads of trade and pilgrimage routes and was a vibrant provincial center of the Ottoman empire.

The hand-held fan was an unexpected muse for some of the most innovative artists in 19th-century Europe. Fans became hugely popular across many levels of society during this period, serving as functional and fashionable objects of adornment and communication. Well-known French Impressionists such as Edgar Degas and Camille Pissarro not only featured this feminine accessory in their work but also adopted it as an experimental format for their art.

Fanmania investigates why avant-garde artists incorporated fans into their work and sheds light on themes of gender, courtship, consumerism, and appropriation. Artists were attracted to the semicircular form for myriad reasons, including fascination with fans from Asia and Spain, commercial ambition, and their interest in formal and technical innovation. Displaying more than 75 artworks from across The Met collection, this multimedia exhibition features painted and printed fans from Europe and Asia as well as artworks that depict women wielding fans to explore the phenomenon of “fanmania.”
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 12, 2026

Adele and Stanley Herzman Collection

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries

This pre-Revolutionary room in The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries features the only remaining suite of New York City eighteenth-century furniture. It belonged to Samuel Verplanck (1739–1820), a member of an influential New York City family, and his Dutch-born wife, Judith Crommelin Verplanck (d. 1803). The room itself, including the fireplace wall, paneled dado, and cornice, comes from a house built in 1767 in the Hudson River valley by Cadwallader and Elizabeth Ellison Colden, descendants of another storied New York family. The Verplancks lived in an elegant Georgian-style brick town house at 3 Wall Street whose interior was probably more ornate than the relatively simple architectural setting seen here. No eighteenth-century New York City parlors have survived, however, and the Colden House setting provides a fine example of mid-eighteenth-century American woodwork.

properties.popup_body:
The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 10, 2026

Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

The Met is fortunate to have a large collection of works by Gustave Courbet, one of the most provocative and controversial painters of the nineteenth century. This gallery features several key landscapes and seascapes by Courbet, as well as related works by his contemporary, Charles-François Daubigny. One of Courbet's earliest masterpieces, The Young Ladies of the Village, appears along with Rosa Bonheur's equally ambitious The Horse Fair of roughly the same date.

Gallery 809 and Gallery 811 are also dedicated to Courbet's works.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

Evelyn Borchard Metzger Gallery

Over the course of their country’s struggle for independence from Spain, many seventeenth-century Dutch painters embraced the broad vistas and dramatic skies of their homeland. In their hands, ordinary fields and harbors became meditations on the relationship between people and their environment and assertions of a newfound national identity. Other artists traveled abroad or used their imaginations to conjure more unfamiliar landscapes that appealed to a nation of traders and colonizers. As merchants adopted the trappings of the aristocracy, they decorated their estates with images that expressed an ideal of land ownership and rural abundance. In a highly specialized art market, painters carved out reputations for distinctive subject matter, such as seaside villages or nocturnal scenes.

The Annenberg Galleries

The Museum's collection includes twenty-four paintings by Paul Cézanne, ranging from his early Manet-inspired portraits and figure groups to his evanescent late landscapes and famous genre scene The Card Players.

The Metropolitan was the first American museum to purchase a work by the artist—from the historic Armory Show—in 1913.

This gallery is one of nine that comprise The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818-826).

properties.popup_header: Nishapur and the Sabz Pushan Room (9th–12th centuries)
properties.popup_body:
This gallery presents materials excavated at Nishapur, in Iran, by The Met from 1935 to 1947 and came to the museum through an agreement with the Iranian authorities at the time. Amongst the most outstanding objects are elements of the architectural decoration from an excavated mound known locally as Tepe Sabz Pushan ("The Green-covered Mound"). These include fragments of figural wall paintings, elements from a painted stalactite squinch (muqarnas), and a complete set of carved stucco decorative panels of a small room, now called the Sabz Pushan Room.

The reinstallation of this gallery is made possible through the generosity of the Iranian-American community.

properties.popup_header: Chinese Courtyard in the Style of the Ming Dynasty
properties.popup_body:
The Astor Court

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

Inspired by Brooke Russell Astor, who spent part of her childhood in China, The Astor Court and its adjoining reception room (Gallery 218) featuring Ming dynasty hardwood furniture opened to the public in 1981, a gift of the Vincent Astor Foundation.

Modeled on a seventeenth-century courtyard in the Garden of the Master of the Fishing Nets in Suzhou, the court was entirely constructed using traditional tools and techniques. An eighteenth-century imperial kiln was reopened to fire the ceramic tiles; rare nan wood was hand-planed into columns; specimen Taihu rocks were used for the rockeries, and a granite terrace was hand chiseled from a Suzhou quarry.

A team of twenty-six Chinese craftsmen (along with a chef) spent six months in New York assembling the components.

This gallery, hung with paintings from the peak of Courbet's career in the 1850s and 1860s, highlights the artist's versatility and invention as a portraitist. From his portrayal of the dashing tenor Louis Gueymard to his depiction of the demure Madame Auguste Cuoq, Courbet captured the personalities of his sitters with inimitable directness.

Courbet's work in other genres can be seen in Gallery 811 and Gallery 812.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles.

Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

The distinctive visual cultures that emerged in the Americas from the trauma of the Spanish conquest are deeply indebted to both Indigenous and European artistic traditions. They are also informed by the advent of trade across the Pacific Ocean in the late 1500s and the resulting presence of Asian art. Throughout Spanish America, painters and sculptors not only reworked imported visual models but also invented entirely new ones as they created images needed for worship, instruction, and self-presentation. Similarly, artisans fashioned new types of vessels and ornaments from locally abundant precious materials to embellish altars and adorn sacred images.

By the early seventeenth century, in response to local tastes and social practices, distinct artistic forms, styles, and genres had taken shape in far-flung locations such as Mexico City, Quito, Cuzco, and Potosí. Attracted by the visual and material qualities of local artistic traditions, European and American elites sought out aromatic earthenware vessels from Tonalá (in western Mexico); dazzling lacquerware from Pasto (in the northern Andes); richly polychromed sculpture from Guatemala; and luminous paintings on copper and inlaid mother of pearl from Mexico City.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millenium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Major strengths include pre–Angkorian Khmer and related stone and bronze sculpture, Javanese gold jewelry and bronze sculpture, and glazed ceramics from Thailand and Vietnam.

Note: The arts of the non–Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

A revolution in the subject matter of European art occurred around the year 1560. While scenes from the Bible and ancient mythology continued to enjoy the highest critical prestige, many painters in this period responded to controversies about devotional images and a growing art market by specializing in new kinds of subject matter—most notably, scenes of everyday life, often referred to as genre painting.

Artists began to depict the experiences of ordinary people in taverns or marketplaces, and to explore the morality of activities such as drinking, smoking, or conspicuous consumption. These subjects might spur viewers not only to laugh but also to reflect on their own behavior. Printmakers translated these images into engravings and reproduced them for a wide audience, creating a rich dialogue between artists north and south of the Alps.

We hardly see a beautiful hall or fine apartment of any cost that is not decorated with pictures of beggars, bordellos, taverns, tobacco smokers, musicians, dirty children on their pots, and other things more filthy and worse.
—Gerard de Lairesse, 1707

Largely forgotten today, De Lairesse was celebrated in his lifetime as a painter and advocate for an idealizing manner based on the study of classical antiquity—hence his objection to the new down-to-earth subject matter that appealed to collectors.

Charlotte C. Weber Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The largest in the West, the Museum's collection of Chinese Buddhist sculpture is featured in Gallery 206 and Gallery 208. Elegant bronze icons, monumental stone sculptures, and colorful sculptures made of wood, clay, and lacquer illustrate China's complicated adaptation of the Indian religion of Buddhism. Early Chinese Buddhist sculpture often shows ties to Indian and Central Asian traditions; after the twelfth century, however, when Buddhism disappeared in India, Chinese sculptures often illustrate exchanges with artistic traditions from Nepal and Tibet.

Highlights of the collection include several monumental stone sculptures, a nearly lifesize gilt-bronze sculpture of Buddha Maitreya dated 486, an example of a seventh-century piece made using the dry lacquer technique, and rare and important wood sculptures with inscriptions dating to 1282 and 1385.
