properties.popup_header: Prehistoric Art from Cyprus: Seventh Millennium–ca. 1000 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The Norbert Schimmel Gallery
The Lamont Wing

The Norbert Schimmel Gallery houses a collection of ancient art from Cyprus that was acquired by the Museum in 1874, having been assembled on the island by General Luigi Palma di Cesnola, who was American consul there and later became the Met's first director. Because of the sites that he explored, he did not find the very earliest levels of material culture, which are now dated back to 10,000 B.C.

This gallery focuses on objects of the Bronze Age (ca. 2500–1050 B.C.). Though not surely from Cyprus, the bronze ingot represents the island's single most significant resource.

This gallery is dominated by paintings made in eighteenth-century Venice that represent ancient Roman conquests. The action takes place both far beyond the bounds of present-day Europe—in Tunisia and Numidia, both in northern Africa—and in regions that today seem well within its borders, such as northern Italy. The paintings are juxtaposed with other works from The Met collection that offer an expanded narrative of legacies of the ancient Mediterranean world.
Tiepolo’s paintings visualize the expansion of empire, attesting to ever-shifting relations between periphery and center. Rather than take their overwhelming scale and theatrical display of might at face value, we can read them against the grain and in the context of their time: they were created at a moment of identity crisis for Venice, when a grand vision of the past compensated for the city’s political decline and the ghost of longstanding military threats from the Ottoman Empire. These paintings set the stage for stories of artists grappling with ancient legacies and often violent colonial entanglements that play out in nearby galleries and continue to resonate today.

Landscape emerged as an independent subject of European paintings in the sixteenth century. One starting point for this gradual process was the so-called world landscapes of Joachim Patinir, where the foreground figures playing out religious narratives are almost overwhelmed by their expansive natural backdrops. Over the years, specialists in painting human figures began seeking out skilled landscape artists as collaborators.

At a time when most of the population of Europe lived in rural areas and labored in agriculture, landscape painting offered artists a way to explore the cycle of the seasons and appeal to the landowning aristocracy through harmonious depictions of harvesting and hunting.

Few, if any, of these paintings embrace strict topographical accuracy. Some artists of the period used landscape to create hellish visions of the afterlife. Others imagined fanciful or idealized settings for ancient stories, reflecting a renewed interest in Greek and Roman literature and establishing a tradition that would flourish in later centuries.

Goya’s career bridges an age confident in the powers of reason, during the 1770s, with one that suspected the world might be an irrational nightmare, by 1800. Long viewed by historians as a solitary genius, the artist is increasingly understood to have emerged from a complex artistic milieu in Spain and as the head of a significant workshop.

After studying in Rome, in 1777 Goya returned to Madrid, where he designed tapestries that earned him the royal court’s recognition. He became the king’s official painter in 1789, but skirted convention by exploring an incisive and often unflattering form of portraiture. Goya responded to the French invasion and occupation of Spain (1808–14) with artworks that reflect on the horrors of war—themes anticipated by his earlier provocative print series that had critiqued superstition, ignorance, and inherited wealth. Goya’s propensity for critique weaves subtly through the works here; his innovations in paint and politics link these galleries to those featuring nineteenth-century paintings.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

Music is central to nearly all aspects of human endeavor and culture. Through the theme of the art of music, these galleries explore the artistry of music and instruments across 4,000 years of history and around the globe in the diverse context of the Met’s encyclopedic collections. The Art of Music through Time gallery is organized chronologically to illustrate that people worldwide have simultaneously created extraordinary music and instruments for millennia. Such a global perspective enables us to identify underlying commonalities in the creation and function of the instruments encountered here. The use of music and instruments to express status, identity, and spirituality, and the impact of trade, changing tastes, availability of materials, and emerging technologies are shared elements that span the sweep of time and geography. Although primarily intended to be heard, instruments also function as powerful vehicles of visual expression and are often prized as works of art in their own right. As such, their appearance frequently reflects contemporary style, and the production techniques and materials used to make them are shared with other art forms. Here, related objects and paintings are displayed to illustrate the universal presence of music and instruments in art and society.

Through its strategic position along the Adriatic Sea, Venice built far-reaching trade networks. This provided artists in the 1400s with the finest pigments from the East, knowledge of oil painting techniques from the Netherlands, and exposure to wide-ranging styles from the Byzantine empire to the Islamic world. Led by two artistic dynasties, the Vivarini and the Bellini, a signature Venetian style was born that celebrated these advantages.

They became among the first Italians to experiment with the new visual effects enabled by oil paint. Foregrounding the variety and intensity of the colors at their disposal, they laid the foundation for the Venetian school’s enduring reputation for appealing to the senses. Venetian painters also responded to pivotal artistic shifts taking place in Florence, such as the single-point perspective system. Perhaps owing to the strange beauty of the local landscape, many artists also exercised profound care in depicting the natural world in their religious scenes.

properties.popup_header: Archaic Art from Cyprus: ca. 1000–ca. 480 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The greatest strength of the Museum's Cypriot collection lies in stone sculpture of the sixth through third century B.C., examples of which are on view in this gallery. Made of the soft local limestone, the works preserve considerable polychromy.

The most significant object in terms of scale, quality, and importance is the sarcophagus from the site of Amathus. Also well represented here are metal vases, particularly of silver. The works on display reflect the successive influence of Assyrian, Egyptian, Persian, and Greek domination.

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The most distinctive feature of the Astor forecourt is the round "moon gate" that forms the entrance to The Court and its adjoining reception room displaying Ming dynasty hardwood furniture. Located at the heart of the Asian Art galleries, the forecourt is a crossroads that links the galleries for the arts of China, Japan, and South and Southeast Asia.

properties.popup_header: Gothic by Design: The Dawn of Architectural Draftsmanship
properties.popup_body:
Long before the towers of Gothic cathedrals could pierce the sky, architects outlined their vision and ambitions by creating intricate designs. The Gothic era, known for soaring structures like Notre-Dame in Paris, produced some of the Western world’s most breathtaking buildings. Their overall height, tall spires, pointed arches, and light-filled spaces were a striking departure from the more rounded and fortress-like features of the preceding Romanesque period. These innovative elements resulted from a new focus on the design process that is documented in original architectural drawings.



Gothic by Design: The Dawn of Architectural Draftsmanship reveals how master masons and other artists began to visualize and communicate their complex ideas for cathedrals and other architecture-inspired structures in drawings and, later, prints. Between the 13th and 16th century, these little-known artworks on parchment and paper became a significant factor in the stylistic evolution of Gothic architecture and art at large.



This rare presentation of more than 90 works of art—drawings and prints alongside goldsmith works, architectural elements, sculpture, and painting—sheds new light on the artistic choices and expansive knowledge that informed the Gothic building practice. Explore the vision, imagination, and skillful artistry embedded in the carefully drawn plans of medieval architects and other artists of the day.
  
Exhibition Open: Through July 19, 2026

properties.popup_header: The Infinite Artistry of Japanese Ceramics
properties.popup_body:
From the earliest hand-built figurines rooted in spiritual beliefs to the vibrant works that define today’s contemporary ceramic art scene, Japanese pottery reflects exceptional creativity and a refined sense of beauty. Its distinctiveness arises from the ingenuity and mastery of the potters, as well as the wide range of wares that have long supported daily life. This exhibition explores Japan’s extensive and rich history of ceramic art through approximately 350 extraordinary works presented in themes that offer fresh perspectives on the diverse forms and functions, from everyday tableware to vessels created for tea masters and elite households to modern sculptural compositions.

Japan’s earliest pottery dates back more than 12,000 years, when makers shaped and fired clay vessels for cooking and storage—objects that bear the traces of these early potters’ hands. Expertise transmitted from China and Korea in medieval times facilitated the development of new Japanese techniques and wares. Over time, the reverence for prized ceramics led to finding beauty even through damage. Japanese artisans developed kintsugi, the art of repairing broken ceramics with gold lacquer, restoring the damaged object’s function while making it even more precious and valuable. Japanese cuisine and dining aesthetics inspired vessels designed to harmonize with the color and texture of food. Many porcelains from the Edo period (1615–1868) are embellished with motifs drawn from the natural world or symbols of happiness, longevity, and good fortune.

Featuring refined ceramics and other artworks from the Museum’s Harry G. C. Packard Collection, this exhibition celebrates the 50th anniversary of this landmark acquisition that established the foundation of The Met’s Japanese art holdings. By placing ceramics in dialogue with related art forms—including lacquers, textiles, paintings, and woodblock prints—the display inspires curiosity about the multiverse of Japanese ceramics and its broader cultural contexts.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: January 19–May 12, 2026
Rotation 2: May 14–August 16, 2026
Rotation 3: August 29–December 8, 2026
Rotation 4: December 10, 2026–March 28, 2027
Rotation 5: April 10–August 8, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through August 8, 2027

properties.popup_header: The Rise of Plein-Air Oil Sketching and Landscape Painting about 1800
properties.popup_body:
Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

This gallery features small-scale works, including many painted on paper rather than canvas, that attest to the proliferation of plein-air painting among French artists working at home and in Italy about 1800. The practice of oil sketching out of doors tended to be prefatory to painting more finished compositions in the studio and eventually led to the successive innovations of the Romantics, Realists, and Impressionists.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

properties.popup_header: The Infinite Artistry of Japanese Ceramics
properties.popup_body:
From the earliest hand-built figurines rooted in spiritual beliefs to the vibrant works that define today’s contemporary ceramic art scene, Japanese pottery reflects exceptional creativity and a refined sense of beauty. Its distinctiveness arises from the ingenuity and mastery of the potters, as well as the wide range of wares that have long supported daily life. This exhibition explores Japan’s extensive and rich history of ceramic art through approximately 350 extraordinary works presented in themes that offer fresh perspectives on the diverse forms and functions, from everyday tableware to vessels created for tea masters and elite households to modern sculptural compositions.

Japan’s earliest pottery dates back more than 12,000 years, when makers shaped and fired clay vessels for cooking and storage—objects that bear the traces of these early potters’ hands. Expertise transmitted from China and Korea in medieval times facilitated the development of new Japanese techniques and wares. Over time, the reverence for prized ceramics led to finding beauty even through damage. Japanese artisans developed kintsugi, the art of repairing broken ceramics with gold lacquer, restoring the damaged object’s function while making it even more precious and valuable. Japanese cuisine and dining aesthetics inspired vessels designed to harmonize with the color and texture of food. Many porcelains from the Edo period (1615–1868) are embellished with motifs drawn from the natural world or symbols of happiness, longevity, and good fortune.

Featuring refined ceramics and other artworks from the Museum’s Harry G. C. Packard Collection, this exhibition celebrates the 50th anniversary of this landmark acquisition that established the foundation of The Met’s Japanese art holdings. By placing ceramics in dialogue with related art forms—including lacquers, textiles, paintings, and woodblock prints—the display inspires curiosity about the multiverse of Japanese ceramics and its broader cultural contexts.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: January 19–May 12, 2026
Rotation 2: May 14–August 16, 2026
Rotation 3: August 29–December 8, 2026
Rotation 4: December 10, 2026–March 28, 2027
Rotation 5: April 10–August 8, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through August 8, 2027

properties.popup_body:
Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

This gallery has no description.

properties.popup_header: Painters, Critics, and Rivals in the Age of Rembrandt
properties.popup_body:
No figure has dominated the story of seventeenth-century Dutch painting like Rembrandt van Rijn. Celebrated from an early age, he established a large studio in Amsterdam with many pupils who emulated his manner. A number of these students, such as Gerrit Dou and Nicolaes Maes, eventually succeeded in creating independent styles and successful careers of their own. As Rembrandt aged, his art fell out of fashion, and he became the target of rivals and critics, including some of his former followers.

Although he died in poverty, Rembrandt’s work was rediscovered by eighteenth-century collectors and has remained coveted ever since—including by The Met’s founders and donors. At the same time, doubts have arisen about whether certain paintings were made by Rembrandt himself, given the way he encouraged his students to mimic his style. This gallery presents his work with that of his contemporaries to explore the stylistic diversity of painting in his time.

I do not want to deny that I once had a special preference for [Rembrandt’s] manner; but at that time I had hardly begun to understand the infallible rules of art. I found it necessary to recant my error and to repudiate his.
—Gerard de Lairesse, 1707

As a young artist, De Lairesse admired Rembrandt’s work, but the two eventually came to represent opposing camps of idealism and realism.
Rembrandt’s portrait of a young De Lairesse is in The Robert Lehman Collection here at The Met.

The area of northwestern Europe that is today Belgium and the Netherlands experienced an economic boom in the 1400s that was spurred by the textile trade and strong economic ties with the Mediterranean, the Baltic, and the British Isles. Although the cities of this region fell under the rule of the dukes of Burgundy and, later, the Habsburg dynasty, they retained considerable independence as well as strong civic traditions expressed in architecture and ritual.

Against this backdrop, local painters enjoyed both increased patronage from merchants and the traditional support of the aristocracy and the church. Artists experimented with new materials and techniques, particularly in the medium of oil paint. Jan van Eyck, who served both the duke of Burgundy and wealthy merchants, inaugurated a revolutionary use of thin layers of oil paint to convey effects of realism and minute detail. As Van Eyck’s fame spread, collectors throughout Europe clamored for Netherlandish paintings. A number of artists from the region found success at Italian and Iberian courts.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles.

Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

Koç Family Galleries

The Koç Family Galleries (459 and 460)—Carpets, Textiles and the Greater Ottoman World and Arts of the Ottoman Court (14th–20th centuries)—present the art of the Ottoman world in a series of grand spaces, showing the rich diversity of courtly, provincial, and village art. The galleries provide a comprehensive overview of the multilayered nature of Ottoman patronage. Gallery 459 primarily displays the Museum's collection of Ottoman carpets, textiles, and arms and armor, shown under a spectacular wooden painted Mudejar ceiling, from 16th century Spain.

Before the late sixteenth century, Europe’s artistic establishment deemed scenes from everyday life too inconsequential for the noble art of painting. Yet these subjects became increasingly popular in the market around 1650—particularly in northern Europe, where Protestant reforms encouraged artists to abandon religious art and its traditional patron, the church. Innovative contemporary scenes set in upscale townhouses and rural inns often focused on class divisions through the pointed representation of etiquette, social and family dynamics, clothing, and interior decoration. Contrasting privileged and humble settings, artists offered models for emulation but also levied biting critiques and presented figures of ridicule.

By the second quarter of the eighteenth century, two new categories of painting had developed out of these shifts. In France, tableaux de modes, or fashionable subjects, recorded the latest trends in clothing and decor; in England, “conversation pieces,” often representing families in newly casual relationships and as if caught by chance, brought a new intimacy to portraiture.

This gallery is devoted to major figure paintings by the progenitors of modernism. Manet, Degas, and Fantin were considered to be the harbingers of The New Painting, also known as Impressionism. The Metropolitan—the first museum in the world to own Manet's work (1889)—has the most important collection of large-scale painting by these masters outside of France.

In the late fourteenth and early fifteenth centuries, artists from prominent courts across Europe began producing works that displayed a shared interest in sophisticated splendor, calligraphic line, and sharp contrasts between naturalism and fantasy. Now known as the International Gothic style, this movement is in many ways a manifestation of an increasingly mobile world. Artists and luxury goods traveled widely at this time, fueling rivalries and the exchange of influences among competing European courts, including those in Castile, Paris, Prague, Westminster, and the artistic centers of Italy.

While this artistic style became popular at all levels of society, attracting patrons from the merchant and working classes, it was primarily tied to the tastes and fashions of aristocratic members of powerful courts. Its visual language intertwined so closely with the ruling class that painters sometimes imposed hierarchies in their imagery, lending artificially elegant proportions to prominent figures while portraying more humble ones with unembellished naturalism. Though artists of the period experimented with the use of perspective, the spatial settings in these paintings are often surreal, focusing more on rich patterns and glittering gold backgrounds than rational architectural or natural settings.

properties.popup_header: Celebrating the Year of the Horse
properties.popup_body:
The traditional East Asian lunar calendar marks time through a twelve-year cycle, each year represented by the animals of the Chinese zodiac: rat, ox, tiger, rabbit, dragon, snake, horse, ram, monkey, rooster, dog, and pig. First associated with the Chinese calendar in the third century BCE and firmly established by the first century CE, these symbolic creatures are believed to embody certain traits that are manifested in the personalities of people born in that animal’s year. February 17, 2026, marks the beginning of the Year of the Horse, a creature symbolizing power, strength, and vitality.



Celebrating the Year of the Horse brings together works from The Met collection to explore the horse’s enduring nature and vital place in Chinese civilization. Domesticated in prehistoric times, this animal has shaped nearly every facet of life—from agriculture and transportation to trade, warfare, sport, and entertainment. In ancient China, horse-drawn chariots transported military commanders and the social elite, while in the Tang dynasty (618–907), tall and robust horses came to embody the strength of the state and cosmopolitan spirit of the empire. In Chinese popular culture, horses are among the most celebrated animals, and in the spiritual realm they serve as noble mounts for divine guardians believed to bring joy, protection, and prosperity to the household. This presentation, featuring a range of expressive works in ceramics, glass, jade, and metal, and woodblock prints, illuminates these roles while celebrating the horse’s power, vitality, and spirit.
  
Exhibition Open: Through January 26, 2027

properties.popup_header: Northern European Painters in Italy about 1800
properties.popup_body:
Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

This gallery features works by artists from throughout northern Europe who were active in Italy either as official members of arts academies or as part of more informal communities of artists, especially in and around Rome and the Bay of Naples. The selection ranges from topographical sketches to idealized landscapes and from figure studies to genre scenes that illustrate episodes from antiquity, religious themes, local customs, and brigands. Many of these paintings contain the seeds of Romanticism and Realism, even as they extend the Neoclassical tradition.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

"You can be a museum, or you can be modern, but you cannot be both." It was thanks to that famous dictum that the Metropolitan received, in 1947, its first and arguably most famous painting by Pablo Picasso, the 1906 portrait of Gertrude Stein, as a bequest of the sitter.

She wanted her portrait, tangible proof of her link to the man she considered to be the greatest artist of the twentieth century, to be displayed at the Metropolitan rather than New York's Museum of Modern Art.

In the sixty-five years since 1947, the Museum's Picasso collection has grown to nearly one hundred original paintings and works on paper and nearly four hundred prints. Especially rich in early works, the collection boasts masterpieces such as Seated Harlequin, The Blind Man's Meal, The Actor, and La Coiffure.

Matisse's great 1912 canvas Nasturtiums with the Painting "Dance" often hangs in this gallery. Thanks to the generosity of Pierre and Maria-Gaetana Matisse, the artist's son and daughter-in-law, many more works by Matisse are displayed here.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

properties.popup_header: Chinese Painting and Calligraphy: Selections from the Collection
properties.popup_body:
The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display.



The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 10, 2026

The Annenberg Galleries

The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818-826) include more than fifty works by eighteen of the most renowned artists working in France during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, among them Manet, Monet, Renoir, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Picasso.

Key early works by Monet, the quintessential Impressionist, are displayed in this gallery, from the Barbizon School The Bodmer Oak, Fontainebleau Forest to the iconic Garden at Sainte-Adresse.

Later works by Monet are on view in Gallery 819.

This gallery is one of nine that comprise The Annenberg Galleries.

Patti Cadby Birch Gallery

The Patti Cadby Birch Gallery showcases masterpieces from across the collection in the major media employed in the arts of the Islamic worlds: ceramics, metalware, glass, carpets and textiles, jeweled arts, calligraphy, painting, and architectural decoration. The styles, themes, and motifs that visitors encounter here will recur in successive rooms, thereby connecting distinct cultures. This gallery is one of three rooms named in honor of Patti Cadby Birch. The others are galleries 456 and 457.

Working across various European courts, the Venetian painter Giovanni Battista Tiepolo engineered enormous allegorical decorations that glorified family lineage. On view here are preparatory oil studies for some of his most important commissions, including illusionistic ceilings in Germany and Spain, alongside oil sketches by painters from Italy, England, Spain, and Hungary that demonstrate his international impact.

The Met offers one of the most comprehensive collections of Tiepolo’s work outside of Italy. Read together, these paintings also document the Italian peninsula’s multiracial composition. Black men and women appear repeatedly in his paintings, in roles ranging from the symbolic to the everyday. Black Venetians had been part of the city since at least the fifteenth century, when an international slave trade forcibly transported people from North and sub-Saharan Africa to Europe. Within their lifetimes, many individuals obtained their freedom, and their descendants practiced a wide range of professions.

Tiepolo’s career and his fame were international. In addition to making multiyear journeys to execute site-specific works in oil and fresco (indicated above in black), he also received commissions that he fulfilled remotely (indicated in white).

The Museum's preeminent collections of American silver, ceramics, glass, and jewelry are installed on this level and on the mezzanine balcony below.

Aesthetic Movement

Among the nineteenth-century works on view on the West Balcony of The Charles Engelhard Court are silver, glass, and ceramics of the Aesthetic movement, objects made between the 1870s and 1890s and characterized by conventionalized surface decoration, an eclectic synthesis of styles, and a diversity of innovative techniques. Like their British counterparts, American designers found inspiration in Chinese, Islamic, and especially Japanese precedents. This strong interest in the exotic was translated into new forms and a creative use of materials often employed to simulate precious materials. Stylistically, the influence of Japanese art is evident in the use of such natural motifs as birds, fish, flowers, and insects, and the prevalence of asymmetrical composition.

Expositions

The West Balcony features a number of objects originally made for and exhibited at the Philadelphia Centennial Exposition of 1876 and the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, two of the country's most important international expositions of the last quarter of the nineteenth century. These exhibitions were critical venues for manufacturers to publicize and present their latest artistic and technical achievements to an increasingly sophisticated and style-conscious world audience. Unparalleled in conceit, craftsmanship, and design–and often scale–these presentation pieces often evoked national pride through symbolism and historical references or celebrated the country's bounty, as seen in the eclectic subject matter. The exemplary objects on display here are among the most ambitious works ever produced by the major firms of the day, notably Tiffany & Co. and Union Porcelain Works.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles.

Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

Florence Gould Gallery in the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

During the first half of the twentieth century, Pierre Bonnard and Édouard Vuillard were recognized as two of the most skilled colorists working in France. This gallery is devoted to their domestic interiors and lushly colored garden scenes.

The Florence Gould Gallery is one of ten rooms that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

properties.popup_header: The Annenberg Collection: Nineteenth- and Twentieth-Century Masters
properties.popup_body:
The Annenberg Galleries

The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818–826) includes more than fifty works by eighteen of the most renowned artists working in France during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, among them Manet, Monet, Renoir, Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, and Picasso.

The Annenberg Collection (Galleries 821-823) was assembled by Walter H. Annenberg (1908–2002) and his wife, Leonore Annenberg (1918–2009), over a period of four decades, beginning in the early 1950s. The Annenberg gift stipulates that the collection must be exhibited together and always remain on the premises of the Metropolitan Museum.

The Museum's preeminent collections of American silver, ceramics, and glass are installed on this level and on the mezzanine balcony below.

Late Classical and Rococo Revival Styles

The South Balcony of The Charles Engelhard Court displays silver, ceramics, and glass in the late Classical and Rococo revival styles. Ranging in date from 1815 through the 1870s, these objects reflect keen awareness of the latest European styles. By the second decade of the nineteenth century, the French Empire style held sway, bringing with it a bolder, more archaeologically correct interpretation of a classical decorative vocabulary. Around mid-century, the Rococo idiom was revived, manifesting itself in exuberant, naturalistic ornament, particularly on silver and ceramic wares. These objects illustrate the increasing sophistication of American artists and craftsmen, who employed technological innovations to produce an increasing variety of goods for an expanding group of consumers. Other examples of silver, ceramics, and glass from this period are on view in Gallery 731, Gallery 732, Gallery 733, and Gallery 736.

properties.popup_header: Hierarchy, Gender, and the French Academy
properties.popup_body:
Jayne Wrightsman Gallery

Academic hierarchy structured the lives of successful French painters. Artists skilled in depicting ancient and religious history ranked highest, followed by those proficient in portraiture, then animals, then landscape, then still life. The men making these distinctions hoped to ensure that painting was taken seriously as a liberal, intellectual profession and not a craft like woodworking or metalsmithing. Ironically, an expanding exhibition culture and art market rewarded painters in a different way: the public and collectors tended to prefer to look at portraits, scenes of daily life, or animals and flowers.

Until the fall of the French monarchy in 1789, the academy permitted only four women members at any given time. This limited their voice and their ability to exhibit. Although art history has often associated women with landscape and still life, they mostly painted portraiture and history subjects. The collection at The Met affords a unique opportunity to view works by women artists of this period.

If a Painter wishes to instruct his daughter in his Art, he will never plan to make her a History Painter; he will be sure to tell her she may only pretend to the genre of portraiture, miniature or flowers. Thus he discourages her, and extinguishes the spark of her imagination. She will paint only roses; she was perhaps born to paint Heroes!
—Stéphanie-Félicité de Genlis, 1784

Weekly intellectual gatherings, typically hosted by women such as Genlis, were a dominant feature of Parisian society and created spaces for lively debate.

The Annenberg Galleries

During the last forty years of his long career, Monet often depicted the same subject a number of times under various weather and lighting conditions. These "series paintings," several of which are represented by works on view in this gallery, are celebrated for their uncompromising naturalism yet they are actually among Monet's most intensely subjective works. In general, he began the series paintings out-of-doors but completed them in the quiet of his studio where he focused on the purely formal and decorative aspects of his compositions. The extensive gardens he cultivated on his property in Giverny became the principal subject for his late works.

Key early works by Monet are on view in Gallery 818.

This gallery is one of nine that comprise The Annenberg Galleries.

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

The André Mertens Galleries for Musical Instruments
The John Pierpont Morgan Wing

The Met is home to one of the world’s most diverse and important collections of musical instruments. Explore the artistry, diverse forms and interwoven uses of brass instruments in the Fanfare installation. This display showcases 74 instruments spanning two millennia and five continents.

At the start of his career, Courbet provoked a series of scandals with politically and aesthetically inflammatory paintings of everyday life. This gallery focuses on two principal themes that subsequently occupied the artist in the late 1850s and 1860s: hunting, as dramatized in After the Hunt, and sensual female nudes ranging from the famous Woman with a Parrot to Woman in the Waves. Courbet remained a controversial figure, but not at the expense of popular and commercial success.

Additional paintings by Courbet are on view in Gallery 809 and Gallery 812.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millenium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Major strengths include pre–Angkorian Khmer and related stone and bronze sculpture, Javanese gold jewelry and bronze sculpture, and glazed ceramics from Thailand and Vietnam.

Note: The arts of the non–Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

Lila Acheson Wallace Wing

Although The Met has been collecting and exhibiting work by living artists since its founding in 1870, it only created its first department devoted exclusively to contemporary (and, later, to modern and contemporary) art in 1967. Today, the department's holdings in post-World War II and early twenty-first century art comprise almost half of the holdings in the department’s collection, including paintings, sculpture, design, decorative arts, and time-based media. Since 2012, The Met has greatly expanded and diversified its collection of contemporary art, especially of works by women artists and artists of color, with particular emphasis placed on artists working in Latin America, the Middle East, Turkey, South Asia, Africa and North America. The department presents rotating displays of this newly dynamic collection in these galleries of the Lila Acheson Wallace Wing.

The Great Hall Balcony (Gallery 200 through Gallery 205) traces the development of Chinese ceramics, including the creation of vibrant green and blue (or celadon) glazes of the tenth to the thirteenth century and the flowering of porcelains painted in underglaze cobalt blue in the fourteenth. Also presented here are the interchanges between Chinese ceramics and those in other parts of the world. Among the works on view, highlights include a group of ceramics illustrating the transformation of Chinese ceramics, in part due to the rise of global trade, from the late sixteenth to the seventeenth century and Japanese trade ceramics in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

The Havemeyer Galleries

Four galleries (Gallery 814–817) feature the Museum's collection of works by Degas, one of the world's largest and most comprehensive.

The richness of these holdings is due in large part to Mrs. Louisine Havemeyer, one of the principal collectors of modern European art in America at the turn of the twentieth century. She began acquiring Degas's work in the late 1870s at the urging of her friend, Mary Cassatt, the famous American Impressionist, and came to prefer his work above all other living artists.

By the time of her death, Mrs. Havemeyer had amassed a collection of more than sixty paintings, drawings, and pastels by Degas, as well as all seventy bronzes and a large number of prints. The bulk of her collection—the most complete group of Degas's works ever assembled—entered the Metropolitan upon Mrs. Havemeyer's death in 1929. This gallery also includes small-scale works by Tissot and other artists in Degas's circle.

This gallery is one of three that comprise the Havemeyer Galleries.

properties.popup_header: Cities, States, and Early Empires: The Ancient Near East ca. 3000–1200 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
The rise of territorial states and empires in Mesopotamia, Anatolia, and Syria during the third and second millennia B.C., with their palaces and temples, provided the context for the creation of elite works of art, including stone sculptures, metalwork, jewelry, carved ivories, ceramics, and cylinder seals.

They document regional cultural developments and illustrate interconnections between peoples that stretched from the Mesopotamian heartland eastward to Iran, Bactria-Margiana in western Central Asia, and the Indus Valley, and westward to the lands bordering the Mediterranean Sea.

properties.popup_body:

Its distinctiveness arises from the ingenuity and mastery of the potters, as well as the wide range of wares that have long supported daily life. Japanese artisans developed kintsugi, the art of repairing broken ceramics with gold lacquer, restoring the damaged object’s function while making it even more precious and valuable. Japanese cuisine and dining aesthetics inspired vessels designed to harmonize with the color and texture of food. Many porcelains from the Edo period (1615–1868) are embellished with motifs drawn from the natural world or symbols of happiness, longevity, and good fortune.

Featuring refined ceramics and other artworks from the Museum’s Harry G. C. Packard Collection, this exhibition celebrates the 50th anniversary of this landmark acquisition that established the foundation of The Met’s Japanese art holdings. During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:
Rotation 1: January 19–May 12, 2026
Rotation 2: May 14–August 16, 2026
Rotation 3: August 29–December 8, 2026
Rotation 4: December 10, 2026–March 28, 2027
Rotation 5: April 10–August 8, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through August 8, 2027

Oil paint on canvas won out as the dominant choice for European painting, but at various points in history and in different regions, artists painted on other materials: copper, wood, plaster, and stone each had their appeal and allowed for visual effects impossible on canvas. Artists often learned how best to apply pigments to new surfaces by borrowing techniques from other media, such as sculpture, ceramics, stone work, and jewelry.

Through groupings of paintings on canvas, copper, wood, plaster, and stone, this gallery offers an opportunity to compare features of some of the supports European painters used from about 1320 to 1880.

Expertise transmitted from China and Korea in medieval times facilitated the development of new Japanese techniques and wares. Over time, the reverence for prized ceramics led to finding beauty even through damage. Japanese artisans developed kintsugi, the art of repairing broken ceramics with gold lacquer, restoring the damaged object’s function while making it even more precious and valuable. Japanese cuisine and dining aesthetics inspired vessels designed to harmonize with the color and texture of food. Many porcelains from the Edo period (1615–1868) are embellished with motifs drawn from the natural world or symbols of happiness, longevity, and good fortune.

Featuring refined ceramics and other artworks from the Museum’s Harry G. C. Packard Collection, this exhibition celebrates the 50th anniversary of this landmark acquisition that established the foundation of The Met’s Japanese art holdings. By placing ceramics in dialogue with related art forms—including lacquers, textiles, paintings, and woodblock prints—the display inspires curiosity about the multiverse of Japanese ceramics and its broader cultural contexts.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:

Rotation 2: May 14–August 16, 2026
Rotation 3: August 29–December 8, 2026
Rotation 4: December 10, 2026–March 28, 2027
Rotation 5: April 10–August 8, 2027
  
Exhibition Open: Through August 8, 2027

The Sharmin and Bijan Mossavar-Rahmani Gallery
The Lamont Wing

The Sharmin and Bijan Mossavar-Rahmani Gallery continues a chronological overview of the art of the Iranian world while underscoring its many connections with other cultures and regions. It features masterpieces created in Tabriz and Isfahan under the imperial Safavid dynasty in the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries and its successors. Highlights include masterly creations in a range of media, from carpets to lacquer and the famous illustrations to the early 16th century Book of Kings commissioned by Shah Tahmasp (r. 1524-76) known as the Shahnama of Shah Tahmasp, displayed in specially designed cases for seated visitors to view.

Early eighteenth-century visual arts reflect a cultural fascination with the supposed simplicity of country life—experienced vicariously by wealthy Parisians—and with performance, in which role reversal and masquerade unraveled social protocols. These new attitudes are encapsulated by the fête galante, a term coined to describe innovative paintings by Antoine Watteau representing “gallant” outdoor pursuits—notably amorous games imagined in romantically disheveled gardens. Musical themes suggest the lovers’ harmony and echo the amateur theater performances and concerts familiar to Watteau’s patrons. These intimate images spoke to a more informal world outside the royal court and became enormously popular; prints based on Watteau’s work and paintings by his immediate followers disseminated the fête galante across Europe.

Watteau’s world turned away from Italian grandeur and took cues from northern European painting, an often-underappreciated influence on French visual culture. Another artist attuned to Dutch and Flemish painting, Jean Siméon Chardin, pursued unassuming subject matter in still lifes and depictions of seemingly mundane daily activities. Clarity of composition, brilliance of paint handling, and focused attention counteracted the bombast of Baroque art.

The Annenberg Galleries

Among the most influential and experimental of the Impressionist artists, Camille Pissarro emerged in the 1860s as a specialist in broadly painted landscapes in the tradition of Courbet and Monet.

In the late 1880s and early 1890s, Pissarro explored the virtues of the newly minted divisionist (or pointillist) technique, often for heroic representations of peasants. At the end of his career, the artist returned to his Impressionist roots, producing a series of market scenes and cityscapes that evoke the looser, more spontaneous style of his youth.

This gallery is one of nine that comprise The Annenberg Galleries (Galleries 818-826).

The Doris and Stanley Tananbaum Galleries

By the mid-eighteenth century, portraiture and decorative arts were firmly established in colonial British America as domestic art forms embodying the ambitions and tastes of a wealthy society. Expanded trade and travel created networks of exchange as artists, objects, ideas, and cultural practices spread across the Atlantic. This gallery features paintings by émigré and colonial-born artists that captured the status and values of their sitters on the eve of the American Revolution. The decorative arts on view also reveal the personal wealth accumulated through thriving mercantile trade in the colonies, which fueled patronage of various workshops.

Academic training opportunities drew many artists abroad, notably to London, where they established themselves as professional painters. Benjamin West, for example, advanced the genre of history painting by depicting contemporary events, an approach later embraced by John Singleton Copley. Following their studies, artists often returned to the United States to paint celebratory works that expressed the pride of a young republic born of revolution. Other works suggest how the exclusion of women, Indigenous peoples, and enslaved individuals from the nation’s conception of civil society contradicted its founding principles of equality and liberty.

properties.popup_header: Chinese Painting and Calligraphy: Selections from the Collection
properties.popup_body:
The Met acquired its first Chinese painting in 1902. Since then, the Museum has added more than two thousand works of painting and calligraphy, building one of the most comprehensive collections in the world. Spanning fifteen hundred years of cultural production and featuring a variety of genres, techniques, and styles, The Met's collection has become a key resource for the study of Chinese painting and calligraphy. This exhibition presents a rich selection of works from the collection arranged in a largely chronological display.



The final gallery tells the story of Wen C. Fong (1930–2018), chairman of the Museum’s Department of Asian Art from 1971 to 2000, and his teacher Li Jian (1881–1956). As a young calligraphy prodigy in Shanghai in the 1940s, Fong studied brush arts with Li. The display centers on a group of fans, painted and written by Li, that were intended to transmit the canonical models of the classical tradition from teacher to student. Brought to the United States in 1949 and treasured by the Fong family since then, these fans are presented publicly for the first time.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 10, 2026

Kenneth Jay Lane Gallery in the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

In 1798 Napoleon conquered Egypt, which had been a province of the Ottoman Empire since 1517. Although he was driven out by the British after one year, his campaign unleashed in Europe a profound curiosity about North Africa and the Middle East, which was immediately made manifest in European painting.

Throughout the nineteenth century, artists crossed the straits of Gibraltar and roamed as far as Syria and Turkey in search of picturesque motifs and exotic landscapes. This gallery displays works by several generations of artists, from David's student Girodet, to Ingres's student Chassériau, to one of the last Neoclassical painters, J. L. Gérôme.

The Kenneth Jay Lane Gallery is one of ten rooms that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

properties.popup_header: From the Achaemenid to the Sasanian Empire: The Ancient Near East and Silk Road Contacts ca. 559 B.C.–A.D. 651
properties.popup_body:
This gallery focuses on the four great, late powers of the Near East: the Achaemenid Persian, Seleucid, Parthian, and Sasanian empires.

Achaemenid Persian arts (ca. 559–331 B.C.) are represented by fine metal vessels, jewelry, and architectural reliefs from Persepolis, the ceremonial capital of the empire. The Seleucid (331–138 B.C.) and Parthian empires (247 B.C.–A.D. 224) were characterized by a hybrid culture that incorporated both Near Eastern and Greek elements in works of art such as ceramic and metal rhytons, jewelry, and distinctive sculpture from the city of Palmyra. Works of art of the succeeding Sasanian empire (A.D. 224–651) demonstrate connections between Iran, Central Asia, and China through royal iconography and luxury works in silver, silk, and gold. Further glimpses of exchange along the Silk Road are offered by votive panels from Bactria and a rare caftan of the eighth century from the Caucasus region.

By the 1300s, painting on panel took on renewed importance as an artistic medium in Europe. Changes in church practices led to a focus on decorating altars with paintings, giving artists a prominent space to develop novel styles and techniques of storytelling. Political events also played a role: After the 1204 sacking and looting of Constantinople, one of the most vibrant artistic centers in the world, religious icons and other artworks dating back to the ancient world were dispersed. Paintings that evaded destruction reached artists working throughout Europe, inspiring a new generation.
The artworks here reflect the great variety of Christian religious imagery produced over the course of several centuries in Europe when painting took center stage, including multipanel altarpieces, processional banners, and works that suggest images could be divinely created. Indeed, an important aspect of the history of European devotional painting can be found in Christian legend itself, in which Saint Luke is discussed as the first icon painter, and Jesus as the author of the first “true image”—an imprint of his own likeness left on a cloth used to wipe his face on the way to his crucifixion.

Patti Cadby Birch Court

The Patti Cadby Birch Court, based on Moroccan late medieval design, was constructed in 2011 by craftsmen from Fez as an intimate interior court. This area of repose and quiet reflection underscores the living artistic practices of northern Africa. Here, original Nasrid columns from medieval Spain define the patio space, and dadoes of custom-made glazed tiles in a traditional pattern frame a fountain that brings the sound of falling water to the galleries. It sits adjacent to the Patti Cadby Birch Gallery for art from Spain, North Africa, and the Western Mediterranean (Gallery 457).

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millenium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Major strengths include pre–Angkorian Khmer and related stone and bronze sculpture, Javanese gold jewelry and bronze sculpture, and glazed ceramics from Thailand and Vietnam.

Note: The arts of the non–Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving Southeast Asian galleries (Gallery 244 through Gallery 250) are devoted to the early bronze cultures, spanning from the early centuries B.C. to the first millenium A.D. and to the sculpture of the Hindu-Buddhist period, eighth to eighteenth century. The latter encompass the arts of Burma (Myanmar), Thailand, Cambodia, Vietnam, and Indonesia. Note: The arts of the non–Hindu-Buddhist cultures from Indonesia and the Philippines are exhibited in Gallery 355, within the galleries for the Arts of Africa, Oceania, and the Americas.

Henry J. Heinz II Galleries

For much of the nineteenth century, British painting was a powerful stimulus to artists throughout Europe. In no area was Britain more influential than landscape painting. The lively brushwork, sparkling light, and realistic detail found in John Constable's scenes of the English countryside—which caused a sensation at the Paris Salon of 1824—spurred artists to depict their native scenery with new directness. J. M. W. Turner's dramatic, sometimes nearly abstract, evocations of sea, air, and sky, inspired painters as diverse as Eugène Delacroix and Claude Monet to portray the fleeting effects of nature in their work. A similar vigor and candor animates British figure painting of the period, which is often rooted in contemporary life.

This gallery is one of ten that comprise the Henry J. Heinz II Galleries.

The Havemeyer Galleries

Four galleries (Gallery 814–817) feature the Museum's collection of works by Degas, one of the world's largest and most comprehensive.

The richness of these holdings is due in large part to Mrs. Louisine Havemeyer, one of the principal collectors of modern European art in America at the turn of the twentieth century. She began acquiring Degas's work in the late 1870s at the urging of her friend, Mary Cassatt, the famous American Impressionist, and came to prefer his work above all other living artists.

By the time of her death, Mrs. Havemeyer had amassed a collection of more than sixty paintings, drawings, and pastels by Degas, as well as all seventy bronzes and a large number of prints. The bulk of her collection—the most complete group of Degas's works ever assembled—entered the Metropolitan upon Mrs. Havemeyer's death in 1929.

This gallery is one of three that comprise the Havemeyer Galleries.

Janice H. Levin Gallery

The Michael C. Rockefeller Wing

After the French national collections in Paris, The Met owns the largest group of paintings by Camille Corot—the most protean and poetic landscapist of nineteenth-century France—many of which are displayed in the Janice H. Levin Gallery. His late views of Ville d'Avray veiled in silvery mists are universally admired, but only at The Met can one also see the limpid Italian landscapes of his youth, the grand history paintings of his maturity, and the many fine figure paintings that he made in the last decade of his life.

properties.popup_header: The Calculated Curve: Eighteenth-Century American Furniture
properties.popup_body:
The George M. and Linda H. Kaufman Galleries in the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

Visitors to the Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries will encounter a dramatic presentation of masterworks in The Met collection of North American furniture produced between 1720 and 1775. During this period of great aesthetic achievement, talented makers rejected the heft and rectilinearity of earlier approaches and embraced more liberating designs conceived almost entirely of curves and abstract motifs inspired by nature. This display encourages greater consideration of the works’ sculptural expression and elastic forms, the interplay of light, and the artists’ abilities to push materials to their limits.

Furniture has always been intertwined with everyday life, but it is also an essential art form. Underlining its utility are thoughtful mathematical and architectural strategies, structural engineering, and aesthetic innovations akin to other mediums. The shapely forms and textured ornament, as well as the incorporation of ergonomics to accommodate the human body, reveal the fundamental artistry of furniture design.

Some of the brilliant furniture makers featured in this installation are identified, while others’ names were not recorded. Despite their crucial contributions, talented people of color, immigrants, and teenagers were least likely to receive credit for their work. Women were also critical to the production and reception of early North American furniture, providing immeasurable operational support to workshops and access to capital and patrons through kinship networks. Additional furniture holdings—and their fascinating stories—can be explored in the historical interiors, or period rooms, throughout the American Wing.

The Havemeyer Galleries

Four galleries (Gallery 814–817) feature the Museum's collection of works by Degas, one of the world's largest and most comprehensive.

Edgar Degas made sculptures throughout his long career, always working in wax. On display in this room are bronzes cast after some of his small-scale wax figures.

The subjects largely coincide with the themes of his most popular paintings and pastels: dancers, bathers, and racehorses. Only one of Degas's sculptures was shown during his lifetime: the nearly lifesize Little Fourteen-Year-Old Dancer (on view in Gallery 815). The other wax models were discovered in his studio after his death.

The Metropolitan's patron Louisine W. Havemeyer paid for bronzes to be cast of nearly half of the pieces and gave the Museum the first set ("A").

This gallery is one of three that comprise the Havemeyer Galleries.

properties.popup_header: Spain, North Africa, and the Western Mediterranean (8th–19th centuries)
properties.popup_body:
Patti Cadby Birch Court and Gallery

The Patti Cadby Birch Court and Gallery showcases the spread of Arab influence to the west through the rich material culture of al-Andalus, highlighting the arts of the tenth-century caliphate of Cordoba and the fourteenth- and fifteenth-century Nasrid emirate of Granada. Manuscripts on view illuminate the reciprocal creative exchanges between Muslim, Christian, and Jewish populations in Spain and shared styles with north Africa. Highlights on display include important loans from the Hispanic Society of America.

properties.popup_header: South Asian Hindu-Buddhist and Jain Sculpture
properties.popup_body:
Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The Florence and Herbert Irving South Asian Galleries (Gallery 234 through Gallery 243) represent India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. They primarily trace the development of the sculptural arts associated with the temples and shrines of Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. The arts of the Indian subcontinent are represented from early centuries B.C. up to around the sixteenth century, and encompass all the major regional and dynastic styles. Highlight areas include early arts of Shunga India, Buddhist art of the Gandharan and Amaravati regions, the Kushan and Gupta dynasties of northern India, the Kashmiri kingdoms, Pala eastern India, Pandyan and Chola south India, and medieval northern India.

Note: Islamic arts of South Asia are displayed in the galleries overseen by the Department of Islamic Art.

The André Meyer Galleries
Michael C. Rockefeller Wing

Artistic production in Europe during the first half of the nineteenth century was dominated by the conflict between two aesthetic movements, Neoclassicism and Romanticism.

This gallery features works by the artists generally regarded as the most prominent representatives: J. A. D. Ingres and his students Chassériau and Lehmann; and Eugène Delacroix and his mentor, Théodore Gericault. Ingres's works were said to elevate the prominence of line over color, exemplified by his Odalisque in Grisaille and the two portraits of M. and Mme. Leblanc. Delacroix's works promoted the expressive use of color and furious, Baroque-style compositions.

This gallery is one of two that comprise The André Meyer Galleries (see also Gallery 802).

properties.popup_header: European Migration and North American Furniture, 1600-1730
properties.popup_body:
The Anthony W. and Lulu C. Wang Galleries of Eighteenth-Century American Art

The artworks in this gallery represent the earliest forms in the North American Colonies produced by European settlers, predominantly from England, France, Germany, and the Netherlands. Their design, function, markings, and condition reveal the complexities of spirituality, social hierachies, and commerce in the 1600s and early 1700s.

North American furniture of this period was frequently made of oak with nailed or mortise-and-tenon joinery. Whether constructed using turned parts or frames and inset panels, the furniture was durable and solid with straight, sober lines. Colonial artisans in the 1600s were heavily influenced by Mannerism, a northern European interpretation of art from the late Italian Renaissance. Their designs feature stylized plant forms carved in low relief and abstract, geometric shapes. Makers applied split-turned elements and moldings to surfaces and sometimes painted them to achieve dramatic architectural depth.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries for Chinese Decorative Arts

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

Opened on May 19, 2014, the Chinese Treasury re-creates the type of collecting and display found in the treasure cabinets (duo bao ge) assembled throughout the late Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1911) dynasties. Extraordinary works in ivory, rhinoceros horn, glass, porcelain, and jade and other hard stones celebrate the skill and imagination of Chinese artists in designing and crafting these materials. Touchpads allow viewers to read introductory texts for all of the objects and to explore further by grouping the works of art by material and by themes.

The Wentworth Room is from the Portsmouth, New Hampshire, home of John Wentworth (1671–1730) and his wife, Sarah Hunking Wentworth (ca. 1673–1741). The room is furnished with imported and American-made furniture in the William and Mary style.

Originally a "best chamber," or bedroom, on the second floor, the Wentworth Room is one of the largest domestic interiors surviving from the early colonial period. Period diaries reveal the best chamber was used for dining and entertaining in addition to sleeping. The room probably contained a high bedstead with a handsome set of bed hangings, but this installation has omitted the bed in order to show a variety of new furniture forms and the activities that took place in the room.

properties.popup_header: Embracing Color: Enamel in Chinese Decorative Arts, 1300–1900
properties.popup_body:
Enamel decoration is a significant element of Chinese decorative arts that has long been overlooked. This exhibition reveals the aesthetic, technical, and cultural achievement of Chinese enamel wares by demonstrating the transformative role of enamel during the Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1911) dynasties. The first transformational moment occurred in the late 14th to 15th century, when the introduction of cloisonné enamel from the West, along with the development of porcelain with overglaze enamels, led to a shift away from a monochromatic palette to colorful works. The second transformation occurred in the late 17th to 18th century, when European enameling materials and techniques were brought to the Qing court and more subtle and varied color tones were developed on enamels applied over porcelain, metal, glass, and other mediums. In both moments, Chinese artists did not simply adopt or copy foreign techniques; they actively created new colors and styles that reflected their own taste. The more than 100 objects on view are drawn mainly from The Met collection.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:

The New England meetinghouse provided a place for religious, social, and civic gatherings. The heavy timber-framed ceiling of this gallery is a 1924 reproduction of the ceiling in the 1681 meetinghouse in Hingham, Massachusetts—the last surviving example of the once-numerous seventeenth-century meetinghouses. The high, open-timbered space is based on the halls of English manor houses built in the Gothic tradition and serves to recall the late medieval heritage of the first American colonists. The large windows at the gable ends of the gallery, intended to provide adequate natural light, follow the precedent of such English halls.

Florence and Herbert Irving Galleries

Florence and Herbert Irving Asian Wing

The religious arts of Tibet and Nepal are represented in Gallery 252 and Gallery 253 by important early cloth paintings (tangkas), including rare portrait paintings, and by Vajrayana Buddhist sculptures. The early Nepalese metal, stone, and wood sculptures form a major ensemble, as do the Buddhist and Hindu painted book covers. Ritual utensils such as crowns, weapons, and libation implements form another important aspect of the displays. The tangkas and manuscript objects are rotated periodically.

Jan and Warren Adelson Gallery

Displayed in this gallery are examples of imported household objects that could be found in a well-to-do home in early America. Although most imports came from England, goods made on the Continent and in the Far East were also central to daily life. Indeed, globalization is not a recent phenomenon: even in the seventeenth century, a typical colonist's "worldly goods" came from all over the globe.

The Hart Room is the earliest period room in the American Wing. Its architectural elements are typical of seventeenth-century New England interiors, and the furnishings are fine examples of late seventeenth-century Massachusetts-made furniture, a melding of late Renaissance and Mannerist designs from northern Europe and England and craft practices brought to the colonies by English joiners and turners.

The room is from the home of Samuel (1645–1725) and Sarah Norton (1647–1727) Hart of Ipswich, Massachusetts.

The Hewlett Room is presented as a gallery of painted furniture rather than furnished as a period room. During the seventeenth century, newly instituted trade with the Far East and India introduced materials and decorative techniques previously unknown to Europe. Motifs from these popular imported wares, such as ceramics, lacquer, and textiles, found their way onto American painted furniture. Chests were often painted to simulate exotic woods, marbles, tortoiseshell, or ivory. Naturalistic landscapes and courting couples similar to those found in contemporary needlework sometimes decorated this bright and fanciful furniture.

Select pieces of eighteenth-century New York furniture are displayed in this alcove, the reinstallation of which was made possible by The Peggy N. and Roger G. Gerry Charitable Trust. The paneling on this fireplace wall is made of gumwood, which in the eighteenth century was used almost exclusively in the New York area. The Benjamin Hasbrouck House, from which the paneling was taken, still stands.

properties.popup_header: A Passion for Jade: The Bishop Collection
properties.popup_body:
More than a hundred remarkable objects from the Heber Bishop collection, including carvings of jade, the most esteemed stone in China, and many other hardstones, are on view in this focused presentation. The refined works represent the sophisticated art of Chinese gemstone carvers during the Qing dynasty (1644–1911) as well as the highly accomplished skills of Mogul Indian (1526–1857) craftsmen, which provided an exotic inspiration to their Chinese counterparts. Also on view are a set of Chinese stone-working tools and illustrations of jade workshops, which will introduce the traditional method of working jade.

The exhibition is made possible by the Florence and Herbert Irving Fund for Asian Art Exhibitions.

properties.popup_header: Embracing Color: Enamel in Chinese Decorative Arts, 1300–1900
properties.popup_body:
Enamel decoration is a significant element of Chinese decorative arts that has long been overlooked. This exhibition reveals the aesthetic, technical, and cultural achievement of Chinese enamel wares by demonstrating the transformative role of enamel during the Ming (1368–1644) and Qing (1644–1911) dynasties. The first transformational moment occurred in the late 14th to 15th century, when the introduction of cloisonné enamel from the West, along with the development of porcelain with overglaze enamels, led to a shift away from a monochromatic palette to colorful works. The second transformation occurred in the late 17th to 18th century, when European enameling materials and techniques were brought to the Qing court and more subtle and varied color tones were developed on enamels applied over porcelain, metal, glass, and other mediums. In both moments, Chinese artists did not simply adopt or copy foreign techniques; they actively created new colors and styles that reflected their own taste. The more than 100 objects on view are drawn mainly from The Met collection.

In Hinduism, the act of darshan, or “seeing god,” is central to worship. This intimate exchange between deity and devotee traditionally takes place in the temple. But within each home is also a shrine, dedicated to that householder’s chosen deity. The need to display an image of the divine in the home was traditionally fulfilled by small icons made of clay or metal. In the mid-19th century, new technologies were introduced into India, first photography and then the chromolithographic press. The latter permitted the production of inexpensive prints of the Hindu gods for mass consumption. These proved immensely popular and for the first time in India, even the humblest home could afford a colorful icon of their chosen god to display in the household shrine.

Household Gods: Hindu Devotional Prints, 1860–1930 presents the first encyclopedic exhibition of these chromolithographic prints from the pioneering studio presses of Calcutta (Kolkata), Poona (Pune), and Bombay (Mumbai). These mass-produced prints became a powerful means of expressing Indian religious identity at a time when the country was experiencing the first stirrings of the Independence movement.

Featuring approximately 120 works, shown in four rotations, from The Met’s collection of chromolithographic prints, along with paintings and portable triptych shrines, Household Gods provides a unique window on the vibrant tradition of Indian devotional imagery on the cusp of modernity.

During the exhibition, a selection of artworks will come off view and be replaced by new works:

The front door of the Wentworth House opened directly onto this small but beautifully detailed staircase. The spiral turnings on the stair balusters are the earliest known in New England. The Wentworth Room (Gallery 711) was on the second story of the same house.

properties.popup_header: The Third Intermediate Period and Kushite Art
properties.popup_body:
Lila Acheson Wallace Galleries of Egyptian Art

Most Egyptian art that survives from about 1100 to 700 B.C. comes from workshops that produced beautiful objects dedicated to divine beings for temples. Much of the art displayed in this gallery is therefore identified as temple donations or cultic equipment. Many pieces reflect the high quality of metalworking associated with the time. Later when the Kushite kings conquered Egypt around 700 B.C., they continued to support artistic production, but added their own vigor to traditional Egyptian forms. At the gallery’s entrance, temple offerings in faience express elements of a well-known Egyptian story, “The Myth of the Faraway Goddess.”

properties.popup_header: Baseball Cards from the Collection of Jefferson R. Burdick
properties.popup_body: Jefferson R. Burdick (1900–1963) donated 303,000 trade cards, postcards, and posters to The Met in the 1940s. His gift included baseball cards produced in the United States that date from the 1880s to the 1960s. Highlights from the baseball card collection, which represents one of the most comprehensive collections of its type in a public institution, are on rotating view.

properties.popup_header: Iba Ndiaye: Between Latitude and Longitude
properties.popup_body:
Between Latitude and Longitude constitutes the inaugural exhibition in the Michael C. Rockefeller Wing’s in-focus gallery, part of the complete major reenvisioning of The Met collection of African art. In celebration of the initiative, an artistic landmark work by Senegalese Modernist Iba Ndiaye (1928–2008), Tabaski III, a gift to The Met, is being ushered into the collection. Since the 1982 opening of the Rockefeller Wing, a canon of African Modernist painting has taken shape, and Ndiaye emerges consistently as a foundational figure of international importance, yet his contributions remain largely unknown outside Senegal.

Born in Saint Louis to a Catholic mother and Muslim father, Ndiaye, upon his completion of a secondary education at the prestigious Lycée Faidherbe, left for France to pursue fine arts studies. On the eve of Senegal’s independence, he was summoned to Dakar by its first president, the poet and statesman Léopold Sédar Senghor, to establish a national school of fine arts. Tabaski III was painted upon his subsequent return to France in 1970, where he remained the rest of his career. Its subject is the Feast of Sacrifice, Eid al-Adha, known as Tabaski in Wolof, the second of the most important celebrations in Islam that honors the willingness of Abraham to sacrifice his son as an act of obedience to God’s command. With this depiction, Ndiaye selected a notable subject in the history of art that he boldly represented as experienced within a West African context. At the same time, he does so in a painterly idiom adopted from his careful study and embrace of European old master painting. A partnership between Met curators specializing in African art and European paintings, this presentation introduces the work in relation to the artist’s diverse source material from across The Met, an institution that was a source of profound inspiration. Among the selection are works by Rembrandt, Goya, Degas, Derain, and Bacon, along with key works of African sculpture, textiles, and metalwork, and Islamic illumination.
  
Exhibition Open: Through May 31, 2026

properties.popup_header: The Jake and Nancy Hamon Gallery
properties.popup_body:
Jake and Nancy Hamon Gallery

This gallery features works of Cypriot art from the periods of Hellenistic and Roman rule. Artistic production followed the prevailing styles of the Hellenistic world, although regional traditions continued—as represented, for example, by the sculptures of "temple boys." The art of Roman Cyprus is particularly well illustrated by glass and jewelry as well as sculpture.

properties.popup_header: Classical Art from Cyprus: ca. 480–ca. 310 B.C.
properties.popup_body:
A.G. Leventis Foundation Gallery

The Lamont Wing

Although Cyprus was under Persian rule from about 525 B.C. until it was liberated by Alexander the Great in 333 B.C., the Cypriot sculpture on view in the A.G. Leventis Foundation Gallery demonstrates Greek influence, though in a form modified by the variety of peoples that occupied the island.

For this period especially, sculpture represents the great strength of the Museum's holdings. The sarcophagus from Golgoi is the most important work. The lively scenes decorating it would have been enhanced with added color, traces of which remain on many of the other pieces in the Cypriot galleries.
